feature: Gate Request — container initiates action approval
user_story: As a container runtime (OpenClaw agent), I want to request approval for a destructive action so that the user can explicitly approve or deny it before it is executed.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/internal/runtime/<tenant_id>/gate/request. Authenticated via X-Internal-Key + X-Tenant-Id headers (validated by validate_internal_runtime_request; returns 403 on failure). Body: {action_type, payload, display_summary}. Validates action_type against ActionType enum (returns 400 on unknown type) and requires non-empty display_summary (400 if missing). If tenant.model_tier == 'starter', returns 403 with status='blocked' and a store-safe message about prompt injection risk — this check precedes all other logic and cannot be bypassed even if gate_all_actions=False. If auto-approve is in effect (tenant.gate_all_actions=False AND tenant.gate_acknowledged_risk=True, OR a GatePreference.require_confirmation=False record exists), creates ActionAuditLog with result=approved and returns 200 {action_id: null, status: 'approved', auto_approved: true}. Otherwise creates PendingAction (status=pending, expires_at=now+5min), dispatches send_gate_confirmation to the tenant's preferred channel, and returns 202 {action_id, status: 'pending', expires_at}.
dependencies: apps/integrations/internal_auth (validate_internal_runtime_request); apps/tenants/models (Tenant, gate_all_actions, gate_acknowledged_risk, model_tier); apps/actions/messaging (send_gate_confirmation); Telegram Bot API or LINE Messaging API
inventory_notes: Starter-tier block is intentionally store-safe (no upgrade link) per App Store Guideline 3.1.1. Auto-approval path logs an ActionAuditLog even without creating a PendingAction. The action_type must be one of: gmail_trash, gmail_delete, gmail_send, calendar_delete, drive_delete, task_delete.

feature: Gate Poll — container polls for approval decision
user_story: As a container runtime (OpenClaw agent), I want to poll for whether the user has approved or denied a pending action so that I can proceed or abort accordingly.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/internal/runtime/<tenant_id>/gate/<action_id>/poll. Authenticated via X-Internal-Key + X-Tenant-Id headers (403 on failure). Resolves PendingAction by id + tenant_id (404 if not found). If action.is_expired (now > expires_at AND status == PENDING), transitions status to 'expired', creates ActionAuditLog with result=expired, and returns 200 {action_id, status: 'expired'}. Otherwise returns 200 {action_id, status} with the current status unchanged. The container polls in a loop until status is approved, denied, or expired.
inventory_notes: Expiry transition happens lazily on poll — there is no background sweeper that sets status=expired proactively (the Celery task in tasks.py does that as a belt-and-suspenders sweep). Both poll and the Celery sweeper emit an ActionAuditLog on expiry, so duplicate logs are possible for expired actions.

feature: Gate Respond — Django-internal callback from button press
user_story: As Django's own Telegram/LINE callback handler, I want to record the user's approve/deny button press so that the agent container can learn the decision via the poll endpoint.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/gate/<action_id>/respond/. Authenticated via X-Deploy-Secret header matched against DEPLOY_SECRET setting (403 if absent or wrong; 500 if DEPLOY_SECRET not configured). Body: {action: 'approve' | 'deny'} (400 on any other value). Resolves PendingAction by id (404 if not found). If action.status != PENDING returns 409 with current status. If action.is_expired, transitions to expired, creates ActionAuditLog, and returns 410. On valid response, writes action.status (approved/denied), action.responded_at=now, creates ActionAuditLog, then calls update_gate_message to edit/follow-up the platform confirmation message. Returns 200 {status}.
dependencies: DEPLOY_SECRET setting; apps/actions/messaging (update_gate_message); Telegram Bot API (editMessageText) or LINE Messaging API (push)
inventory_notes: This endpoint is called by the Telegram poller and LINE webhook handlers directly in-process, not via HTTP — but the view exists as a proper HTTP endpoint. The same logic is duplicated in-process in poller._handle_gate_callback and line_webhook._handle_gate_postback for Telegram and LINE respectively, bypassing this endpoint. The HTTP endpoint path is present in urls.py and respond_urls.py.

feature: Telegram inline button — user approves/denies via Telegram callback query
user_story: As a subscriber using Telegram, I want to tap Approve or Deny buttons in the confirmation message so that I can control whether the agent executes a destructive action.
expected_behaviour: The Telegram poller receives a callback_query with callback_data of 'gate_approve:<id>' or 'gate_deny:<id>'. The poller calls _handle_gate_callback, which resolves the PendingAction scoped to the tenant (raises not-found and answers callback with 'Action not found' on miss). If action.status != PENDING answers with 'Already <status>' and returns. If is_expired, transitions to expired, creates ActionAuditLog, calls update_gate_message, answers '⏰ Expired'. Otherwise sets status to approved/denied, sets responded_at, creates ActionAuditLog, calls update_gate_message (which calls Telegram editMessageText to remove the inline keyboard and show the result), answers the callback with '✅ Approved' or '❌ Denied'. No HTTP round-trip to GateRespondView.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (answerCallbackQuery, editMessageText); apps/actions/models (PendingAction, ActionAuditLog); apps/pii/redactor (rehydrate_for_tenant)
inventory_notes: The poller handles this entirely in-process and does not call the GateRespondView HTTP endpoint. The PendingAction is scoped to both id AND tenant — a user cannot approve another tenant's action.

feature: LINE postback — user approves/denies via LINE Flex Message button
user_story: As a subscriber using LINE, I want to tap Approve or Deny buttons in the confirmation Flex Message so that I can control whether the agent executes a destructive action.
expected_behaviour: The LINE webhook receives a postback event with data 'gate_approve:<id>' or 'gate_deny:<id>'. The handler calls _handle_gate_postback, which resolves the PendingAction scoped to the tenant. Handles already-resolved (silent return) and expired (transitions, logs, calls update_gate_message which sends a follow-up push text since LINE does not support editing). On valid response sets status + responded_at, creates ActionAuditLog, calls update_gate_message. LINE cannot edit messages so a new push message is sent showing the result.
dependencies: LINE Messaging API (push); apps/actions/models (PendingAction, ActionAuditLog); apps/pii/redactor (rehydrate_for_tenant)
inventory_notes: LINE has no message-edit API so _edit_line_message sends a new push message as a follow-up. This differs from Telegram which edits in place.

feature: Confirmation message dispatch — send gate prompt to user's platform
user_story: As the action gating system, I want to send a formatted confirmation prompt with Approve/Deny buttons to the user's preferred messaging channel so that they can review and decide on the pending action.
expected_behaviour: send_gate_confirmation(tenant, action) reads tenant.user.preferred_channel (defaults to 'telegram'). For Telegram: calls Telegram sendMessage with MarkdownV2 text including the rehydrated display_summary (PII tokens expanded) and an inline keyboard with callback_data 'gate_approve:<id>' and 'gate_deny:<id>'. Falls back to plain-text sendMessage if MarkdownV2 fails. Stores the returned message_id in action.platform_message_id and 'telegram' in action.platform_channel. For LINE: sends a Flex Message bubble with postback buttons, stores 'line-push-<action_id>' as the message_id (LINE push API does not return a message ID). If no suitable sender is found for the channel, logs a warning and does nothing.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (sendMessage); LINE Messaging API (push); apps/pii/redactor (rehydrate_for_tenant); TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, LINE_CHANNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN settings
inventory_notes: PII tokens in display_summary are rehydrated before display so users see real names/emails, not anonymised tokens. Telegram fallback to plain text silently swallows the first error and retries without parse_mode.
error_detail: Confirmed real. send_gate_confirmation reads tenant.user.preferred_channel directly and indexes _SENDERS, which only maps 'telegram' and 'line'. It does NOT call resolve_user_channel, the function that knows to fall back to the 'app' channel for iOS-only users. An iOS-only user has no telegram_chat_id/line_user_id but does have a DeviceToken, and preferred_channel defaults to 'telegram' (the model only allows 'telegram'/'line' as choices; 'app' is not a valid stored value). So the Telegram sender runs, finds chat_id is None, logs 'Tenant X has no Telegram chat_id' and returns None. The PendingAction is still created but platform_message_id/platform_channel are never set, so the user receives no approve/deny prompt. There is no in-app gate surface: approval handlers exist only in the Telegram poller and LINE webhook; nothing consumes a gate via APNs/DeviceToken. By default gating is ON (Tenant.gate_all_actions=True, gate_acknowledged_risk=False, GatePreference.require_confirmation defaults True), so destructive actions (gmail_delete, calendar_delete, drive_delete, task_delete, gmail_send) require confirmation and silently expire after 5 minutes for iOS-only users, returning 'expired' to the container with no explanation. Blast radius is limited because all ActionTypes are Google Workspace / task deletes, so an iOS-only App Store user must have GWS connected AND trigger such an action to hit it -- hence medium, not high.

user_story: As the platform, I want to periodically expire pending actions that have passed their deadline so that the agent container can receive a definitive 'expired' status on the next poll and the user's platform message is updated.
expected_behaviour: expire_stale_pending_actions() queries all PendingAction records with status=PENDING and expires_at < now. For each: sets status=EXPIRED, creates ActionAuditLog with result=expired, calls update_gate_message to edit/follow-up the platform message. Returns a summary string 'Expired N actions'. Errors in update_gate_message are caught and logged per action but do not abort the sweep. Intended to run every 60 seconds via Celery Beat or QStash cron.
dependencies: Celery or QStash cron trigger; apps/actions/messaging (update_gate_message); apps/actions/models (PendingAction, ActionAuditLog)
inventory_notes: The task is decorated @shared_task but this codebase uses QStash not Celery Beat — it is unclear whether this task is actually registered with QStash or remains unused. The comment says 'Celery Beat (or QStash cron)'. Expiry also happens lazily on poll and on GateRespondView calls, so this sweep is belt-and-suspenders.
error_detail: Confirmed dead code with a real but minor user-facing consequence. apps/actions/tasks.py imports celery (from celery import shared_task) and decorates expire_stale_pending_actions with @shared_task, yet (1) Celery is NOT a dependency: it appears only inside comments in requirements.in ("replaces Celery") and the removed django_celery_beat note in config/settings/base.py:37; importing this module would raise ImportError. (2) The task is never invoked: the only reference to expire_stale_pending_actions in the repo is its own definition (verified via grep) -- no QStash endpoint, no apps/cron registration, no .delay() call. So the proactive sweep never runs. Impact is real but contained. Pending actions are still expired lazily in GatePollView (apps/actions/views.py:213) and GateRespondView (:285), but the lazy GatePollView path only flips DB status and does NOT call update_gate_message(). The only place expiry triggers a platform-message button refresh is the dead Celery task (tasks.py:41). update_gate_message IS called on real user responses (poller.py:1524/1545, line_webhook.py:1348/1365, GateRespondView via views.py:330) but never on timeout-expiry. Result: when an action expires by timeout (user ignores it, or the container hibernates and stops polling), the Approve/Deny buttons on the Telegram/LINE confirmation message stay visible indefinitely, and abandoned actions leave stale PENDING rows. There is NO security/data-integrity risk: tapping a stale button hits GateRespondView, which returns 410 GONE and refuses to apply the action (views.py:285-298). Worst case is UX confusion -- a tap on an old button silently does nothing and the message looks unresolved. Severity is low.

user_story: As an admin/operator, I want to configure whether individual action types require user confirmation or are auto-approved per tenant so that power users can skip the confirmation prompt for specific operations.
expected_behaviour: GatePreference model has unique_together(tenant, action_type). require_confirmation=True (default) means the action goes through the full confirmation flow. require_confirmation=False means GateRequestView returns 200 approved immediately and writes an ActionAuditLog. The master switch is on the Tenant model: if gate_all_actions=False AND gate_acknowledged_risk=True, ALL action types are auto-approved regardless of GatePreference. Managed only via Django admin (GatePreferenceAdmin). No subscriber-facing API endpoint exists.
inventory_notes: Starter-tier check in GateRequestView runs before _should_auto_approve, so even a tenant with gate_all_actions=False and gate_acknowledged_risk=True on the starter tier is blocked. GatePreference has no subscriber-facing API — changes require Django admin or direct DB.

user_story: As the platform, I want to block all destructive actions for subscribers on the Starter plan so that they are protected from prompt-injection attacks that could cause irreversible data loss.
expected_behaviour: On POST /api/v1/internal/runtime/<tenant_id>/gate/request, if tenant.model_tier == 'starter', GateRequestView returns 403 {status: 'blocked', tier: 'starter', message: STARTER_BLOCKED_MESSAGE} for every action_type without any further checks. This check is applied before auto-approve logic and before creating any PendingAction. The message text deliberately avoids mentioning upgrades or billing URLs to comply with App Store Guideline 3.1.1. The OpenClaw config generator still loads the nbhd-action-gate skill for starter tenants with Google integration, but the gate endpoint will always block them.
inventory_notes: Config generator at apps/orchestrator/config_generator.py:2178 loads nbhd-action-gate for all GWS tenants including starter. The starter block is enforced at the API level, not at config-load time. Starter tier also receives only read-only GWS skills (gws-shared, gws-gmail-triage, gws-calendar-agenda) and never gets gws-gmail, gws-drive, gws-calendar, or gws-tasks.

feature: Action audit log — permanent record of every gate decision
user_story: As an admin/operator, I want a permanent audit trail of every gated action and its outcome so that I can review the history of destructive operations taken or rejected by agents.
expected_behaviour: ActionAuditLog records are created in four code paths: (1) auto-approval in GateRequestView (result=approved, responded_at=now); (2) user approval/denial in GateRespondView or poller/_handle_gate_callback or line_webhook/_handle_gate_postback (result=approved/denied, responded_at=now); (3) expiry in GatePollView when poll detects expiry; (4) expiry sweep in expire_stale_pending_actions task. Fields: tenant, action_type, action_payload (full JSON), display_summary, result, responded_at (null for expired), created_at. Ordering is newest-first. No API endpoint; accessible only via Django admin (ActionAuditLogAdmin, read-only created_at/responded_at).
inventory_notes: Expiry can be logged by multiple code paths (poll lazy-expiry + Celery sweep), potentially creating duplicate ActionAuditLog entries for the same PendingAction. There is no dedup guard.

feature: OpenClaw config — gate skill and GWS skill tier gating
user_story: As the orchestrator config generator, I want to inject the nbhd-action-gate skill and GWS tool skills into OpenClaw container config (with tier-appropriate restrictions) so that agents have access to gated destructive tools only when appropriate.
expected_behaviour: generate_openclaw_config checks if the tenant has an active Google integration. If yes: adds gws-shared, gws-gmail-triage, gws-calendar-agenda skill paths to skills.load.extraDirs (read-only, all tiers); always also adds nbhd-action-gate; sets env vars NBHD_TENANT_ID and NBHD_API_BASE_URL for the gate script to call Django. Starter tier: receives only the read-only GWS skills above. Higher tiers in principle receive destructive skills (gws-gmail, gws-gmail-send, gws-calendar, gws-drive, gws-tasks) but this conditional is not present in config_generator.py lines 2148-2186 — those lines only add the read-only set for all tiers. Gate env vars are not set without Google integration.
dependencies: apps/integrations/models (Integration, provider=google); apps/tenants/models (Tenant.model_tier); OpenClaw container runtime (nbhd-action-gate skill)
inventory_notes: As of the audited code, ALL tenants with Google integration (including non-starter) receive only the read-only GWS skills — the destructive skills (gws-gmail, etc.) are not added anywhere in config_generator. The gate skill is always added alongside GWS skills but the gate API would block starter tier. NBHD_TENANT_ID and NBHD_API_BASE_URL are required for the gate script to make HTTP calls back to Django.

user_story: As a system developer, I want per-tenant agent session and memory storage so that future agentic features can track conversation history and persistent key-value memories per tenant.
expected_behaviour: The agents app exists only as two migration files (0001_initial + 0002_initial) that define three database tables: agent_sessions (UUID PK, title, is_active, message_count, timestamps, FK→tenants.tenant), memory_items (UUID PK, key, value, category in [general/preference/fact/instruction/contact], timestamps, FK→tenants.tenant; unique_together on (tenant, key)), and messages (UUID PK, role in [user/assistant/system], content, metadata JSON, tokens_used, model_used, cost_estimate, timestamps, FK→agents.AgentSession). There is no models.py, views.py, admin.py, urls.py, apps.py, or any other Python module. The app is not listed in INSTALLED_APPS. No other app in the codebase imports from apps.agents. The migrations would be applied only if the app were added to INSTALLED_APPS and migrate was run; currently the tables may or may not exist in the database but no live code reads or writes them.
inventory_notes: The app was scaffolded on 2026-02-08 and never wired up. It is absent from INSTALLED_APPS in config/settings/base.py. All three models are purely speculative schema — no views, serializers, signals, admin registrations, or URL routes exist. The memory_items table's unique_together (tenant, key) suggests it was intended as a per-tenant key-value store for the AI assistant. Any activation would require adding 'apps.agents' to INSTALLED_APPS, creating models.py to match the migration schema, and wiring URLs/views.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to list all my automations so that I can see what recurring assistant workflows I have configured.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/automations/ requires IsAuthenticated. Resolves the requesting user's Tenant via user.tenant (raises 404 if missing). Returns all Automation rows for that tenant ordered by created_at ascending, serialized as a flat JSON array (no pagination). Fields include id, kind, status, timezone, schedule_type, schedule_time, schedule_days, quiet_hours_start, quiet_hours_end, last_run_at, next_run_at, created_at, updated_at. Automations belonging to other tenants are never returned — strict tenant scoping via filter(tenant=tenant).
inventory_notes: No pagination on the list endpoint — if a tenant somehow accumulates many automations (up to MAX_ACTIVE_AUTOMATIONS=5 active, plus paused ones) the full set is returned in one response.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to create a new recurring automation (daily brief or weekly review) so that my assistant proactively runs PKM workflows on a schedule.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/automations/ requires IsAuthenticated. Body fields: kind (daily_brief|weekly_review), status (active|paused, default active), timezone (IANA name validated via ZoneInfo), schedule_type (daily|weekly), schedule_time (HH:MM:SS), schedule_days (list of 0–6 integers; required for weekly, ignored/cleared for daily), quiet_hours_start and quiet_hours_end (optional TimeFields). On validation: timezone is checked with ZoneInfo — unknown names return 400 with timezone error. schedule_days integers outside 0–6 or non-integer values return 400. weekly schedule without schedule_days returns 400. If status=active, the system checks _active_automations_count(tenant); if already at MAX_ACTIVE_AUTOMATIONS=5, raises AutomationLimitError and returns 400 with detail. On success, compute_next_run_at calculates first UTC fire time from the timezone-local schedule. Returns 201 with the created automation JSON. schedule_days is deduplicated and sorted before storage.
inventory_notes: MAX_ACTIVE_AUTOMATIONS=5 is a hard tenant-level cap enforced only when status=active at creation time. Creating a paused automation bypasses the cap check. quiet_hours_start/quiet_hours_end are stored but not enforced by the scheduler — they are metadata for the client/future use.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to retrieve the details of a specific automation so that I can inspect its current schedule and status.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/ requires IsAuthenticated. Fetches the Automation via id + tenant filter with select_related('tenant', 'tenant__user'). Returns 404 if not found or belongs to another tenant. Returns 200 with full AutomationSerializer JSON on success.
inventory_notes: Tenant isolation is enforced by the combined id + tenant=tenant filter in _get_automation_for_tenant — cross-tenant access yields 404, not 403.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to update my automation's schedule, timezone, or kind so that I can adjust when and how the assistant runs.
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/ requires IsAuthenticated. Partial update — only provided fields are changed. Validates timezone and schedule_days using the same rules as creation. If the update transitions status to active (from paused), checks active automation count excluding the current automation; raises 400 with AutomationLimitError if cap exceeded. If schedule-affecting fields (timezone, schedule_type, schedule_time, schedule_days) or status change, and the resulting status is active, next_run_at is recomputed. Returns 200 with updated automation JSON.
inventory_notes: next_run_at is only recomputed when the resulting automation is active AND at least one schedule field or the status field is in the request body. Updating only quiet_hours_* on an active automation does not recompute next_run_at.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to delete an automation so that it stops running and is removed from my account.
expected_behaviour: DELETE /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/ requires IsAuthenticated. Retrieves the automation with tenant scoping (404 if missing or other tenant). Calls automation.delete() which cascades to all AutomationRun rows (on_delete=CASCADE). Returns 204 No Content on success.
inventory_notes: Cascade delete removes all run history for the automation. No soft-delete or archival mechanism exists.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to pause an active automation so that it stops firing on schedule without losing its configuration.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/pause/ requires IsAuthenticated. Calls pause_automation(automation): if status is not already PAUSED, sets status=paused and saves with update_fields=['status', 'updated_at']. Idempotent — calling on an already-paused automation returns 200 with the unchanged object. Does not clear next_run_at. Returns 200 with the updated automation JSON.
inventory_notes: Because next_run_at is not cleared, a paused automation that is later resumed will have its next_run_at recomputed from now — the stale value is overwritten on resume.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to resume a paused automation so that it starts firing on schedule again.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/resume/ requires IsAuthenticated. Calls resume_automation(automation): if status is not already ACTIVE, checks _active_automations_count(tenant, exclude_id=automation.id) against MAX_ACTIVE_AUTOMATIONS=5. If cap is exceeded, raises AutomationLimitError and the view returns 400 with {'detail': '...'}. Otherwise sets status=active, recomputes next_run_at via compute_next_run_at from now, and saves with update_fields=['status', 'next_run_at', 'updated_at']. Idempotent on an already-active automation (no-op, returns 200). Returns 200 with updated automation JSON.
inventory_notes: Resume always recomputes next_run_at from the current wall-clock time — it never uses the stale stored value, preventing a flood of catch-up runs after a long pause.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to trigger an automation immediately so that I can get a daily brief or weekly review on demand without waiting for the schedule.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/run/ requires IsAuthenticated. Calls execute_automation(automation, trigger_source='manual'). Enforces two rate limits before dispatching: (1) MIN_RUN_INTERVAL=120 minutes since last_run_at; (2) MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY=12 runs within the tenant's local calendar day. If either limit is breached, raises AutomationLimitError, view returns 400 with {'detail': reason}. If limits pass, creates an AutomationRun with idempotency_key='manual:<automation_id>:<timestamp>:<uuid4>' (always unique for manual runs). Builds a synthetic Telegram update with a PKM-prompt text for the automation kind, then calls forward_to_openclaw(tenant.container_fqdn, synthetic_update, user_timezone=...) in a new asyncio event loop. Requires tenant.status=ACTIVE and tenant.container_fqdn set — otherwise raises AutomationExecutionError and the run is marked FAILED. On success, run.status=succeeded and automation.last_run_at is updated. Manual trigger does NOT advance next_run_at. Returns 201 with AutomationRunSerializer JSON. If the run was a duplicate idempotency_key (get_or_create returned existing), returns the existing run without re-dispatching.
dependencies: OpenClaw container runtime (forward_to_openclaw via apps.router.services), Supabase/Postgres (automation_runs table)
inventory_notes: The asyncio event loop is created fresh (asyncio.new_event_loop()) and closed in a finally block for each dispatch — this is synchronous HTTP thread blocking. forward_to_openclaw returning None is treated as a failure (AutomationExecutionError 'OpenClaw dispatch returned no response'). Missing telegram_chat_id on the tenant user raises AutomationExecutionError before any network call.
error_detail: Confirmed at the code level. AutomationManualRunView.post (views.py:112-119) only catches AutomationLimitError (returns HTTP 400). When _dispatch_to_openclaw raises AutomationExecutionError -- tenant not ACTIVE (services.py:204-205), no container_fqdn (services.py:206-207), or missing telegram_chat_id (services.py:177-179) -- that exception is swallowed by the broad except Exception in execute_automation (services.py:447-449), which marks run.status=FAILED, records error_message, persists the run, and returns it normally with no re-raise. The view then unconditionally returns HTTP 201 Created with the FAILED run serialized. AutomationRunSerializer exposes status and error_message, so the failure IS surfaced -- but only in the response body, with an HTTP-level success (201) signal. A subscriber clicking Run now gets a success status code even when the automation could not be dispatched/delivered; a client must inspect body.status == FAILED to detect it. This is a real REST-semantics/UX inconsistency. Severity is low because the failure is reported in the body (not silently dropped), the run record is persisted and queryable via the runs-list endpoint, and the frontend can branch on the status field. It is not a data-integrity or security issue -- purely a mismatch between HTTP status and actual outcome on the manual-run path.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see the history of all my automation run attempts so that I can audit what ran, when, and whether it succeeded.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/automations/runs/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns all AutomationRun rows for the tenant, ordered by -created_at, with select_related('automation'). Uses PageNumberPagination (Django REST framework default page size). Response shape: {'count': N, 'next': url|null, 'previous': url|null, 'results': [...]}. Each run includes id, automation (FK UUID), tenant (FK UUID), status, trigger_source, scheduled_for, started_at, finished_at, idempotency_key, input_payload, result_payload, error_message, created_at, updated_at — all read-only.
inventory_notes: Default page size is the DRF default (100 unless overridden in settings). The automation_id path parameter is None here, so all tenant runs are returned without per-automation filtering.

feature: List automation runs for specific automation
user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see the run history for a specific automation so that I can debug or review that particular workflow.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/runs/ requires IsAuthenticated. Fetches the Automation via _get_automation_for_tenant (404 if missing or cross-tenant). Filters AutomationRun queryset to that automation additionally. Applies the same PageNumberPagination as the tenant-wide endpoint. Returns paginated AutomationRunSerializer JSON. If the automation_id is valid for the tenant but has no runs, returns an empty results list with count=0.
inventory_notes: The same AutomationRunsListView handles both the tenant-wide and per-automation paths; the automation_id kwarg presence is the branch condition.

user_story: As the system, I want to periodically poll for due automations and execute them so that subscriber-configured schedules fire without manual intervention.
expected_behaviour: run_due_automations_task() (apps/automations/tasks.py) delegates to run_due_automations() (apps/automations/scheduler.py). Queries Automation rows with status=active and next_run_at__lte=now, ordered by next_run_at, limited to DEFAULT_DUE_BATCH_SIZE=100 per invocation. For each due automation, calls execute_automation(automation, trigger_source='schedule', scheduled_for=automation.next_run_at). Limit/rate checks apply: if last_run_at is within MIN_RUN_INTERVAL=120 minutes, or MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY=12 runs already exist in the tenant's local calendar day, the run is created with status=SKIPPED and next_run_at is still advanced. On dispatch success: run.status=SUCCEEDED, automation.last_run_at and automation.next_run_at are updated. On dispatch failure: run.status=FAILED, error_message set, next_run_at advanced (so schedule continues). Idempotency key for scheduled runs: 'schedule:<automation_id>:<epoch_timestamp>' — prevents duplicate runs if the task fires twice for the same window. Returns a summary dict with due_count, processed_count, succeeded, failed, skipped, errors counts. Unhandled exceptions per automation are caught, logged, and counted in 'errors' without aborting the batch.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (automations, automation_runs tables), OpenClaw container runtime (forward_to_openclaw)
inventory_notes: As of the audited codebase, run_due_automations_task is defined but NOT wired to any QStash cron registration or URL endpoint — it exists as an internal callable only. The index on (tenant, status, next_run_at) supports this query efficiently. next_run_at is always advanced after execution (even on FAILED or SKIPPED) so the automation never gets stuck.
error_detail: Confirmed defect. The automations feature ships a complete user-facing scheduling surface: a CRUD API (create/list/pause/resume + manual run in apps/automations/urls.py), an ACTIVE status, a computed next_run_at (compute_next_run_at in services.py), and a working dispatcher run_due_automations (scheduler.py) that selects Automation rows where status=ACTIVE and next_run_at<=now and executes them. But nothing ever calls run_due_automations_task on a schedule. It is absent from TASK_MAP in apps/cron/views.py (lines 70-198), so /api/cron/trigger/<name>/ cannot reach it; it is absent from SYSTEM_CRONS in register_system_crons.py (lines 19-111), so no QStash schedule is ever created; and there is no dedicated URL in apps/cron/urls.py or apps/automations/urls.py that invokes it. A repo-wide grep finds the function referenced only at its own definition, an agent-skills doc, and the audit notes. The audit's own inventory_notes (docs/feature-audit/areas/automations.json:132) confirm it is defined but NOT wired to any QStash cron registration or URL endpoint and exists as an internal callable only. User impact: a subscriber can create an automation, see it listed as ACTIVE, and run it manually, but it will NEVER fire automatically on its configured daily/weekly schedule. The core promise of the feature (automatic scheduled execution) is dead.

user_story: As the system, I want to compute the next UTC fire time for an automation from its timezone-local schedule so that runs fire at the subscriber's intended local time.
expected_behaviour: compute_next_run_at(timezone_name, schedule_type, schedule_time, schedule_days, reference_utc=None) in apps/automations/services.py converts reference_utc (or now) to the automation's local timezone, then finds the next wall-clock occurrence of schedule_time after reference_utc. For DAILY: advances to tomorrow if today's occurrence has already passed. For WEEKLY: iterates up to 14 calendar days ahead to find the next matching weekday in schedule_days; raises AutomationValidationError if no match found within 14 days (only if schedule_days is somehow empty for a weekly automation, which create/update validation prevents). Returns a UTC-aware datetime. Called on create, resume, and whenever schedule fields change on update, and after each scheduled execution to set next_run_at.
inventory_notes: The 14-day lookahead cap for weekly schedules is a gotcha: if the server's system timezone data is outdated, ZoneInfo may silently fall back or error. DST transitions are handled transparently by ZoneInfo but the returned UTC time will differ from non-DST periods for the same local time.

user_story: As the system, I want to fabricate a Telegram update payload and forward it to the tenant's OpenClaw container so that the automation runs in the same conversation context as real Telegram messages.
expected_behaviour: _dispatch_to_openclaw(automation, run_id) in services.py: checks tenant.status==ACTIVE (raises AutomationExecutionError otherwise), checks tenant.container_fqdn is set (raises otherwise), checks tenant.user.telegram_chat_id is set (raises AutomationExecutionError 'tenant user is missing telegram_chat_id'). Builds a synthetic Telegram update dict with update_id derived from epoch + run_id suffix, message.text set to the kind-specific PKM prompt string prefixed by [AUTOMATION:kind run_id=<uuid>], from.id set to telegram_user_id or chat_id. Calls apps.router.services.forward_to_openclaw(container_fqdn, synthetic_update, user_timezone=automation.timezone or tenant.user.timezone or 'UTC') via asyncio.new_event_loop().run_until_complete(). If the coroutine returns None, raises AutomationExecutionError. Returns (synthetic_update, result) tuple on success.
inventory_notes: Automation kind=daily_brief sends a PKM-Loop daily-reflection prompt; kind=weekly_review sends a 7-day PKM review prompt that also triggers monthly goals-check on the 1st of month. The synthetic update masquerades as coming from the real Telegram user (using their telegram_user_id/chat_id), so OpenClaw processes it identically to a real Telegram message. No APNs push is triggered by this path — it goes through the Telegram router, not the iOS app_chat path.

feature: Automation run rate limiting and idempotency
user_story: As the system, I want to enforce per-automation rate limits and idempotent run creation so that automations cannot run too frequently and duplicate cron invocations do not cause double-fires.
expected_behaviour: _check_limits(automation, reference_utc) enforces: (1) MIN_RUN_INTERVAL=120 minutes between consecutive runs (compared against automation.last_run_at); (2) MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY=12 runs within the tenant's calendar day in the automation's own timezone (computed via _day_window_for_timezone). For manual trigger_source, a failed limit check raises AutomationLimitError (view returns 400). For schedule trigger_source, a failed limit check creates a SKIPPED run (with error_message) and advances next_run_at — no exception is raised to the caller. Idempotency is enforced via AutomationRun.idempotency_key (unique DB constraint). Scheduled keys are 'schedule:<automation_id>:<epoch>'; manual keys are 'manual:<automation_id>:<epoch>:<uuid4>'. get_or_create inside an atomic transaction ensures that concurrent duplicate scheduled invocations result in only one run being dispatched.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (automation_runs table, unique constraint on idempotency_key)
inventory_notes: The daily cap (_runs_today_count) counts all AutomationRun rows in the day window regardless of status — including SKIPPED and FAILED runs count against the cap. The idempotency get_or_create is wrapped in transaction.atomic() to prevent race conditions, but the dispatch call (_dispatch_to_openclaw) happens outside the atomic block, so a crash between run creation and dispatch leaves the run in RUNNING status permanently.
error_detail: Confirmed real. In execute_automation, RUNNING is committed at services.py:435-437, but dispatch (line 440) and the next_run_at advance (468-470) happen after it, outside the get_or_create atomic block. A hard process kill (OOM/eviction/SIGKILL) between the RUNNING save and dispatch leaves the AutomationRun permanently RUNNING and never advances next_run_at. The scheduled idempotency key is window-deterministic (services.py:234-236 derives epoch from scheduled_for; scheduler.py:48 always passes scheduled_for=automation.next_run_at). With next_run_at not advanced, the next tick re-selects the same automation (scheduler.py:22-26) and rebuilds the identical key; get_or_create returns the existing RUNNING row (created=False) and short-circuits at services.py:432-433 without dispatching or advancing. The automation is re-selected forever while doing nothing. No stale-RUNNING reaper exists for AutomationRun (one exists for router messages at cron/views.py:177). Impact: the automation is silently dead until an operator manually resolves the row; no error surfaces (run_due_automations counts it merely as processed) and it permanently holds a due-batch slot. Medium not high: requires a hard kill in a sub-ms window (normal dispatch exceptions are caught at line 447, marked FAILED, and still advance next_run_at), so per-occurrence probability is low, but Azure Container Apps do get evicted/restarted and the failure is permanent and unrecoverable without manual intervention.

user_story: As the platform, I want to cap each tenant at 5 active automations so that a single subscriber cannot overwhelm the system with scheduled assistant invocations.
expected_behaviour: MAX_ACTIVE_AUTOMATIONS=5 is checked in three places: create_automation (if new status=active), update_automation (if transitioning status to active), and resume_automation. The check uses _active_automations_count(tenant, exclude_id=...) which counts Automation rows with status=active, optionally excluding the automation being resumed/updated. If count >= 5, AutomationLimitError is raised with message 'active automation limit reached (5)'. The serializer converts this to a 400 response with {'detail': '...'}. Creating a paused automation does not check or increment the cap.
inventory_notes: The cap is a count query, not enforced by a DB constraint, so a race condition between two concurrent create requests could both pass the check and both insert — bringing the tenant to 6 active automations. No Stripe plan gating is applied; the cap is flat for all subscribers.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to start a Stripe subscription so that my AI container gets provisioned.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/billing/checkout/ requires IsAuthenticated. Guards on STRIPE_PRICE_ID being set (503 if not). Creates a Stripe Checkout Session in subscription mode using the user's email (NOT a stored customer id, so it's immune to stale-customer bugs). Session carries metadata {user_id, tenant_id, tier='starter'}. Requires ToS acceptance via consent_collection. Returns {url} for frontend redirect. On any StripeError returns 502. The checkout.session.completed webhook (not the success_url redirect) finalises provisioning.
inventory_notes: Subscription checkout uses customer_email, not stripe_customer_id, so it never hits the stale-customer-after-account-migration bug. The 503 'billing disabled' path keys on STRIPE_PRICE_ID being empty, not a feature flag.

user_story: As the platform, I want to receive Stripe webhook events so that subscription and payment changes are reflected in the system in real time.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/billing/webhook/ is csrf_exempt and require_POST. Verifies Stripe signature using DJSTRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET; returns 400 on invalid. Sets service-role RLS context for cross-tenant DB writes. Dispatches on event type: checkout.session.completed/async_payment_succeeded (subscription or credit top-up), charge.refunded (credit clawback), charge.dispute.created (log + manual review), customer.subscription.deleted (deprovision or hard-delete), customer.subscription.updated (logged, no-op), invoice.payment_failed (suspend tenant). Uses stripe-py to_dict() on the StripeObject to work around stripe-py 15.x dict-like removal. Returns 200 on all handled and unhandled events.
inventory_notes: Stripe redelivers events and may deliver concurrently; idempotency is handled per-handler (credit grants use DB unique constraints; subscription handlers guard on current status). The stripe-py 15.x StripeObject.to_dict() boundary fix is critical — without it every webhook 500s.
error_detail: Confirmed asymmetry in the webhook dispatcher (apps/billing/views.py:161-172). Both checkout.session.completed and checkout.session.async_payment_succeeded enter the same match arm. Branch logic: (1) credit top-up (mode==payment, kind==credit_topup) routes to handle_credit_topup_completed, which handles BOTH events including async; (2) elif event_type == checkout.session.completed routes to handle_checkout_completed; (3) else, log only. The else branch is reached exactly when the event is async_payment_succeeded AND it is NOT a credit top-up, i.e. a SUBSCRIPTION whose payment cleared asynchronously. In that case handle_checkout_completed is never called, so the tenant is never provisioned/reactivated and tier/customer/subscription ids are never written. The feature expected_behaviour explicitly lists async_payment_succeeded as a trigger for subscription OR credit top-up, so the missing subscription leg is a genuine gap, not intended behaviour. Compounding it, handle_checkout_completed (services.py:296) does not gate on payment_status, so for a true async subscription the synchronous completed event arrives with payment_status unpaid yet the only confirmation event (async_payment_succeeded) is dropped. WHY LOW SEVERITY: reachability depends on Stripe Dashboard config. The subscription Checkout session is created at views.py:288 with mode=subscription and NO payment_method_types argument, defaulting to whatever methods are enabled in the Stripe Dashboard. With card-only (Stripe default) the async event never fires for subscriptions, so no real subscriber is affected today, and nothing in the repo enables ACH/SEPA/bank-debit. It is a real but latent, config-conditional logic bug that only bites if an async/delayed payment method is enabled in the Dashboard.

user_story: As the platform, I want to provision or reactivate a tenant's container when their Stripe subscription checkout succeeds.
expected_behaviour: handle_checkout_completed() is called from stripe_webhook on checkout.session.completed. Ignores payment-mode sessions (credit top-ups). Looks up the tenant via user_id metadata → subscription_id → customer_id fallback chain. If the tenant was SUSPENDED, marks it ACTIVE, wakes the container (scale min=1), queues resume_tenant_crons (30s delay via QStash), and refreshes cron rows from seed. If the tenant was PROVISIONING or new, sets status PROVISIONING and publishes provision_tenant task. Resets monthly_cost_budget and monthly_token_budget to 0 (tier defaults). Guards against duplicate completions (same subscription + already active). On wake also applies pending configs.
dependencies: Stripe API, QStash, Azure Container Apps (scale_container_app), apps/orchestrator/services.py
inventory_notes: Cold-start race: container is not yet ready at wake time, so resume_tenant_crons is enqueued with a 30s delay rather than called synchronously. Cron-payload drift accumulated during suspension is eagerly corrected via refresh_system_cron_rows_from_seed.

user_story: As the platform, I want to deprovision a tenant's container when their Stripe subscription is cancelled so that Azure costs stop.
expected_behaviour: handle_subscription_deleted() is called from stripe_webhook on customer.subscription.deleted. Resolves tenant via subscription_id → customer_id. If tenant has pending_deletion=True, triggers a hard-delete (_do_hard_delete) finalising the account deletion the user previously requested. If tenant is is_budget_exempt, skips deprovision entirely (logged). Otherwise sets status DEPROVISIONING and publishes deprovision_tenant QStash task. No-ops if already DEPROVISIONING/DELETED.
inventory_notes: Budget-exempt tenants (canary, internal) never auto-deprovision on Stripe cancel events — protects against test-account Stripe churn. pending_deletion override still fires even for exempt tenants.

user_story: As the platform, I want to suspend a tenant's container when a recurring invoice fails so that Azure resources are not wasted.
expected_behaviour: handle_invoice_payment_failed() is called from stripe_webhook on invoice.payment_failed. Resolves tenant via customer_id/subscription_id. Calls suspend_tenant_crons before suspension while container is still reachable. Sets status SUSPENDED, clears hibernated_at (billing suspension supersedes idle hibernation), then scales the container to 0 replicas. No-ops if already SUSPENDED.
dependencies: Stripe API, Azure Container Apps (scale_container_app), apps/cron/suspension.py
inventory_notes: Cron disable must happen before the scale-to-zero call while the container gateway is still reachable; failure is logged but not fatal to the suspension path.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to access the Stripe billing portal so that I can manage my subscription, update payment methods, or cancel.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/billing/portal/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns 503 if billing is disabled (STRIPE_PRICE_ID empty) or Stripe API key is missing. Returns 404 if user has no tenant. Returns 400 if tenant has no stripe_customer_id. Creates a Stripe Billing Portal Session with return_url to /billing. Returns {url}. On StripeError with 'No such customer' (stale customer after account migration), clears the stored stripe_customer_id and returns 409 with instructions to re-link via a new subscription. Other Stripe errors return 502.
inventory_notes: The stale-customer self-heal path clears stripe_customer_id so the next checkout creates a fresh customer in the current Stripe account. The portal cannot transparently retry unlike the credit checkout because a portal strictly needs a live customer.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to buy prepaid credit so that I can use the AI assistant beyond my monthly included allowance.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/billing/credits/checkout/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns 503 if Stripe key missing or CREDIT_PACKS empty. Returns 404 if no tenant. Accepts {pack_id} from request.data and validates against server-defined CREDIT_PACKS (400 on unknown). Creates a Stripe Checkout Session in mode=payment with metadata {kind: credit_topup, pack_id, tenant_id, user_id} on both session and payment_intent. Uses stored stripe_customer_id if present; on missing-customer error, clears stale customer and retries without it (email-based). Returns {url}. Credit is never granted at checkout start — only by the webhook.
inventory_notes: The pack amount is NEVER taken from client input or session metadata — always re-derived from CREDIT_PACKS on both checkout and webhook sides. Stale-customer self-heal retries without customer, and the webhook backfills the new customer id via the stripe_customer_id='' guard.

user_story: As the platform, I want to grant prepaid credit when a one-time Stripe payment completes so that the subscriber can use the additional allowance immediately.
expected_behaviour: handle_credit_topup_completed() is called from stripe_webhook on checkout.session.completed with mode=payment and metadata.kind=credit_topup. Checks payment_status==paid (returns silently if not). Resolves pack from CREDIT_PACKS[pack_id] (returns on unknown). Resolves tenant from metadata.tenant_id or client_reference_id or stripe_customer_id. Calls grant_credit() which atomically creates a CreditLedger GRANT row and increments Tenant.purchased_credit. Uses partial DB unique constraint on (stripe_event_id, kind=grant) for idempotency — duplicate Stripe redeliveries are no-ops. Also backfills stripe_customer_id on tenant if not set. After grant, calls sync_or_key_limit to raise the OR sub-key spend ceiling.
inventory_notes: grant_credit() refuses to proceed with an empty stripe_event_id (would break idempotency). Amount is re-derived from CREDIT_PACKS server-side. sync_or_key_limit is best-effort — failure never fails the grant.

user_story: As the platform, I want to claw back prepaid credit proportionally when Stripe issues a refund so that the platform doesn't absorb the cost.
expected_behaviour: handle_credit_refund() is called from stripe_webhook on charge.refunded. Looks up the original CreditLedger GRANT by PaymentIntent id (locking the row with select_for_update to prevent concurrent over-claw). Computes claw-back as (amount_refunded/amount * grant_amount) minus already-reversed amount for this PI (handles partial refunds cumulatively without double-counting). Calls refund_credit() which atomically creates a CreditLedger REVERSAL row and decrements Tenant.purchased_credit using Greatest(..., 0) to never go negative. Idempotent per stripe_event_id via partial DB unique constraint. Also calls sync_or_key_limit to lower the OR sub-key ceiling.
inventory_notes: Platform absorbs already-spent credit bounded by small pack sizes. Concurrent Stripe redeliveries of the same charge.refunded are serialized on the grant row lock; per-event idempotency via the partial unique handles the same event arriving twice.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see my prepaid credit balance, included allowance usage, and available top-up packs so that I can decide whether to buy more credit.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/billing/credits/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns 404 if no tenant. Calls credits_state(tenant) which returns: purchased_credit (4dp), included_budget (effective_cost_budget), included_used (estimated_cost_this_month), included_remaining (null if unlimited), packs (list of server-defined CREDIT_PACKS with price/credit display strings), recent_entries (last 20 CreditLedger rows with kind/amount/description/created_at). No writes.
inventory_notes: included_remaining is null (not 0) when the tenant's effective_cost_budget is 0 (unlimited — budget-exempt tenants). The pack list is always the full CREDIT_PACKS regardless of the tenant's tier.

user_story: As the platform, I want to draw from a tenant's prepaid credit balance when their usage exceeds the included monthly allowance so that they can continue chatting beyond the cap.
expected_behaviour: debit_overage_for_turn() is called from record_usage() after each non-BYO non-system turn when cost > 0. Pre-checks purchased_credit and is_budget_exempt (skips non-credit-holders cheaply). For credit holders, acquires select_for_update on the Tenant row to serialize concurrent turns. Computes attributable overage as overage(post) - overage(post-cost). Calls debit_overage_credit() which atomically updates purchased_credit and creates a CreditLedger DEBIT row. Uses conditional UPDATE (purchased_credit >= actual) to prevent going negative; retries once on a lost race. Returns amount drawn (0 if nothing drawn).
inventory_notes: BYO model calls (Anthropic Claude via CLI subscription) set cost=0 so they never trigger a debit. The conditional UPDATE favours the customer on lost races — the reconcile cron trues up any under-draw later.

user_story: As the platform, I want to record every AI inference event with token counts and cost so that quota enforcement and billing analytics work correctly.
expected_behaviour: record_usage() creates a UsageRecord row and atomically increments Tenant counters (messages_today, messages_this_month, tokens_this_month, estimated_cost_this_month) via F-expressions. For is_system=True events (weekly synthesis, platform-side calls), tenant counters are NOT incremented but MonthlyBudget.spent_dollars is still updated. For BYO model calls (Anthropic/OpenAI via user's own subscription), cost is forced to 0 and estimated_cost_this_month is NOT incremented. Triggers debit_overage_for_turn when real cost > 0 and there is credit. message_count parameter controls how many messages a single inference event represents (used by cold-start coalescing).
inventory_notes: Model attribution uses extract_model_from_response (usage.model_used > usage.model > top-level) to avoid the 'openclaw' placeholder. Falls back to resolve_tenant_primary_model when response carries no real model id.

user_story: As the platform, I want to check whether a tenant is within budget before allowing a chat turn so that the platform's costs are bounded.
expected_behaviour: check_budget(tenant) returns '' (allow) or a reason string ('personal' or 'global'). Personal check: refreshes tenant from DB, returns '' for is_budget_exempt tenants, checks has_spendable_budget (within included allowance OR has purchased_credit). Global check: reads current MonthlyBudget row; if remaining <= 0 returns 'global'. Callers (router) hibernate the container when a reason is returned. Credit extends the personal allowance without changing is_over_budget, so threshold emails and the OR 402 breaker keep their meaning.
inventory_notes: has_spendable_budget is on Tenant model and combines is_over_budget and purchased_credit. check_budget does a fresh DB read (refresh_from_db) to avoid stale cached values from concurrent record_usage increments.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see my current-month AI usage summary so that I can monitor my token and cost consumption.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/billing/usage/summary/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns 404 if no tenant. Aggregates UsageRecord rows for the current calendar month: total input/output/combined tokens, total cost, message count, per-model breakdown (model id, display name, tokens, cost, count) ordered by cost descending. Also includes budget info: tenant_tokens_used, token budget, estimated_cost, cost_budget, budget_percentage, global_spent, global_remaining.
inventory_notes: Uses the calendar month boundary (not a rolling 30-day window). display_name_for_model handles both billed MODEL_RATES and BYO models.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see daily usage data for the past N days so that I can chart my AI spending trends.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/billing/usage/daily/?days=N requires IsAuthenticated. days param must be integer 1-90 (400 otherwise, default 30). Returns a list of {date, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost, message_count} for each day in range. Missing days (no usage) are filled with zeros so the client gets a complete contiguous series.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see an open-books cost breakdown including infrastructure, surplus, and donation amounts so that I understand where my subscription money goes.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/billing/usage/transparency/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns current-month totals: subscription_price ($12.00 hardcoded), actual AI cost, platform infra breakdown (container/database/storage from InfraCostSnapshot or estimates), calculated surplus (subscription - AI - infra), donation_amount (surplus * donation_percentage if donation_enabled), model rate card (filtered to tier-relevant models). InfraCostSnapshot is read from DB (populated by the daily infra cost cron); falls back to static estimates if no snapshot exists.
inventory_notes: subscription_price is hardcoded at $12.00 in _get_subscription_price() — not derived from Stripe or a model field. Infra breakdown shows 'azure' source if real data was fetched, 'estimate' if fallback.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to configure whether my subscription surplus is donated and at what percentage so that I can choose how my unused spend is allocated.
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/billing/donation-preference/ requires IsAuthenticated. Accepts {donation_enabled: bool, donation_percentage: int 0-100} (both optional; partial update). Returns 404 if no tenant. Validates via DRF serializer (400 on type errors or out-of-range percentage). Saves only the changed fields via update_fields. Returns current donation_enabled and donation_percentage values.
inventory_notes: Donation amount is computed at read time (transparency endpoint); no separate donation-processing logic exists yet — DonationLedger rows are the tracking mechanism for future implementation.

user_story: As the platform, I want to hourly reconcile internal cost estimates against OpenRouter provider truth so that quota enforcement reflects actual spend.
expected_behaviour: reconcile_all() (triggered via QStash; also runnable as `python manage.py reconcile_openrouter_spend`) walks all ACTIVE tenants with openrouter_key_secret_name set. For each, reads the sub-key from Azure Key Vault, calls GET /api/v1/key on OpenRouter to get usage_monthly, and updates estimated_cost_this_month upward-only to max(internal, provider). Also calls debit_overage_credit for the reconciled delta to draw from prepaid credit. Fires threshold emails (90%/100%) if the true-up crosses them. Updates MonthlyBudget.spent_dollars with per-tenant sum + shared-key usage. One tenant failure logs and continues. Returns {updated, failed, checked}.
dependencies: OpenRouter admin API, Azure Key Vault, Supabase/Postgres, apps/router/billing_quota_handlers.py
inventory_notes: Never reduces estimated_cost_this_month (safe error direction). BYO Claude calls are not counted (no OR usage). One pass is fully idempotent — a second pass immediately after finds provider_truth <= before and skips.

user_story: As the platform, I want to periodically probe the free model offer so that tenants are automatically switched to/from the free model based on its availability and pricing.
expected_behaviour: model_health_check() runs every 30 minutes via QStash. Fetches OpenRouter /models pricing for MONITORED_MODELS. Records pricing/free-status in ModelHealth rows. Sends a 1-token completion ping to the offer model. Transitions FreeModelOffer singleton: activates if offer_free=True and reachable; deactivates if no longer free (immediate) or unreachable for >= 3 consecutive failures (OFFER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD). On transition, bumps all affected tenants' config (tenants on rolling default or explicitly set to offer model), publishes apply_single_tenant_config task for each, and sends an in-channel system notification (capped at 500 tenants). Auto-disables the offer if the slug is absent from a successful /models read and has never activated (retired slug).
inventory_notes: Activation requires both free AND reachable this tick. Deactivation on unreachability is threshold-gated (3 consecutive failures) to avoid flapping on a blip; pricing-change deactivation (free→paid) is immediate. Transition notifications are capped at NOTIFY_CAP=500 to protect large fleets.
error_detail: Both prongs are factually confirmed in the code but narrow and edge-gated. Prong A (hardcoded display name): _transition_message (model_health.py:257-278) hardcodes NEMOTRON_FREE_DISPLAY in all three notification templates while reading the fallback name data-drivenly via display_name_for_model() at line 258. FreeModelOffer.model_id is operator-configurable (CharField with default, models.py:209-213), so if an operator changed the offer model the notifications would still say 'Nemotron 3 Ultra (Free)'. However the entire offer system is single-model and hardcoded around Nemotron: model_offers.py:168 uses NEMOTRON_FREE_DISPLAY for the offer's own display_name regardless of model_id, the allowlist alias is 'nemotron' (model_offers.py:86), and REASONING_MODELS hardcodes Nemotron. Pointing the offer at a different model is an unsupported operation that would mislabel many surfaces, not just this message; impact is cosmetic. Prong B (SUSPENDED tenants not reset): _apply_to_tenants (model_health.py:221-225) filters status=ACTIVE, hibernated_at__isnull=True, so a SUSPENDED tenant whose preferred_model equals the offer model is never reset to '' on deactivation. Real gap but narrow: suspended tenants run no container/inference, the resolver only substitutes the offer model while the offer is active (model_offers.py:70-76), and OpenClaw's runWithModelFallback absorbs a stranded dead slug at turn time, so the only concrete failure is an explicit pick of a now-retired model surfacing at reactivation. Net: genuine latent inconsistencies, both low-impact; the code facts hold so this is not a false positive, but it is not something a normal user/operator experiences today since the offer is single-model (Nemotron) and suspended tenants do not infer.

user_story: As the platform, I want to daily fetch real Azure infrastructure costs per tenant so that the transparency endpoint shows accurate cost data.
expected_behaviour: refresh_infra_costs() (triggered via QStash) queries Azure Cost Management API for all resources in rg-nbhd-prod grouped by ResourceId for the current month-to-date. Maps oc-* resources to container costs and ws-* to storage costs per tenant. Calculates database_share as Supabase monthly cost / active tenant count. Upserts InfraCostSnapshot rows with source='azure' when real data is found, 'estimate' otherwise. Falls back entirely to estimates if the Azure query fails. AZURE_MOCK=true uses estimate fallback. Returns {updated, source, containers_found, shares_found}.
inventory_notes: Container-to-tenant mapping uses the container_id field (oc-xxx prefix). If a specific tenant's container isn't found in Azure results, its cost falls back to ESTIMATE_CONTAINER=$4.00. SUPABASE_MONTHLY_COST is a settings value.

user_story: As the platform, I want to create a per-tenant OpenRouter sub-key during provisioning so that each tenant's spend is isolated and server-enforced.
expected_behaviour: create_sub_key() is called during provision_tenant when OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED=True. Creates a sub-key via POST /api/v1/keys (management key auth) with label='tenant-{uuid[:8]}', limit=tier_cost_budget (e.g. $5 for starter), limit_reset='monthly'. Returns (api_key_string, key_hash). The caller writes the api_key to Azure Key Vault at {prefix}-openrouter-key, persists openrouter_key_secret_name and openrouter_key_hash on Tenant, then rebinds the container's OPENROUTER_API_KEY secret to the new KV entry. Raises OpenRouterAdminError on HTTP failures.
dependencies: OpenRouter admin API, Azure Key Vault, Azure Container Apps
inventory_notes: The management key is re-read from Key Vault on every call (never cached in process). Only visible once — must be persisted to KV immediately. Key hash is OR's stable delete/update handle. Container rebind forces a new revision so the key takes effect.

feature: Per-tenant OR sub-key spend limit sync
user_story: As the platform, I want to update the OR sub-key spend limit when a tenant purchases credit so that they can actually spend the credit on AI usage beyond the included cap.
expected_behaviour: sync_or_key_limit() is called from grant_credit() and refund_credit(). If OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED is False or tenant has no openrouter_key_hash, is a no-op. For budget-exempt tenants, sets the ceiling to OPENROUTER_EXEMPT_KEY_LIMIT (default 1000.0). For normal tenants, sets ceiling to effective_cost_budget + purchased_credit. Calls update_sub_key() which PATCHes /api/v1/keys/{hash} with the new limit. Best-effort: logs warning on failure, never fails the calling function.
dependencies: OpenRouter admin API, Azure Key Vault
inventory_notes: Without this, a tenant's OR sub-key stays pinned at the included cap and OpenRouter 402s before any purchased credit is drawn. Budget-exempt tenants get the high fixed ceiling to prevent the $5 starter cap from tripping the 402 breaker on internal accounts.

user_story: As the platform, I want to delete a tenant's OpenRouter sub-key when their container is deprovisioned so that orphaned keys don't accumulate and aren't exploitable.
expected_behaviour: delete_sub_key(key_hash) is called during deprovision flow. Sends DELETE /api/v1/keys/{hash} with management key auth. A 404 response is treated as success (idempotent — key already gone). Any other 4xx/5xx or network error raises OpenRouterAdminError. Empty key_hash is a no-op (logged).
dependencies: OpenRouter admin API, Azure Key Vault
inventory_notes: 404-as-success prevents re-run failures after a partial deprovision. Orphan keys from failed provisioning paths are cleaned up separately by the sweep_orphan_openrouter_keys management command.

feature: Backfill per-tenant OpenRouter keys management command
user_story: As an operator, I want to backfill per-tenant OpenRouter sub-keys for existing tenants that predate the feature so that all tenants get isolated OR spend tracking.
expected_behaviour: `python manage.py backfill_openrouter_keys [--tenant <uuid>] [--dry-run]` requires OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED=True (CommandError if not). Finds tenants with status ACTIVE or SUSPENDED where openrouter_key_secret_name is empty. For each: resolves KV secret name first (fails fast before any OR call to avoid orphan keys), creates sub-key on OR, writes key to KV, grants tenant managed identity read access on the secret, persists openrouter_key_secret_name and openrouter_key_hash on Tenant, bumps pending_config_version, then rebinds the container's OPENROUTER_API_KEY for ACTIVE tenants. SUSPENDED tenants get the DB/KV update but skip the container rebind. Idempotent: already-backfilled tenants are skipped by the eligibility filter.
dependencies: OpenRouter admin API, Azure Key Vault, Azure Container Apps, Azure Managed Identity
inventory_notes: Critical ordering: secret_name is resolved BEFORE OR sub-key creation to prevent orphan keys when the tenant lacks a key_vault_prefix. Container rebind forces a new revision — a plain restart won't re-fetch the updated KV binding.

feature: Sweep orphan OpenRouter keys management command
user_story: As an operator, I want to identify and delete OpenRouter sub-keys with no matching tenant so that stale keys from failed provisioning don't accumulate.
expected_behaviour: `python manage.py sweep_orphan_openrouter_keys [--min-age-hours N] [--dry-run]` lists all sub-keys from OR via paginated GET /api/v1/keys (management key auth). Cross-references each key's hash against Tenant.openrouter_key_hash. Keys absent from the Tenant table AND older than min-age-hours (default 24h) are deleted via DELETE /api/v1/keys/{hash}. Keys younger than the age threshold are skipped (protects in-flight provisioning). Raises CommandError if list_sub_keys fails.
inventory_notes: KV orphan cleanup is not included — listed as manual operator step via az keyvault. The 24h age gate is critical to avoid racing a provision in flight.

user_story: As an operator, I want to manually cap or uncap the global platform budget so that I can block all AI messages if spend is out of control.
expected_behaviour: `python manage.py cap_budget` sets MonthlyBudget.spent_dollars = budget_dollars (blocks all messages via check_budget global check). `--uncap` resets spent_dollars to 0 (resumes service). `--status` shows current month/budget/spent/over values without changes. Gets-or-creates the MonthlyBudget row for the current month with defaults {budget_dollars: 100, spent_dollars: 0}.
inventory_notes: Cap is immediate — the next check_budget call for any tenant will see global_remaining <= 0 and return 'global'. The actual is_capped field on MonthlyBudget is not used by check_budget; the remaining <= 0 math is the effective gate.
error_detail: Confirmed admin observability gap. The MonthlyBudget.is_capped boolean field (models.py:128) is referenced only in the model definition, a migration, and the admin's list_display + list_filter (admin.py:23-24). A repo-wide grep shows NO code path ever writes it — it stays at default=False forever. The cap_budget command caps service by setting spent_dollars = budget_dollars (cap_budget.py:55-56) and uncaps via spent_dollars = 0 (lines 50-51), never touching is_capped. The real enforcement (check_budget in services.py:288) routes to 'global' purely on remaining <= 0, and reconciliation (reconcile_openrouter_spend.py) only updates spent_dollars. Impact: an operator using the admin filter is_capped=Yes to find manually-capped months always gets zero results, and the is_capped column always renders No even on a capped month — the admin field is permanently misleading. This is purely an admin-facing display defect; billing enforcement and end-user gating are unaffected and work correctly via the spent/remaining path. Low severity: operator-only blast radius, and the admin already surfaces the truthful spent_dollars/budget_dollars columns alongside the broken flag.

user_story: As an operator, I want to simulate a quota breach for a specific tenant so that I can test the budget enforcement flow without waiting for real usage.
expected_behaviour: `python manage.py simulate_quota <tenant_prefix> --trigger` sets estimated_cost_this_month to effective_cost_budget + $0.01 and reports what check_budget() returns. `--reset` sets it back to $0.00. `--status` shows tenant id, container, status, hibernated_at, budget, cost MTD, usage percentage, and check_budget result. Looks up tenant by id prefix (first 8+ chars).
inventory_notes: This is a dev/ops tool — it directly manipulates estimated_cost_this_month. After --trigger, the tenant needs to send a message to trigger actual hibernation (the command doesn't hibernate the container itself).

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to receive email warnings when my usage approaches (90%) and reaches (100%) my monthly budget so that I'm not surprised by service interruption.
expected_behaviour: fire_threshold_emails_if_crossed(tenant, before, after) is called by the reconcile cron and the in-flight 402 detector whenever estimated_cost_this_month changes. Checks if the update crossed the 90% threshold (sends approaching email) or 100% threshold (sends exhausted email). Both emails are idempotent via cost_warn_sent_at / cost_exhausted_email_sent_at timestamp markers on Tenant (cleared monthly by reset_monthly_counters). Skips tenants with no email, is_budget_exempt=True, or non-ACTIVE/SUSPENDED status. Sends multipart text+html email via DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL. Returns {warn: bool, exhausted: bool}.
inventory_notes: Idempotency is timestamp-based, not event-id-based — a second call within the same month with the counter already over the threshold is a no-op because cost_warn_sent_at is already set. Templates are in apps/router/templates/email/billing_quota/.

user_story: As the platform, I want to track per-model reachability and pricing so that the free-offer logic and the settings UI can show accurate health badges.
expected_behaviour: ModelHealth rows are upserted by two paths: (1) the model_health_check cron calls record_model_pricing() for MONITORED_MODELS on every tick; (2) the apps/common/openrouter.py chat_completion wrapper calls record_model_success() or record_model_failure() on every real inference call for any model. record_model_failure increments consecutive_failures and sets is_reachable=False. record_model_success resets consecutive_failures to 0 and sets is_reachable=True. _pricing_is_free checks prompt+completion both equal 0.0. offer_state() reads the ModelHealth row for the offer model to surface health badges in the settings API.
inventory_notes: Self-reporting via real traffic means the control-plane fallback wrapper updates health for models it actually uses (synthesis, extraction, arbiter), not just the probed offer model. Two sources: cron pricing reads + real-traffic success/failure callbacks.

feature: Free-model offer resolver for config generation
user_story: As the platform, I want to resolve the effective default primary model for a tenant based on the free-offer state so that all tenants on the rolling default use the free model while it's available.
expected_behaviour: resolve_default_primary_model(base_primary) returns FreeModelOffer.model_id when enabled=True and is_active=True and model_id is non-empty; otherwise returns base_primary (the tier's configured primary). Called by config_generator.py when computing the primary model for tenants with preferred_model=''. offer_model_entry() returns an allowlist fragment to merge into tier model entries while active. Both are pure DB reads — no HTTP, no side effects.
inventory_notes: Used at config generation time (bump_pending_config → apply_pending_configs cron). Late import of FreeModelOffer avoids circular import issues at Django setup time since this module is imported from config_generator.

user_story: As the platform, I want to read a sub-key's provider-side monthly usage via the OpenRouter self-read endpoint so that the reconcile cron can true up internal estimates against actual spend.
expected_behaviour: get_key_usage(api_key) calls GET /api/v1/key authenticated as the sub-key itself (no management key required) and returns data.usage_monthly as a Decimal. Returns Decimal(0) on any failure (network error, non-2xx, missing field) — the reconcile cron logs and continues. get_shared_key_usage() is a convenience wrapper using settings.OPENROUTER_API_KEY for platform-wide system-call usage tracking.
inventory_notes: This endpoint is authenticated by the sub-key, not the management key — the tenant's container can also call it directly to inspect its own usage. Returns Decimal(0) on any error to prevent a single OR outage from breaking all reconcile passes.

user_story: As an operator, I want to view usage records and monthly budget state in Django admin so that I can audit billing data.
expected_behaviour: UsageRecordAdmin is registered with list_display (event_type, tenant, input/output tokens, model_used, cost_estimate, created_at), list_filter by event_type, and date_hierarchy on created_at. MonthlyBudgetAdmin shows month/budget_dollars/spent_dollars/is_capped with is_capped filter. Standard Django admin CRUD — read + (superuser) write access.
inventory_notes: CreditLedger, DonationLedger, InfraCostSnapshot, ModelHealth, and FreeModelOffer are NOT registered in admin — management of those is done via management commands and the API.
error_detail: Confirmed. MonthlyBudgetAdmin puts is_capped in both list_display and list_filter (apps/billing/admin.py:23-24), but the is_capped BooleanField (apps/billing/models.py:128-131) is never written anywhere in the codebase. A repo-wide grep finds is_capped only in the model definition, its migration (0003_reconcile_refactor.py:41), and the admin registration — never assigned True and never read for enforcement. The actual budget-cap mechanism is entirely value-based: cap_budget sets spent_dollars = budget_dollars (cap_budget.py:55-56), and enforcement reads the is_over_budget property (spent_dollars >= budget_dollars, models.py:146-147), used by tenants/serializers.py:167 and usage_services.py. Because is_capped keeps default=False forever, the admin column always renders False and the list_filter Yes option returns zero rows even during a genuine cap. Impact: an operator auditing capped months via the Django admin gets a permanently misleading column/filter. Real, but strictly admin-internal cosmetic — it does not affect end users and does not break the cap itself (which works via spent_dollars). Hence low severity.

user_story: As the platform, I want to correctly attribute each AI inference to the actual upstream model so that per-model cost reporting is accurate.
expected_behaviour: resolve_model_for_attribution(tenant, result) extracts the real model id from an OpenClaw response via extract_model_from_response() which tries usage.model_used → usage.model → top-level model_used → top-level model, skipping the 'openclaw' placeholder. Falls back to resolve_tenant_primary_model(tenant) which tries applied_model → preferred_model → TIER_MODELS[tier][primary]. Logs at INFO when fallback fires. BYO models (anthropic/claude-*) in BYO_MODEL_DISPLAY are zero-cost for billing purposes but still receive a CreditLedger-exempt attribution.
inventory_notes: The 'openclaw' placeholder is the request-side model name in the OpenAI-spec field; the real upstream model id is in usage.model_used (OpenClaw 5.7+ custom field). Without this resolution logic every row bills at DEFAULT_RATE and per-model breakdown collapses.

user_story: As a system, I want a single Postgres row per tenant per provider to record whether an external AI credential has been connected, so the platform knows which model routes are available without ever storing the raw token.
expected_behaviour: The `BYOCredential` table (created by `byo_models.0001_initial`) has a UUID PK, ForeignKey to Tenant (CASCADE), `provider` (anthropic|openai), `mode` (api_key|cli_subscription), `key_vault_secret_name` (path into Azure Key Vault, never the token itself), `status` (pending|verified|expired|error), `last_verified_at`, `last_error` (TextField), `seed_version` (PositiveIntegerField, incremented on every paste), `created_at`/`updated_at`. A UniqueConstraint `byo_one_credential_per_provider` allows at most one row per (tenant, provider) pair. Attempting to create a second row for the same provider raises IntegrityError.
inventory_notes: seed_version is bumped unconditionally on each paste (even re-paste of the same token). Phase 2 design intends to compare it against an on-disk marker for file-share auth.json decisions; Phase 1 ignores it.
