user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see which external AI provider credentials I have connected so I can check their status and act on errors.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/tenants/byo-credentials/ requires a valid JWT (IsAuthenticated). If the requesting user has no tenant or the tenant has `byo_models_enabled=False`, returns 404. Otherwise returns a JSON array of serialized credentials for that tenant, ordered by provider. Each object includes id, provider, mode, status, last_verified_at (ISO-8601 or null), last_error (first 200 chars), created_at. The raw token, key_vault_secret_name, and seed_version are never included in the response.
inventory_notes: Returns empty array (not 404) when flag is on but no credentials exist. The 404-on-flag-off is intentional feature hiding.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to paste my Anthropic Claude CLI OAuth token into the platform so my assistant routes inference through my Anthropic subscription instead of platform credits.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/tenants/byo-credentials/ with JSON body `{provider, mode, token}`. Requires JWT auth. Returns 404 if `byo_models_enabled` is False. Phase 1 allows only `(anthropic, cli_subscription)`; any other (provider, mode) pair returns 400 `This provider/mode combination is not yet supported`. Token must be a string with length in [32, 4096]; otherwise 400 `Token format is invalid`. On valid input, calls `upsert_credential` which writes the token to Azure Key Vault and upserts the BYOCredential row (status=pending, seed_version incremented, last_error cleared) inside a DB transaction. Then sequentially: (1) `tenant.bump_pending_config()` + `regenerate_tenant_config(tenant)` rewrites openclaw.json on the file share and stamps `config_version=pending_config_version`; (2) `apply_byo_credentials_to_container(tenant)` reconciles the container's secrets/env and forces a new revision. Both post-credential steps are wrapped in separate try/except blocks — failures are logged but the 201 is still returned so the DB row is not orphaned. On KV write failure, returns 502 `Credential storage failed`. On tenant with no `key_vault_prefix`, returns 400. Token never appears in logs, response body, or tracebacks (RedactBYOPasteBody filter provides belt-and-suspenders scrubbing).
dependencies: Azure Key Vault, Azure Container Apps, Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: Order of operations is critical: openclaw.json regen MUST precede container env update (apply_byo_credentials_to_container). If reversed, the new revision boots with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY removed but agentRuntime not yet set, causing inference failures. If regenerate_tenant_config fails, the apply-pending-configs cron will eventually fix openclaw.json. A re-paste of the same provider overwrites the KV secret and resets status to pending.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to disconnect my Anthropic credential so the platform reverts to using its own shared API key for my inference.
expected_behaviour: DELETE /api/v1/tenants/byo-credentials/<uuid:cred_id>/ requires JWT auth. Returns 404 if flag is off or if the UUID does not belong to the requesting tenant. Calls `delete_credential(cred)` which removes the DB row first, then soft-deletes the KV secret (idempotent; KV soft-delete errors are logged and swallowed). Then sequentially calls regenerate_tenant_config (removes BYO model entries from openclaw.json) and apply_byo_credentials_to_container (restores ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env binding, removes CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN). Both post-delete steps are individually try/except wrapped. Returns 204 No Content on success.
dependencies: Azure Key Vault, Azure Container Apps, Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: KV uses soft-delete with a 7–90 day retention window. If the tenant reconnects within that window, `_write_secret_to_kv` must recover the deleted secret first (see KV soft-delete recovery below).

feature: Azure Key Vault secret upsert with soft-delete recovery
user_story: As the platform, I want to write a BYO token to Key Vault and automatically recover from soft-deleted secrets so a subscriber can disconnect and reconnect without errors.
expected_behaviour: `_write_secret_to_kv` in `apps/byo_models/services.py` calls `SecretClient.set_secret`. If the KV name is in the soft-deleted state (retention window not elapsed), Azure returns `ResourceExistsError` with message containing `ObjectIsDeletedButRecoverable`. The service calls `begin_recover_deleted_secret(...).wait()` to restore it, then calls `set_secret` again to overwrite with the new value. Any other `ResourceExistsError` (unrecognized conflict reason) is re-raised immediately. In test/AZURE_MOCK mode, writes to the module-level `_BYO_MOCK_KV_STORE` dict instead.
inventory_notes: Secret naming uses `<tenant.key_vault_prefix>-byo-<provider>-<mode_with_underscores_replaced_by_hyphens>` — underscore sanitization is mandatory because KV rejects `^[0-9a-zA-Z-]+$` violations.

feature: Azure Key Vault secret soft-delete on disconnect
user_story: As the platform, I want to soft-delete the KV secret when a subscriber disconnects their credential so the token is no longer accessible by the container.
expected_behaviour: `_delete_secret_from_kv` calls `SecretClient.begin_delete_secret(...).wait()`. Any exception (secret already gone, RBAC error) is logged at WARNING level and swallowed — the caller (delete_credential) proceeds to remove the DB row regardless. In AZURE_MOCK mode pops from `_BYO_MOCK_KV_STORE`.
inventory_notes: Soft-delete leaves the secret recoverable for 7–90 days depending on vault policy. If the reconnect happens within that window, _write_secret_to_kv handles the recovery.

feature: Container env reconciliation on credential change
user_story: As the platform, I want to atomically update the tenant container's secret bindings and force a new revision whenever a BYO credential is added or removed so the change takes effect immediately.
expected_behaviour: `apply_byo_credentials_to_container` fetches the current Container App state via Azure SDK. It reconciles the `secrets` list: drops any existing `claude-code-oauth-token` entry, then re-adds it as a KV-backed secret if an active (non-ERROR, non-disconnected) Anthropic CLI credential exists. For the `openclaw` container's env, it strips both `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` and `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, then re-adds whichever is appropriate: `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` referencing the BYO secret (when cred is active) or `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` referencing `anthropic-key` (when no cred). A new revision suffix is computed as `b<sha256[:6]>` of a nanosecond timestamp. `begin_create_or_update(...).result()` is called synchronously. No-op if tenant has no `container_id`; mock mode skips all SDK calls.
dependencies: Azure Container Apps, Azure Key Vault
inventory_notes: Anthropic's CLI ranks ANTHROPIC_API_KEY above CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN in auth precedence. Removing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY when BYO is active is required to prevent the platform key from winning and billing against shared API credits. Per microsoft/azure-container-apps#856, a plain container restart keeps cached KV values; only a new revision triggers a KV re-fetch.

feature: BYO model registry injection into openclaw.json
user_story: As the platform, I want to include Anthropic Claude model IDs in the tenant's openclaw.json config when a valid BYO credential exists, so the container's model picker can offer those models.
expected_behaviour: `_byo_model_extras(tenant)` in config_generator returns an empty dict if `tenant.byo_models_enabled` is False or no BYO credential for (anthropic, cli_subscription) exists with a status other than ERROR. When a valid credential exists, returns `{'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6': {'alias': 'claude-sonnet'}, 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-5': {'alias': 'claude-opus'}}`. These are merged into the tenant's model allowlist by `resolve_tenant_models`. When the resolved primary model is a BYO model, `fallbacks_list` is explicitly set to `[]` so billing/auth errors surface instead of silently switching to a platform-metered model. `generate_openclaw_config` also adds `cliBackends.claude-cli.command=/opt/nbhd/claude-with-token.sh` when BYO Anthropic Sonnet is in extras.
inventory_notes: Model IDs must use the canonical `anthropic/<model>` prefix. The `anthropic-cli/<model>` prefix is a UI choiceId only in OpenClaw's plugin manifest and is not a valid registry key (bug caught in PR #421/migration 0050). Error-status credentials are excluded from extras — user must reconnect to restore BYO routing.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As the platform, I want to synchronously rewrite the tenant's openclaw.json on the Azure File Share whenever a BYO credential is added or removed so the next container revision boots with the correct config.
expected_behaviour: `regenerate_tenant_config(tenant)` calls `update_tenant_config(str(tenant.id))` (from orchestrator.services), which writes a fresh openclaw.json to the file share. Then it stamps the Tenant row with `config_version=pending_config_version`, `config_refreshed_at=now()`, `applied_model=tenant.preferred_model`, `applied_model_at=now()`. This is a no-op in AZURE_MOCK mode. Designed to run before `apply_byo_credentials_to_container` so the new revision already has the correct config when it boots.
inventory_notes: If this step fails, the apply-pending-configs cron (hourly) will eventually fix the file share. The container env reconciliation still runs — a window exists where the container's env and openclaw.json are transiently mismatched.

user_story: As the OpenClaw container runtime, I want to report a billing or auth failure on a BYO route to Django so the subscriber sees a clear error message in the UI instead of a silent failure.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/internal/runtime/<tenant_id>/byo/error/ (also wired at /api/v1/integrations/runtime/<tenant_id>/byo/error/). No DRF authentication class; authentication is via the internal auth check `_internal_auth_or_401`. Body: `{provider, reason, message, model_used}`. `provider` must be `anthropic` (only supported value); unknown provider → 400. `reason` must be one of `billing|auth|auth_permanent` to flip the credential; other reasons (e.g. rate-limit) return 200 `{status: ignored}` without mutating state. For actionable reasons, calls `mark_credential_error(tenant, provider, hint)` where `hint` is a user-friendly string from `_BYO_ERROR_HINT` (or the first 200 chars of `message`). Returns 200 `{status: ok, credential_id: <uuid>}` if credential was found and updated, or 200 `{status: no_credential}` if no matching non-pending credential exists (idempotent — runtime should not retry either way).
inventory_notes: Error hints are hardcoded per-provider/reason (billing: top-up URL; auth: reconnect; auth_permanent: revoked). Pending credentials are never flipped to error by this endpoint (mark_credential_error skips PENDING status). The endpoint is AllowAny + custom internal auth — not JWT-gated.

user_story: As the platform, I want a safe, idempotent function to flip a BYO credential to error status with a user-facing message so the AI Provider UI can surface the root cause.
expected_behaviour: `mark_credential_error(tenant, provider, last_error)` filters for a BYO credential matching tenant + provider that is NOT in PENDING status. If none found, returns None (credential may have been disconnected after the failure). Otherwise updates `status=error` and `last_error=<truncated_message>` (max 240 chars, truncated with `…`). Returns the refreshed BYOCredential row. Safe to call repeatedly with the same message.
inventory_notes: Skips PENDING credentials intentionally — they haven't completed first verification so an error during reconnect would be confusing. The 240-char truncation matches the one-line mobile banner in `BYOProviderCard`.

user_story: As the platform, I want a defensive logging filter on the BYO paste endpoint so that even if future code or middleware accidentally logs request bodies, the OAuth token never reaches log analytics.
expected_behaviour: `RedactBYOPasteBody` (in `apps/byo_models/logging_filters.py`) is a `logging.Filter` attached to the console handler in production settings. For every log record, it checks whether any of `msg`, `request`, `path`, `request_path`, `url` attributes contain `/api/v1/tenants/byo-credentials/`. If so, it replaces any `{...}` JSON-shaped substring in `msg` and `args` with `[REDACTED]`, and sets `body`, `data`, `request_body` attributes (if present) to `[REDACTED]`. Always returns True (never drops records).
inventory_notes: Belt-and-suspenders only — the primary defense is that view code never puts the token into a named variable visible in tracebacks or log calls. The regex is greedy across newlines (`re.DOTALL`) so multi-line JSON bodies are also redacted.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to switch my assistant's primary AI model to any model available on my tier (including BYO models I've connected) so the assistant uses that model for future turns.
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/tenants/settings/preferred-model/ with body `{preferred_model: <model_id>}`. JWT auth required. Validates the requested model_id against `_get_allowed_models(tenant)`, which merges tier base models + active free offer + BYO extras from `_byo_model_extras`. An empty string clears the preference (reverts to tier default). A model not in the allowlist returns 400 `Model not available for your tier`. On success, saves `tenant.preferred_model`, calls `tenant.bump_pending_config()`, and enqueues an immediate QStash `apply_single_tenant_config` task (idempotency-keyed). Returns `{preferred_model, model_tier}`. QStash publish failures are logged but not raised.
inventory_notes: Skips enqueuing if tenant has no container_id, is not ACTIVE, or is hibernated. Hourly apply-pending-configs cron is the safety net. BYO models are only offered in the allowlist when the credential is in a non-ERROR status.

user_story: As the OpenClaw assistant runtime, I want to switch the tenant's preferred model on behalf of the user mid-conversation so the change persists after the session ends.
expected_behaviour: GET/POST /api/v1/tenants/runtime/<tenant_id>/preferred-model/ with internal auth. GET returns `{preferred_model, applied_model, model_tier, allowed_models: [{model_id, alias}]}` where `allowed_models` includes tier models + BYO extras. POST body `{model_id}`: if non-empty, validates against `_get_allowed_models` (same allowlist as consumer API — tier + BYO); model not in allowlist returns 400 with full state payload so the runtime can surface the reason. On success, saves `preferred_model`, bumps pending config, enqueues immediate apply. An empty `model_id` clears to tier default. Returns `{updated: True, ...state}` on success.
inventory_notes: Uses AllowAny + internal auth (not JWT). Mirrors the consumer PreferredModelView so the runtime cannot select a model that the subscriber's tier doesn't permit.
error_detail: Confirmed asymmetry between the runtime endpoint's serializer and its validator. RuntimePreferredModelView._state() (runtime_views.py:208-221) builds allowed_models from only {**TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS[tier], **_byo_model_extras(tenant)} and never calls offer_model_entry(). But the POST validation path uses _get_allowed_models(tenant) (views.py:793-805), whose base is {**offer_model_entry(), **TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS[tier], ...}, so it WILL accept the live free-offer model id. resolve_tenant_models (config_generator.py:1047-1051) likewise leads the real allowlist with the offer model, confirming it is genuinely selectable. The mismatch: GET state and the model_not_allowed 400 error body (which embeds **self._state(tenant) and tells the agent "Available models are listed in allowed_models") both return a list that OMITS the live offer model. The internal assistant agent — the only consumer of this endpoint — cannot learn the free-offer model id from this surface, so it may report that model as unavailable or omit it when listing selectable models, even though a POST for it would succeed. Impact is bounded: the offer is the rolling default (preferred_model == ''), so tenants on the default are already running it; the harm is only in the agent's representation of which models are available, not in actual model switching. Hence low severity.

feature: BYO feature flag on Tenant model (byo_models_enabled)
user_story: As an operator, I want to enable or disable the BYO credential feature per tenant so I can roll it out gradually or emergency-disable it for a specific user.
expected_behaviour: `Tenant.byo_models_enabled` is a BooleanField defaulting to True (migration `tenants.0051_byo_models_enabled_default_true` bulk-set True for all non-deleted tenants). When False, all three BYO credential endpoints (list/paste/delete) return 404 and `_byo_model_extras` returns an empty dict. The field is exposed read-only in `TenantSerializer` and included in the settings API response. Enable path is effectively deprecated (fleet-wide True is the new default); disable path is still used for per-tenant emergency opt-out.
inventory_notes: The 404-on-flag-off pattern intentionally does not advertise that the feature exists. The serializer exposes the flag value to the frontend so the AI Provider tab can be shown/hidden accordingly.

user_story: As an operator, I want a management command to disable BYO for a single tenant as an emergency opt-out without touching the rest of the fleet.
expected_behaviour: `python manage.py enable_byo --tenant <uuid>` is a no-op with a DEPRECATED warning since migration 0051 made the flag True fleet-wide. `--disable` flag is the only path that still mutates: sets `tenant.byo_models_enabled=False` and saves. If already False, prints a no-op warning. Unknown tenant UUID raises CommandError. No `--disable` + flag already True → success message. Re-enabling requires a direct DB update or data migration.
inventory_notes: The enable path is explicitly a no-op to preserve backward compatibility for operator runbooks. A future re-enable must be done via a data migration or direct DB write.

user_story: As the platform, I want to migrate stale preferred_model values that used the wrong `anthropic-cli/` prefix to the canonical `anthropic/` prefix so BYO model routing works correctly after PR #421.
expected_behaviour: Data migration `tenants.0050_migrate_anthropic_cli_preferred_model` runs a single `UPDATE` on all Tenant rows where `preferred_model='anthropic-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6'`, setting it to `'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6'`. Reverse is a no-op. Applied once on deploy; idempotent.
inventory_notes: The `anthropic-cli/` prefix was a UI choiceId (not a valid OpenClaw model registry key) introduced in PR #419. Using it caused 'Unknown model' errors and fallback to MiniMax.

user_story: As the platform, I want to bulk-enable BYO for all non-deleted tenants in a single migration so the feature rolls out to the entire fleet atomically on deploy.
expected_behaviour: Migration `tenants.0051_byo_models_enabled_default_true` runs `Tenant.objects.exclude(status='deleted').update(byo_models_enabled=True)` and changes the model field default to True so new tenants get BYO enabled automatically. Reverse migration sets all tenants to False. Applied once on the 2026-05-02 deploy.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want the settings API to show me the actual model my assistant is using right now (including BYO selection and free-offer overrides) so I can see the real active model.
expected_behaviour: `effective_primary_model(tenant)` calls `resolve_tenant_models(tenant)` and returns the primary from its result. `resolve_tenant_models` applies a four-step resolution: (1) tier default, (2) limited-time free offer override, (3) BYO extras appended to the allowlist, (4) tenant.preferred_model wins if it's in the allowlist. When BYO primary is selected, fallbacks are emptied. `TenantSerializer.get_effective_model` calls this; any exception falls back to `tenant.preferred_model or ''` so the settings payload is never broken by a config error.
inventory_notes: BYO extras require the credential to be non-ERROR status. A credential in ERROR state is excluded and the model is not offered in the allowlist, so the preferred_model may not match any allowed entry — the resolution falls through to the tier default.

feature: BYO secret name builder and KV naming contract
user_story: As the platform, I want a deterministic and Azure-compliant Key Vault secret name for each BYO credential so the container can reference it via a stable KV path.
expected_behaviour: `secret_name_for(tenant, provider, mode)` returns `<tenant.key_vault_prefix>-byo-<provider>-<mode_sanitized>` where sanitization replaces `_` with `-` (e.g. `cli_subscription` → `cli-subscription`). Raises `ValueError` if `tenant.key_vault_prefix` is empty. The resulting name must match `^[0-9a-zA-Z-]+$` (Azure Key Vault constraint). This name is stored in `BYOCredential.key_vault_secret_name` and used as both the KV secret path and the Container App secret reference name.
inventory_notes: Underscore-to-hyphen sanitization is one-way and stable; the DB enum value (`cli_subscription`) is unchanged. Without this, Azure returns 400 BadParameter on set_secret.

feature: BYO-aware allowed models set for tier gating
user_story: As a subscriber, I want the model picker and per-task model validation to include my connected BYO models so I can select them from any surface that offers model choice.
expected_behaviour: `_get_allowed_models(tenant)` in `apps/tenants/views.py` merges the active free offer (if any), the tier's `TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS` dict, and BYO extras from `_byo_model_extras(tenant)`. This combined dict is used by both `PreferredModelView.patch` (subscriber API) and `RuntimePreferredModelView.post` (container API) to gate model selection. A model not in the union dict gets a 400 rejection. This ensures BYO models are selectable only when the credential is non-ERROR.
inventory_notes: BYO models do not belong to any billing tier — they are additive on top of the tier allowlist regardless of the subscriber's plan level.
error_detail: Confirmed inconsistency. RuntimePreferredModelView._state() (runtime_views.py:208-213) builds its allowed_models advisory list from only TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS + _byo_model_extras(tenant). The POST write gate at line 250 instead calls _get_allowed_models(tenant) (views.py:793-805), which additionally merges offer_model_entry() (the limited-time free model). offer_model_entry() (apps/billing/model_offers.py:80-86) returns the offer model entry whenever offer_is_active(), else {}. Consequence: during an active free offer, the offer model IS accepted by POST but is NOT listed in the allowed_models array returned by GET state, the 200 POST success body, or the 400 rejection body — even though the 400 detail string tells the assistant "Available models are listed in 'allowed_models'." This is an assistant-facing advisory surface (internal AllowAny endpoint hit by the in-container OpenClaw runtime), not a direct end-user surface, and only diverges during the active-offer window. Impact is bounded: the in-container assistant reading its own state could fail to surface the free model or claim it is unavailable, contradicting the write gate that actually accepts it. No security/data-integrity/write-path correctness is affected — switching TO the offer model still succeeds. The feature record's own expected_behaviour explicitly lists "the active free offer (if any)" in the allowed-models union, so _state()'s omission diverges from the documented contract.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want API GET responses to be cached per-tenant so that repeated dashboard or data reads are fast without hitting the database on every request.
expected_behaviour: The `@tenant_cache(ttl, tag)` decorator in `apps/common/cache.py` wraps DRF view `get()` methods. On a GET, it resolves `request.user.tenant`; if absent (anonymous or no-tenant user) the view runs uncached. It reads the current tag version from Redis (key `nbhd:tag:<tenant_id>:<tag>:v`), builds a cache key of `nbhd:view:<qualname>:<tenant_id>:<tag>:<tag_v>:<md5_sig>` where the sig covers path, sorted query params, and URL kwargs, then returns cached `(data, status)` wrapped in a fresh DRF `Response` with `X-Cache: HIT`. On a miss the view runs; if status 200, the result is stored at the configured TTL. Non-200 responses are not cached. If Redis is unavailable (`tag_v == 0`), the view runs uncached and the response gets `X-Cache: BYPASS`. Only GET requests are intercepted; other methods pass through immediately.
dependencies: Upstash Redis (via Django cache backend); falls back to LocMemCache in dev/tests
inventory_notes: Falls back to LocMemCache when Redis unavailable — invalidation is process-local only in that case. Tag version 0 means backend unhealthy; decorator bypasses rather than raising 500. Cache key includes tag version so atomic `INCR` on the tag key invalidates all reads for that tenant+tag without scanning Redis. Tag TTL is 30 days.

user_story: As a system operation, I want cache tags to be automatically invalidated when relevant models are mutated so that subscribers never see stale data after a write.
expected_behaviour: The `apps/common/cache_signals.py` module registers `post_save` and `post_delete` Django signal receivers for every data model that backs a cached API view. On any save or delete, `_tenant_id(instance)` resolves the tenant via `instance.tenant` or `instance.tenant_id`, then calls `bump_tags(tenant_id, tags)` which increments each affected tag version in Redis. Affected tag mappings: Workout/BodyWeightLog → `["fuel", "dashboard"]`; WorkoutPlan/PersonalRecord/RestingHeartRateLog/SleepLog/FuelGoal/FuelProfile → `["fuel"]`; JournalEntry/DailyNote → `["journal", "dashboard"]`; Document → `["journal", "sidebar"]`; Tenant → `["dashboard", "tenant"]`. Exceptions are caught and logged; signal handlers never raise into the request that triggered the save.
dependencies: Upstash Redis; apps.fuel, apps.journal, apps.tenants models
inventory_notes: Registered in `CommonConfig.ready()` so signals wire automatically on startup. If `_tenant_id` returns None (e.g. a top-level model with no tenant FK), the bump is silently skipped. Exception in bump is logged but not re-raised — a Redis failure during a write does not break the write request itself.
error_detail: The code asymmetry is genuine: in apps/common/cache_signals.py the _profile_changed handler (lines 86-88) is decorated only with @receiver(post_save, sender=FuelProfile), whereas all 7 other fuel models (Workout, WorkoutPlan, BodyWeightLog, PersonalRecord, RestingHeartRateLog, SleepLog, FuelGoal) and the journal/document models carry both post_save and post_delete. The feature record's expected_behaviour says signals register "for every data model" and lists FuelProfile -> ["fuel"] among mutation-bumping models, so the missing post_delete is a real deviation from the documented contract. However, real-world impact is effectively nil: FuelProfile is a OneToOneField(Tenant, on_delete=CASCADE) singleton (apps/fuel/models.py:321) that no application code ever deletes — it is only ever created via get_or_create and updated (apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:651, apps/fuel/views.py:156). The only delete paths are (a) cascade when the Tenant itself is deleted during deprovisioning, where a stale "fuel" tag belongs to a now-gone tenant with no consumer, and (b) a manual Django-admin delete (registered apps/fuel/admin.py:31), after which the next read/write re-creates the profile via get_or_create and fires post_save, bumping the tag. The cached fuel views also carry DEFAULT_TTL = 60s (apps/common/cache.py:25), bounding any stale window to under a minute. So no real user or operator would observe stale fuel-profile data in practice; this is a consistency / defense-in-depth gap, not a live defect.

user_story: As an admin/operator, I want a quick smoke-test of the Redis cache backend so that I can confirm cache connectivity during health checks and deploys.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/cache.py:ping()` sets the key `nbhd:health:cache-ping` to `'ok'` with a 5-second TTL and immediately reads it back, returning `True` on match and `False` on any exception. Called by the `check_health` management command (`apps/orchestrator/management/commands/check_health.py`) which includes the result in its tabular and JSON output. Never raises; all exceptions are logged and return `False`.
inventory_notes: The 5-second TTL means a test key is never left in Redis longer than 5 s. The management command is also called by CI after deploy (`make health`).

user_story: As a subscriber using the web or mobile app, I want conditional GET support so that my client can avoid re-downloading unchanged responses and reduce bandwidth and latency.
expected_behaviour: `config/cache_middleware.ETagMiddleware` runs as the second outermost middleware (after `RequestTimingMiddleware`). For every 200 GET response with a non-streaming body it forces DRF rendering if not yet rendered, computes `ETag: "<md5-hex>"` over `response.content`, and writes it to the response. If the request carries `If-None-Match` equal to the computed ETag it returns HTTP 304 `HttpResponseNotModified` with `ETag`, `Cache-Control`, `Vary`, and `X-Cache` headers propagated. On a miss it sets `Cache-Control: private, max-age=10, stale-while-revalidate=60` (if not already set) and appends `Authorization` to `Vary` to prevent cross-tenant leakage by CDN or proxy. Non-200, non-GET, and streaming responses are passed through unchanged.
inventory_notes: Runs after `@tenant_cache` so it covers both cache HITs and MISSes identically. `X-Cache` header is propagated onto the 304 so `RequestTimingMiddleware` logging can distinguish HIT/MISS even on a 304 response. md5 is used only for caching (not security), hence `usedforsecurity=False`.

feature: Per-request timing and DB query count logging
user_story: As an admin/operator, I want every non-health request to emit structured PERF log lines so that I can trace latency regressions and N+1 query problems in Azure Container App logs.
expected_behaviour: `config/middleware.RequestTimingMiddleware` is the outermost middleware, wrapping the entire request lifecycle. It skips paths matching `/health`, `/static/`, `/favicon.ico`, and `/admin/jsi18n/`. For all other requests it uses `connection.execute_wrapper` to count DB queries while the downstream stack processes the request, then logs `PERF <method> <path> status=<N> total_ms=<N> db_queries=<N> cache=<HIT|MISS|BYPASS|->` at INFO level on the `nbhd.perf` logger. The `cache` field is taken from `response['X-Cache']` if present, otherwise `-`.
inventory_notes: Must be the first entry in MIDDLEWARE to capture total request time including all inner middleware. Health endpoint is excluded to avoid log noise from readiness probes.

user_story: As a subscriber using a Personal Access Token (PAT) to push sessions from external tools, I want rate limits applied per PAT so that a misbehaving integration cannot exhaust my account's resources.
expected_behaviour: `apps/tenants/throttling.py` defines `PATSessionIngestMinuteThrottle` (60/minute) and `PATSessionIngestDayThrottle` (5000/day) both extending `_PATScopedThrottle` which keys on `request.auth_pat.id`. Returns `None` (no throttle applied) for non-PAT-authenticated requests, so JWT/UI traffic is unaffected. DRF returns HTTP 429 `Too Many Requests` when the bucket is exhausted. `UserPATMintHourThrottle` (10/hour) is user-scoped (by `request.user.pk`) and guards the PAT minting endpoint against bulk token creation.
inventory_notes: Throttle state lives in the same Redis backend as the cache. Revoking one PAT isolates blast radius without affecting other tokens for the same user.

feature: Feature-specific rate throttles (chat, Siri, push, OAuth PKCE)
user_story: As a subscriber, I want per-feature rate limits enforced on AI, push-notification, and OAuth endpoints so that runaway clients cannot rack up unbounded model spend or APNs load.
expected_behaviour: `apps/tenants/throttling.py` defines user-scoped (`_UserScopedThrottle`) throttle classes applied to specific views: `ChatLocalTurnHourThrottle` (240/hour) for local turn record writes; `ChatContextHourThrottle` (120/hour) for the context digest endpoint; `SiriRespondMinuteThrottle` (30/minute) for the Tier-2 Siri fast-responder LLM call; `SiriStatusMinuteThrottle` (60/minute) for the Siri status snapshot (no-LLM); `PushTestMinuteThrottle` (10/minute) for the self-service APNs test-push; `AuthorizeMintMinuteThrottle` (30/minute) for the web→app PKCE begin endpoint. `ExchangeMinuteThrottle` (30/minute) is IP-based (`X-Forwarded-For`) because the PKCE exchange endpoint is `AllowAny`. All return HTTP 429 on exhaustion.
inventory_notes: `ExchangeMinuteThrottle` reads `HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR` to honour the Azure Container Apps proxy header, matching the logic in `auth_views._client_ip`. Unauthenticated requests to user-scoped throttles return `None` (no throttle) rather than defaulting to IP throttling.

feature: Parameterised query view base class with internal-auth gate
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want to POST structured queries to domain data endpoints so that the AI assistant can retrieve filtered, time-windowed data for any domain (finance, fuel, journal, insights) without writing raw SQL.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/query_view.BaseQueryView` is an `AllowAny` DRF `APIView` subclass. On `POST /api/v1/<domain>/runtime/<tenant_id>/query/` it first validates `X-NBHD-Internal-Key` + `X-NBHD-Tenant-Id` headers via `validate_internal_runtime_request`; mismatch or missing key returns 401 `{error: internal_auth_failed}`. On auth success it sets RLS context with `service_role=True`. It resolves the tenant or returns 404. It validates the request body against the subclass `query_model` Pydantic model; validation failure returns 400 `{error: validation_failed, detail: [...]}`. It resolves any `window` field via `resolve_window` in tenant tz. It calls `self.execute(query, tenant, window_resolved)` which subclasses implement; `QueryExecutionError` raised there becomes a structured error at the configured `status_code`. On success it returns a response envelope containing `data`, `meta.query_hash` (sha256 over canonical query tuple), `meta.computed_at`, `meta.tenant_tz`, `meta.window_resolved_to`, `meta.row_count`, `meta.has_more`, and `meta.schema_version`. Decimal/UUID/date values are coerced to strings/ISO via `jsonify`.
inventory_notes: Permission class is `AllowAny` — security is entirely via `X-NBHD-Internal-Key` validation, not Django auth. `has_more` is only set when `limit` is present and `row_count >= limit`. `query_hash` is reproducible for the same logical query at any real-clock time, enabling agent replay and test assertions. All exceptions in `execute()` that are not `QueryExecutionError` propagate upward as 500s.

feature: Deterministic time-window resolution for agent queries
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want to specify time windows by semantic name (today, last_n_days, month_to_date, etc.) so that the AI assistant can query data for meaningful periods without hard-coding date arithmetic.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/windows.Window` is a Pydantic model accepted as part of domain query request bodies. `kind` is a literal enum with 14 values: `today`, `yesterday`, `tomorrow`, `all`, `last_n_days` (int value 1–730), `next_n_days` (1–730), `last_n_weeks` (1–104), `last_n_months` (1–24), `this_week`, `last_week`, `month_to_date`, `last_month`, `year_to_date`, `last_year`, `since` (date value), `between` ([from_date, to_date]). `_validate_value_per_kind` enforces that value-less kinds have `value=None` and value-bearing kinds have an in-range value; mismatches raise Pydantic `ValueError` surfaced as 400. `resolve_window(w, tz_name)` converts to a closed `(from_date, to_date)` tuple in the tenant's IANA timezone; `kind='all'` returns `None` so the caller skips the date filter. Week boundaries are ISO (Monday-anchored). `last_n_months` clamps to the last valid day of short months.
inventory_notes: `resolve_window` accepts an optional `now` parameter for testability; production callers omit it. `this_week`/`last_week` are ISO Monday-to-Sunday, not Sunday-to-Saturday. `between` validates `from_date <= to_date` at the Pydantic layer.

user_story: As a system component, I want a single authoritative function to resolve a tenant's IANA timezone so that date arithmetic is consistently user-local across all features.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/tenant_tz.py` exposes `tenant_tz_name(tenant) -> str` (IANA name or `'UTC'`), `tenant_tz(tenant) -> ZoneInfo`, `safe_zoneinfo(name) -> ZoneInfo` (UTC on missing/unknown), and `tenant_today(tenant) -> date` (today in tenant's local tz). All read `tenant.user.timezone`; if the field is absent, empty, or not a known IANA zone, the function falls back to UTC without raising. This module is import-free of `apps.tenants` (accepts `Any`) to prevent circular imports.
inventory_notes: Used by `BaseQueryView`, `resolve_window`, `resolve_relative_date`, cron scheduling, and any per-tenant date logic. `tenant_today` uses `django.utils.timezone.now()` so it is always UTC-aware before converting.

feature: Relative-date resolution for LLM tool inputs
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want to translate date phrases like 'yesterday', 'Monday', or '3 days ago' into absolute dates in the tenant's timezone so that the AI assistant produces correct date-specific data writes without embedding UTC-based date arithmetic in its prompt.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/llm_contracts.resolve_relative_date(tenant, phrase)` maps common natural-language date phrases to a concrete `date` in the tenant's local timezone. Recognised: `'today'`/`''`/`None` → today; `'yesterday'` → today−1; `'tomorrow'` → today+1; weekday names and 3-letter abbreviations (case-insensitive) → most recent past weekday (same-weekday → previous week); `'N days ago'`; `'in N days'`/`'N days'`; ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` strings. Returns `None` for unrecognised input — callers should prompt the user for clarification. `today_in_tenant_tz(tenant)` is a shortcut for the `'today'` case.
inventory_notes: When today is Monday, 'Monday' resolves to last Monday (not today) — this is intentional to avoid ambiguity in tool calls made early in the day. Returns `None` rather than raising on unknown inputs so callers can handle them gracefully.

user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want Pydantic validation failures in tool calls to be returned as machine-readable structured JSON so that the AI assistant can self-correct and retry with fixed field values.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/llm_contracts.LLMValidationError` is a Pydantic model with fields `error='validation_failed'`, `message` (human-readable count + instruction to retry), and `details` (list of `{loc, msg, type}` dicts). `LLMValidationError.from_pydantic(exc)` converts a Pydantic `ValidationError` to this form. `as_tool_result()` returns `model_dump()`. Runtime views in fuel and other domains catch `ValidationError` and return this envelope as the HTTP response body (200 or 400) so the agent's tool-result message contains structured, self-correctable data rather than an opaque exception string.
inventory_notes: Error shape is documented as stable — agents are trained (via rules/_principles.md) to read the `details` array and retry. The `type` field carries Pydantic's machine error codes (`missing`, `value_error`, `literal_error`, etc.) which are more reliable for automated correction than English prose messages.

feature: Exercise registry normalisation and unit conversion
user_story: As an OpenClaw container runtime, I want free-text exercise names to be normalised to canonical categories and metric types server-side so that mis-classifications by the LLM cannot corrupt workout data with wrong units.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/llm_lookups.normalize_exercise(name)` resolves a free-text exercise name against a closed registry of ~110 canonical entries to `(category, default_metric)`. Resolution order: (1) `weighted <X>` prefix upgrades a bodyweight movement to `('strength', 'weighted_reps')`; (2) exact match; (3) longest substring match. Returns `None` if no match — callers fall back to the LLM-proposed value and should surface a warning. Categories: `strength`, `calisthenics`, `cardio`, `mobility`. Metrics: `weighted_reps`, `bodyweight_reps`, `hold_time`, `distance_time`, `blocks`. `convert_weight(value, from_unit, to_unit)` and `convert_distance(value, from_unit, to_unit)` provide exact unit conversion between kg/lbs and km/mi using fixed constants, raising `ValueError` on unsupported unit pairs.
inventory_notes: Longest-substring match means 'side plank with rotation' resolves to 'side plank'. The registry is the canonical source of truth — exercise names should be added here rather than expanding the LLM's context window. Float conversions use exact constants (`_LBS_PER_KG = 2.20462262185`) rather than rounded approximations.

feature: OpenRouter multi-model fallback client with health recording
user_story: As a system component making LLM calls for synthesis, extraction, or PII arbitration, I want OpenRouter calls to fall through an ordered list of model candidates so that a single model's unavailability does not silently break the feature.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/openrouter.chat_completion(models, messages, ...)` accepts an ordered list of OpenClaw-form model ids (e.g. `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`). `normalize_model_id` strips the `openrouter/` prefix before the HTTP call. For each candidate it POSTs to `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions` with `Authorization: Bearer <OPENROUTER_API_KEY>`. A 200 response is only considered usable if `choices[0].message.content` is non-empty (OpenRouter can return 200 with a top-level `error` on upstream failure). On usable response it calls `record_model_success(model_id)` and returns `(response_json, model_used)`. On failure (network error, non-200, or empty choices) it calls `record_model_failure(model_id, repr(exc))`, logs a warning, and tries the next candidate. If all candidates fail, re-raises the last exception. Raises `RuntimeError` immediately if `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is not configured.
inventory_notes: Health recording (`record_model_success`/`record_model_failure`) is fail-soft — exceptions in those calls are swallowed so model health recording never breaks an actual LLM call. `record_health=False` skips health recording for test scenarios. Timeout per attempt is 45s by default.

user_story: As a system component, I want to send push notifications to iOS devices via Apple Push Notification service so that subscribers are alerted when an AI turn completes or a proactive message is ready, even if the app is backgrounded.
expected_behaviour: `apps/common/apns.send_push(tokens, title, body, ...)` sends an APNs HTTP/2 POST to `/3/device/<token>` for each device token. It requires all four `APNS_AUTH_KEY`, `APNS_KEY_ID`, `APNS_TEAM_ID`, `APNS_BUNDLE_ID` env vars set; otherwise returns `{skipped: 'not_configured'}`. Requires `httpx` with `h2` package; returns `{skipped: 'http2_unavailable'}` if HTTP/2 is absent. Provider JWT (`ES256`) is cached for 50 minutes and refreshed when within expiry. `collapse_id` is truncated to 64 UTF-8 bytes (Apple's limit). `content_available=True` adds `content-available: 1` for a hybrid alert+silent-wake push. APNs 410 or 400/`BadDeviceToken` responses append the token to `result['unregistered']` so callers can prune stale tokens. `sandbox` param selects the APNs host (`api.sandbox.push.apple.com` vs `api.push.apple.com`); defaults to `APNS_USE_SANDBOX` setting. Never raises — all paths return a result dict.
inventory_notes: Module-level JWT cache (`_jwt_cache`) means all in-process calls share one token. When `APNS_USE_SANDBOX` is False (production), tokens from Debug builds will be rejected by Apple — callers must pass `sandbox` based on the stored device environment. The `collapse_id` truncation operates on the byte boundary to avoid breaking multibyte UTF-8 characters and getting a 400.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to toggle the Core mindfulness feature on or off so that the AI assistant gains or loses the meditation plugin.
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/core/settings/ requires JWT auth. Accepts {"core_enabled": bool}; 400 if field absent; 404 if no tenant. Flips Tenant.core_enabled, calls tenant.bump_pending_config(), enqueues a QStash task 'apply_single_tenant_config'. On fresh enable (off→on) clears the 'core' key from Tenant.welcomes_sent so the welcome re-fires. Creates CoreProfile via get_or_create if enabling. Returns {core_enabled, core_profile_status, restart_required}; restart_required is true when the plugin state changed AND the tenant has a container_id, signalling the frontend to prompt a restart.
inventory_notes: Does NOT restart the container itself — that is a separate POST /core/restart/. The welcome-marker clear is intentional so that re-enabling Core re-delivers the welcome message.

feature: Restart Assistant Container for Core Plugin
user_story: As a subscriber, I want to restart my AI assistant container so that the newly-enabled Core meditation plugin is picked up by OpenClaw.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/core/restart/ requires JWT auth; 404 if no tenant, 400 if no container_id. Calls restart_container_app(tenant.container_id) via the Azure SDK; returns 502 on failure. On success, if core_enabled=true and the CoreProfile.onboarding_status is 'pending', enqueues a QStash task 'schedule_core_welcome' with delay_seconds=90 so the welcome fires after the cold start. Returns {restarted: true} on success.
dependencies: Azure Container Apps (restart_container_app), QStash (schedule_core_welcome task with delay), Postgres (CoreProfile)
inventory_notes: The 90-second delay accounts for container cold-start time before the welcome cron fires. Welcome is only scheduled if onboarding_status == 'pending'.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to view my Core mindfulness preferences so that I can see my current voice, duration, and additional context settings.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/core/profile/ requires JWT auth; 404 if no tenant. Uses get_or_create to ensure a CoreProfile always exists. Returns all profile fields: id, onboarding_status, preferred_voice (default 'Achernar'), preferred_duration_minutes (default 10, range 3-30), ambient_bed_enabled, daily_cron_enabled, preferred_time, additional_context, created_at, updated_at. Always 200.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to update my mindfulness preferences so that future meditations reflect my chosen voice, duration, and personal context.
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/core/profile/ requires JWT auth; 404 if no tenant. Partial update via CoreProfileSerializer: writable fields are preferred_voice, preferred_duration_minutes (validated 3-30 by model), ambient_bed_enabled, daily_cron_enabled, preferred_time, additional_context, user_feedback; read-only: id, status fields, audio fields, timestamps. Returns 400 with validation errors on invalid data, 200 with updated profile on success.
inventory_notes: preferred_duration_minutes is validated at the model layer with MinValueValidator(3)/MaxValueValidator(30). The daily_cron_enabled field is stored but the daily auto-compose cron is not yet wired to any scheduler — it is a profile preference only at this time.
error_detail: Real but low-impact mass-assignment hygiene gap, NOT a security defect. CoreProfileSerializer (apps/core/serializers.py:8-23) lists onboarding_status in fields but omits it from read_only_fields (only id/created_at/updated_at are locked), so CoreProfileView.patch (apps/core/views.py:155-163) accepts and persists it from a JWT-authenticated request. This is a genuine spec deviation: the feature record's expected_behaviour says status fields should be read-only, the model docstring says CoreProfile is populated via assistant-led onboarding, and the runtime (internal-auth) endpoint is the intended writer (apps/core/runtime_views.py:94-109, _PROFILE_FIELDS). However the security framing is overstated. The write is entirely self-scoped (request.user.tenant only — no cross-tenant access, no privilege escalation, no data exposure), and DRF's choices validator limits values to the 4 valid enum members (pending/in_progress/completed/declined). The only behavioral consequence in core is CoreRestartView (views.py:133) skipping the user's OWN welcome-meditation scheduling when status != pending; views.py:85 merely echoes the value back. No core cron/scheduler is gated on this field (unlike apps/fuel). Worst case: a user perturbs their own onboarding/welcome state — a self-inflicted UX/consistency nuisance, no trust boundary crossed. Hence data-integrity/low, not security.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to tap the meditation orb so that an AI-personalized guided meditation is composed and rendered for me on demand.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/core/compose/ requires JWT auth; 404 if no tenant; 403 if tenant.core_enabled is false. Coalesces duplicate taps: if a session already exists with status PENDING or RENDERING, returns that session's id and status (200) without creating a new one. Otherwise creates a MeditationSession (status=PENDING, date=tenant_today(tenant)) and enqueues QStash task 'compose_meditation'. Returns {meditation_id, status} with 201 on creation. Enqueue failure is logged but non-fatal (the row is still created).
inventory_notes: The date is stamped in the tenant's LOCAL timezone, not UTC. Idempotency guard prevents charging a second render when the user mashes the orb.
error_detail: CoreComposeView.post (apps/core/views.py:217-243) does a non-atomic read-then-create: it queries for an in-flight MeditationSession (status PENDING/RENDERING) and, if none exists, calls MeditationSession.objects.create(...) then enqueues the compose_meditation task, with no transaction.atomic, no select_for_update, and no DB unique constraint. The model (apps/core/models.py:82-142) has only plain non-unique indexes on (tenant,date) and (tenant,status). Two requests landing in the SELECT-to-INSERT gap can both see no in-flight session, both create a PENDING row, and both enqueue a compose task. The downstream task guard (apps/core/tasks.py:42,69) only re-checks the single session it is handed, so it cannot dedup two distinct rows; each renders and each spends quota. The claimed defect is structurally accurate. However severity is LOW, not medium: the frontend (frontend/app/core/page.tsx:147-180) already guards effectively the orb is inert while phase is loading or composing, and compose() flips React state to composing synchronously BEFORE the network await, absorbing the ordinary single-user double-tap client-side. The remaining race requires genuinely concurrent requests (multiple tabs/devices, a proxy/QStash/client retry, or direct API calls) hitting the narrow window. Impact is a duplicated billable render (cost/quota), not data corruption or broken UX; both rows remain valid and pollable.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to view my past meditations so that I can replay previously generated sessions.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/core/sessions/ requires JWT auth. Returns tenant-scoped MeditationSession list. Defaults to status=ready if no 'status' query param is provided; accepts any MeditationStatus value as a filter. Returns all serializer fields: id, date, status, title, theme, voice, model, guidance_text, audio_url, ogg_url, duration_ms, ambient_bed, user_feedback, created_at, updated_at. Ordered by -date, -created_at. Returns empty list if no tenant.
inventory_notes: The ?status= filter accepts any raw MeditationStatus value (pending, rendering, ready, delivered, failed). Without a filter only status=ready sessions are returned.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to poll or view a single meditation session so that I can track render progress and access the audio URL when ready.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/core/sessions/<uuid:id>/ requires JWT auth. Scoped to the authenticated tenant; returns 404 if the session does not exist or belongs to another tenant. Returns full MeditationSessionSerializer payload including audio_url and ogg_url once status=ready.
inventory_notes: Frontend polls this endpoint to detect when a compose-on-demand session transitions from pending/rendering to ready.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to stream my rendered meditation audio so that the player can scrub, measure duration, and stop cleanly at the end.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/meditations/<uuid:tenant_id>/<str:filename> — no authentication; security is the unguessable UUID filename. Only GET is accepted (400 otherwise). Filename must match ^[\w-]+\.(mp3|ogg)$; returns 404 otherwise (path traversal guard). Downloads the file from the Azure File Share at workspace/meditations/<filename> under share ws-<tenant_id[:20]>. Responds with full HTTP Range support: a Range header yields a 206 partial response with Content-Range; a malformed Range falls back to full 200; a Range starting beyond EOF returns 416. All responses include Accept-Ranges: bytes and Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600.
dependencies: Azure File Share (storage account + per-tenant share ws-<prefix>), Azure Storage Management API (to fetch account key)
inventory_notes: Range support is mandatory for iOS AVPlayer: without a 206 response the player never determines a finite duration and the playhead overruns the audio end. Returns 404 in mock mode (no Azure configured).

feature: QStash Task: Compose Meditation (LLM author → render)
user_story: As the system, I want to author a meditation manifest via LLM and then render it to audio so that a pending session transitions through to ready.
expected_behaviour: Triggered by QStash task 'compose_meditation' with meditation_id. Loads MeditationSession; skips if status != PENDING. Calls gather_meditation_signals (CoreProfile context, last meditation theme, constellation stars via apps/lessons, recent daily-note snippets via apps/journal) then compose.author_manifest (OpenRouter DeepSeek V4 Pro, JSON mode, 60s timeout). On ComposeError: marks session failed (terminal, no QStash retry). On success: saves manifest/title/theme to session, then calls render_meditation synchronously.
dependencies: OpenRouter (LLM manifest authoring via DeepSeek V4 Pro), Postgres (MeditationSession, CoreProfile, Document), apps/lessons (constellation context)
inventory_notes: Journal/constellation signals egress to OpenRouter which is configured for zero-data-retention. additional_context is capped at 800 chars. last_meditation_theme is passed so the composer varies themes across sessions.

feature: QStash Task: Render Meditation (TTS + ffmpeg audio pipeline)
user_story: As the system, I want to render a meditation manifest into MP3 and OGG audio files so that the subscriber can listen to their personalized session.
expected_behaviour: Triggered by QStash task 'render_meditation' with meditation_id. Loads session; skips if status not PENDING or FAILED. render_meditation in services.py: atomically claims the row (PENDING/FAILED → RENDERING, or stale RENDERING older than CORE_RENDER_STALE_MINUTES=15 → RENDERING); validates manifest via render.validate_manifest; if GEMINI_API_KEY absent marks failed (terminal). Calls render.render_manifest_to_audio: concurrent TTS via Gemini (up to CORE_RENDER_CONCURRENCY=4 workers), per-segment retry (3 transient + 6 rate-limit retries with retryDelay backoff), deadline abort at CORE_RENDER_DEADLINE_SECONDS=240s. Failed segments fall back to 1.2s silence. If >50% of segments were rate-limited, marks failed. On success: uploads mp3 and ogg to Azure File Share at workspace/meditations/<session_id>.{mp3,ogg}, sets audio_url/ogg_url/duration_ms/guidance_text/model/voice, sets status=ready. Recovers from idle DB connection timeout with one reconnect+retry. Non-fatally calls notify_meditation_ready. Terminal failures do not re-raise (no retry storm); transient failures re-raise for QStash retry.
dependencies: Gemini TTS API (gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts), ffmpeg (silence generation, concat, loudnorm, transcode), Azure File Share (binary upload), QStash (retry on re-raise), Postgres (MeditationSession)
inventory_notes: TTS emits PCM 24kHz/16-bit/mono; ffmpeg stitches silence via anullsrc (never TTS-silence). Loudnorm target is I=-16:TP=-1.5:LRA=11. The stale-render claim guard (15-min window) prevents wedged RENDERING rows from being un-retryable.

user_story: As the system, I want to validate a meditation manifest before any TTS spend so that malformed or overlong manifests are rejected cheaply.
expected_behaviour: render.validate_manifest(manifest) is called both by RuntimeMeditationCreateView (before persisting) and by render_meditation (before TTS spend). Checks: manifest is a dict; phases is a non-empty list of exactly the 6 canonical phases in order (arrival, breath_anchor, body_scan, core_practice, integration, closing); every segment is a dict with type 'speech' or 'silence'; speech text non-empty and ≤600 chars; silence seconds in [3,150] or literal 'flex'; total speech segment count in [4,12]; closing phase ends on a non-empty speech segment; each phase has a positive target_seconds; estimated total duration ≤ min(1800, target*1.3) seconds. Returns a list of error strings (empty = valid).
inventory_notes: Deliberately does NOT enforce one-speech-per-phase or one-flex-per-phase — the composer may use pure-silence phases. The 1.3× duration tolerance guards against runaway manifests with unbounded explicit silences.

user_story: As the system, I want to notify the subscriber on their linked channel when their meditation is ready so that they know to open the app and listen.
expected_behaviour: notify_meditation_ready is called at the end of a successful render_meditation. Guards: tenant must be ACTIVE, must have a linked user. Resolves the channel via router.cron_delivery.resolve_user_channel. For 'telegram': sends a text message with title + frontend URL via send_telegram_message. For 'line': pushes via LINE Messaging API push endpoint (Bearer token, 10s timeout, text capped at 4900 chars); on 429 trips the monthly quota gate. For 'app': marks delivered=True without sending (APNs push + ?since= feed handle iOS delivery). PII placeholders in the title are rehydrated via pii.redactor.rehydrate_text before sending. On any delivery, records a ProactiveOutbound row for thread continuity. Non-fatal: any exception is logged and swallowed, never propagated to the render.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API, LINE Messaging API, Postgres (ProactiveOutbound), apps/router/cron_delivery (channel resolution), apps/router/proactive_context (record_proactive_outbound), apps/pii/redactor
inventory_notes: The 'app' channel path records a ProactiveOutbound row for the ?since= feed but does not send a Telegram/LINE message. LINE outbound message IDs are recorded for quote-reply attribution.

user_story: As the OpenClaw container, I want to read the tenant's Core summary so that the AI assistant knows how many meditations the user has done and their latest session.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/core/runtime/<uuid:tenant_id>/summary/ — AllowAny permission; authenticated by X-NBHD-Internal-Key header + X-NBHD-Tenant-Id header matching the URL tenant_id (InternalAuthError → 401). Sets service-role RLS context. Returns {total_sessions (count of READY sessions), last: {id, title, date} or null}. Returns 401 on auth failure, 404 if tenant not found.

user_story: As the OpenClaw container, I want to read and update the tenant's mindfulness profile so that the assistant can reflect onboarding progress and user preferences.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/core/runtime/<uuid:tenant_id>/profile/ and PATCH same path — AllowAny permission; authenticated by X-NBHD-Internal-Key + X-NBHD-Tenant-Id headers (401 on failure). Uses get_or_create so the profile always exists. GET returns all _PROFILE_FIELDS: onboarding_status, preferred_voice, preferred_duration_minutes, ambient_bed_enabled, daily_cron_enabled, preferred_time, additional_context. PATCH accepts any subset of those fields, saves only changed ones (update_fields=[...changed, 'updated_at']), returns the updated profile dict. 404 if tenant not found.
inventory_notes: No DRF serializer validation on the PATCH path — fields are set raw via setattr. The OpenClaw plugin is expected to set onboarding_status to 'completed' after the assistant-led onboarding flow.

user_story: As the OpenClaw container, I want to submit an authored meditation manifest so that the backend validates it, persists a session, and enqueues the audio render.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/core/runtime/<uuid:tenant_id>/meditation/ — AllowAny permission; authenticated by X-NBHD-Internal-Key + X-NBHD-Tenant-Id (401 on failure). Body must contain 'manifest' as a JSON object (400 otherwise). manifest is validated via render.validate_manifest; on errors returns 400 {error: 'invalid_manifest', detail: [errors]}. On success: creates MeditationSession (status=PENDING, date=tenant_today, title/theme/voice extracted from manifest[:160], manifest stored). Enqueues QStash task 'render_meditation'. Returns {meditation_id, status=pending} with 201.
inventory_notes: The OpenClaw plugin uses this path when the user requests a meditation inside the chat/Telegram/LINE channel. The web orb uses the separate POST /core/compose/ path which also does LLM authoring server-side.

user_story: As the OpenClaw container, I want to poll a meditation session's status so that I can tell the user when their audio is ready and provide the URL.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/core/runtime/<uuid:tenant_id>/meditation/<uuid:meditation_id>/ — AllowAny; authenticated by X-NBHD-Internal-Key + X-NBHD-Tenant-Id (401 on failure). Loads MeditationSession scoped to (id, tenant_id); returns 404 if not found. Returns {id, status, title, audio_url, duration_ms}.
inventory_notes: Subset of fields vs. the consumer endpoint — audio_url and duration_ms are what the assistant needs to deliver the session link and LINE audio duration.

feature: QStash Task: Schedule Core Welcome (post-restart cron)
user_story: As the system, I want to schedule a one-shot welcome cron ~90 seconds after a container restart so that the assistant sends the subscriber a warm welcome to Core when the container is back up.
expected_behaviour: QStash task 'schedule_core_welcome' receives tenant_id. Loads Tenant (logs warning + returns if not found). Calls _schedule_core_welcome(tenant) which delegates to apps.orchestrator.welcome_scheduler.schedule_welcome with feature='core', cron_name='_core:welcome', and a prompt template instructing the assistant to call nbhd_send_to_user then POST /api/v1/tenants/runtime/<tenant_id>/welcomes/core/ to mark delivery. On any exception logs warning and returns (fire-and-forget). The welcome is re-delivered each time Core is re-enabled.
dependencies: QStash (welcome cron scheduling), OpenClaw container (executes the cron prompt), Postgres (Tenant)
inventory_notes: The actual welcome message is authored and sent by OpenClaw inside the container, not by Django. If nbhd_send_to_user fails, the assistant is instructed NOT to POST the mark endpoint, so the next deploy's backfill will retry.

user_story: As the OpenClaw container, I want to mark the Core welcome as delivered so that the deploy-time backfill does not re-send it on subsequent deploys.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/tenants/runtime/<uuid:tenant_id>/welcomes/core/ — AllowAny; authenticated by X-NBHD-Internal-Key + X-NBHD-Tenant-Id (401 on failure). Feature string must be in the known set (includes 'core'); 400 with {error: 'unknown_feature', valid: [...]} otherwise. Sets Tenant.welcomes_sent['core'] = ISO-8601 timestamp. Idempotent: re-POSTing overwrites the timestamp. Returns {feature, sent_at}.
inventory_notes: This endpoint is feature-generic (used by fuel, finance, core, etc.) but is integral to the Core welcome flow. CoreSettingsView clears the 'core' key when Core is re-enabled so the welcome fires again.

user_story: As the system, I want to inject the subscriber's Core mindfulness state into their USER.md so that the AI assistant is aware of recent meditation sessions when composing responses.
expected_behaviour: Registered at order=41 under key 'core' via @register_section. Only rendered when tenant.core_enabled=True. Queries READY MeditationSession rows for the tenant. Emits up to 2 lines: last meditation (title, date, theme[:120]) and a 7-day session count. Returns empty string if no sessions. Output capped at 600 chars. Triggered automatically on MeditationSession model changes (create/save) via the refresh_on=(MeditationSession,) registration. Output section heading is '## Core — mindfulness state'.
inventory_notes: The 7-day count uses the tenant's local date (tenant_today) to match the locally-stamped session dates, avoiding UTC off-by-one on evening sessions.

feature: iOS Core AI On-Device Model Manifest
user_story: As a subscriber on iOS, I want my app to fetch the on-device AI model manifest so that it can download and verify the correct model bundle for local inference.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/coreai/model/manifest/ requires JWT auth (IsAuthenticated). Reads COREAI_MODEL_BASE_URL and COREAI_MODEL_MANIFEST_PATH from settings. If either is empty or the manifest file does not exist, returns 404 {detail: 'No on-device model is configured.'} so the iOS app falls back gracefully. Parses the JSON manifest file; for each file entry builds an absolute URL as {base_url}/{name}/{version}/{url-encoded-path} and adds sha256 + bytes. Returns {name, version, total_bytes, files: [{path, url, sha256, bytes}]}. If the manifest file is unreadable or malformed (missing keys), returns 503.
dependencies: Local filesystem (COREAI_MODEL_MANIFEST_PATH), Azure Blob/CDN (COREAI_MODEL_BASE_URL, for downloads — not called by this endpoint)
inventory_notes: 503 on corrupt manifest is intentional fail-closed behavior: the iOS client verifies each file's sha256, so a partial manifest would yield an unverifiable download. The manifest JSON is generated by the generate_coreai_manifest management command, not at runtime.

feature: Management Command: Generate Core AI Model Manifest
user_story: As an operator, I want to generate the on-device AI model manifest JSON from an exported LanguageBundle directory so that the iOS app can verify and download the model files.
expected_behaviour: python manage.py generate_coreai_manifest --bundle <dir> --name <name> --model-version <version> --out <path>. Walks the bundle directory recursively, computing streaming SHA-256 and byte size for every file (skipping .DS_Store). Writes a JSON manifest with {name, version, total_bytes, files: [{path, sha256, bytes}]} to the --out path. Raises CommandError if the bundle directory does not exist or contains no files. Prints a success message with file count, total GB, and the expected upload path pattern.
inventory_notes: Operator must separately upload the bundle files to COREAI_MODEL_BASE_URL/<name>/<version>/<path>. The --version flag is named --model-version because Django's BaseCommand reserves --version.

user_story: As an admin, I want to view and edit CoreProfile records so that I can diagnose onboarding issues and manually correct mindfulness preferences.
expected_behaviour: Django admin at /admin/core/coreprofile/. list_display: tenant, onboarding_status, preferred_voice, daily_cron_enabled, created_at. list_filter: onboarding_status, ambient_bed_enabled, daily_cron_enabled. search_fields: tenant__user__display_name. readonly_fields: id, created_at, updated_at. Standard admin create/update/delete.

user_story: As an admin, I want to view and inspect MeditationSession records so that I can diagnose render failures and audit generated content.
expected_behaviour: Django admin at /admin/core/meditationsession/. list_display: title, tenant, date, status, voice, duration_ms, created_at. list_filter: status. search_fields: title, theme, tenant__user__display_name. readonly_fields: id, created_at, updated_at. The error field is visible for failed sessions to expose the last render error message.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As the system, I want a Postgres source-of-truth for every tenant's cron schedules so that cron state survives container restarts and can be managed without hitting the OpenClaw gateway.
expected_behaviour: CronJob rows carry name, data (OC job dict), source (system/user/fuel_session/agent), managed flag, enabled flag, pattern, typed_payload, creation_path (legacy/typed/freeform/internal), and user_confirmed_at. Two DB CHECK constraints enforce that freeform rows must have user_confirmed_at set and typed rows must have pattern set. Unique constraint on (tenant, name). For creation_path=typed, data is derived by the pre_save signal from pattern+typed_payload; edits to data directly are overwritten. For legacy/freeform/internal rows, data is source of truth. Indexes on (tenant, last_synced_at), (tenant, source, managed), (tenant, pattern).
inventory_notes: Dual-mode operation controlled by Tenant.postgres_cron_canonical flag. When False, gateway is truth and this table is a read cache. When True, this table is truth and OpenClaw SQLite is derived. The managed=False flag is used for agent-created _sync:* crons and one-shot (kind:at) crons that OC auto-deletes.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to view my scheduled tasks in the dashboard so that I can see what reminders and proactive messages the assistant will send me.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/cron-jobs/ (tenant-scoped). Requires DRF IsAuthenticated. If tenant.postgres_cron_canonical=True, reads CronJob table directly (no gateway call). If tenant is hibernated, serves from Postgres cache. If gateway is reachable, calls cron.list on the OpenClaw container, upserts results into the CronJob cache, and updates Tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot. Filters out hidden system crons (Background Tasks, Heartbeat Check-in, Personal Question, Project Check-in) and names starting with _sync:/ _fuel: prefixes. Each visible job is tagged with foreground boolean based on presence of Phase 2 sync marker in payload.message. Returns {jobs: [...]} with 200. Returns 502 if gateway is unreachable and no cache exists.
inventory_notes: Hibernated tenants serve from cache without waking the container. The HIDDEN_SYSTEM_CRONS frozenset and HIDDEN_SYSTEM_CRON_PREFIXES tuple drive the filter — Evening Check-in is intentionally NOT hidden.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to create a new scheduled task so that my assistant will proactively contact me at a chosen time.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/cron-jobs/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Validates name is present. Enforces MAX_CRON_JOBS=10 visible job cap (409 CONFLICT if at limit). Enforces unique name check (409 CONFLICT on duplicate). If delivery.channel=telegram and delivery.to is missing, auto-populates to from tenant's telegram_chat_id. Pops foreground flag (Django-only, not sent to OpenClaw). Normalizes sessionTarget to isolated and wakeMode is removed. Appends Phase 2 sync block to payload.message if foreground=true. If postgres_cron_canonical: creates CronJob row (signal triggers debounced reconcile). Else: calls cron.add on the gateway. Returns 201 on success, 400 for missing name, 409 for cap/dup, 502 on gateway error.
inventory_notes: The foreground flag controls whether the Phase 2 sync block is appended to the cron prompt. Visible cap excludes hidden crons from the count. Legacy gateway path does a list call before add to enforce the cap.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to edit a scheduled task's schedule or delivery settings so that I can adjust when and how I receive my messages.
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/cron-jobs/<job_name>/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Returns 403 if job_name is a hidden system cron. Allowed patchable fields without delete+recreate: schedule, delivery, enabled. Payload/message changes (or explicit foreground toggle) require delete+recreate on the gateway path. If postgres_cron_canonical: patches CronJob.data directly (signal fires). Auto-populates telegram delivery.to if missing. On foreground toggle: strips existing Phase 2 block, re-wraps. If job not found in container (vanished after restart), falls back to Tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot then creates fresh. Rollback attempted if recreate fails. Returns 200 on success, 403 for system cron, 404 if job not found (pg path), 502 on gateway error.
inventory_notes: Legacy gateway path uses delete+recreate for any non-patchable field. Backup job is taken before delete to allow rollback. Snapshot fallback covers container-restart wipe scenarios.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to delete a scheduled task so that I stop receiving unwanted automated messages.
expected_behaviour: DELETE /api/v1/cron-jobs/<job_name>/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Returns 403 for hidden system crons. If postgres_cron_canonical: deletes CronJob row (signal triggers debounced reconcile to remove from OpenClaw SQLite). Legacy path: calls cron.remove on gateway directly. Returns 204 on success, 403 for system cron, 404 if not found (pg path), 502 on gateway error.
inventory_notes: System crons cannot be deleted by subscribers in either path.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to toggle a scheduled task on or off without deleting it so that I can temporarily pause reminders.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/cron-jobs/<job_name>/toggle/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Returns 403 for hidden system crons. Requires enabled boolean in request body (400 if missing). If postgres_cron_canonical: updates CronJob.enabled column only (save update_fields=[enabled, updated_at]), signal fires. Legacy: calls cron.update with patch={enabled: bool} on gateway. Returns 200 with updated job dict, 400 for missing field, 403 for system cron, 502 on gateway error.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to delete multiple scheduled tasks at once so that I can quickly clean up my task list.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/cron-jobs/bulk-delete/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Accepts {ids: [name-or-id, ...]}. Returns 400 if ids is not a non-empty list. Returns 403 if any id is a hidden system cron. Deduplicates ids preserving order. If postgres_cron_canonical: deletes rows in atomic transaction, signal fires per-row. Legacy: if tenant is hibernated, wakes container and returns 503 with container_waking=true. Iterates gateway cron.remove calls; if mid-loop unavailability detected, aborts and returns 503. Returns 200 on full success, 207 on partial success, 502 if all fail.
inventory_notes: Hibernation-aware: detects Azure Container App unavailability mid-loop and aborts rather than surfacing raw Azure HTML. Wake + retry pattern.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

feature: Cron job bulk foreground/background toggle (subscriber dashboard)
user_story: As a subscriber, I want to switch multiple cron jobs between foreground and background mode so that I can control whether they sync turn results into my main conversation.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/cron-jobs/bulk-update-foreground/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Accepts {ids: [name-or-id, ...], foreground: bool}. Returns 400 if ids invalid or foreground not a boolean. Returns 403 if any id is a hidden system cron. If postgres_cron_canonical: strips existing Phase 2 block, re-wraps per foreground flag, saves each row, signal fires. Legacy path: fetches all jobs, rebuilds each job with delete+recreate, rolls back on failure. Skips jobs already in the desired state. Returns 200, 207 (partial), or 502.
inventory_notes: Foreground mode appends the Phase 2 sync block to payload.message (idempotent). Background strips it. Snapshot fallback used when job not found in live gateway list.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see upcoming one-off reminders that my assistant scheduled for me so that I know what's coming.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/cron-jobs/pending-at/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Always reads from gateway (no Postgres path). If tenant is hibernated, serves from Postgres cache (stale=true). Calls cron.list, filters to kind:at jobs with enabled=true. Returns {jobs: [{jobId, name, firesAtMs, schedule, payload, delivery}], soft_cap: 20, stale: bool}. Jobs sorted ascending by firesAtMs. Disabled at-jobs are excluded. Returns 200. Falls back to cache with stale=true on gateway unavailability.
inventory_notes: Kept separate from recurring task list so a gateway hiccup degrades pending reminders without blanking the entire Scheduled Tasks card. AT_CRON_SOFT_CAP=20 is server-published for frontend rendering.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to cancel a pending one-off reminder so that I don't receive a message I no longer need.
expected_behaviour: DELETE /api/v1/cron-jobs/pending-at/<name>/ (tenant-scoped). Requires IsAuthenticated. Requires active tenant container (502 if not). Fetches cron.list to verify target exists and is a kind:at cron before deleting (prevents deleting recurring tasks via this path). Returns 404 if not found. Calls cron.remove by jobId. Idempotent: a not-found gateway response returns 204. Returns 204 on success, 404 if not found, 502 on gateway error.
inventory_notes: Only deletes at-kind crons, protecting recurring tasks from accidental deletion via this endpoint.

user_story: As QStash, I want to trigger registered platform tasks so that scheduled and on-demand platform work is executed without Celery workers.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/cron/trigger/<task_name>/. Verifies Upstash-Signature via QSTASH_CURRENT_SIGNING_KEY; returns 401 if invalid. Sets RLS service role context. Looks up task_name in TASK_MAP (40+ registered tasks); returns 404 if unknown. Parses JSON body for {args: [...], kwargs: {...}}; coerces malformed shapes to empty defaults. Validates args against task function signature — returns 400 (not 500) on bad args so QStash parks in DLQ immediately instead of retrying. Executes task synchronously. Returns {status, task_name, execution_id, result} with 200, or {status: error, error: ...} with 400/500.
inventory_notes: 400 response on bad args is intentional to park in QStash DLQ instead of burning 3 retries. The TASK_MAP covers tenant maintenance, billing, journal sync, provisioning, media, lessons, hibernation, OpenClaw fleet rollout, and more (~40 tasks). Dev fallback: if QSTASH_TOKEN unset, publish_task executes synchronously.

user_story: As an operator, I want to trigger tasks without QStash signature verification in local development so that I can test task execution directly.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/cron/trigger-debug/<task_name>/. Returns 403 if DEBUG=False. Same task lookup and execution logic as trigger_task but skips signature verification. Same arg validation (400 on bad args). Returns {status, task_name, execution_id, result} with 200, or error response with 400/500 (includes traceback in 500 response unlike the production endpoint).
inventory_notes: Only available when DEBUG=True. 500 responses include full traceback (unlike production endpoint).
