On success: marks `delivered=True`, `delivery_status=DELIVERED`, clears lease. On failure: increments `delivery_attempts`, clears lease, breaks (preserves queue order). At `_MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3`: marks `delivered=True/FAILED`, calls `_send_apology_for_dropped_message` (LINE only), increments `dropped`. Raises RuntimeError at end if any failed (signals QStash to retry).
dependencies: OpenClaw container (/v1/chat/completions), LINE Messaging API (relay), Supabase/Postgres (BufferedMessage), QStash
inventory_notes: BYO Claude and reasoning models get REASONING_MODEL_TIMEOUT via `_resolve_chat_timeout`. Inner `_post_chat_completion_with_backoff` retries 5xx/connection errors with (5, 15, 45)s backoffs before counting as a delivery attempt. Telegram apology not implemented — only LINE gets the dropped-message apology.
error_detail: Confirmed: a Telegram user whose hibernation-buffered message is permanently dropped after 3 failed delivery attempts receives no notification, while a LINE user in the identical scenario does. When a BufferedMessage reaches _MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3 (hibernation.py:1324), it is marked delivered=True/FAILED and _send_apology_for_dropped_message is called (hibernation.py:1343). Inside that function the LINE branch sends a localized apology via _send_line_text (lines 966-978), but the else/Telegram branch only emits logger.info and sends nothing to the user (lines 979-986). The user's message silently vanishes. Impact is real but narrow: it triggers only when a buffered message fails delivery 3 consecutive times (e.g. the container keeps erroring on /telegram-webhook after wake), and the user can still re-send. Both the code comment and the feature record document the Telegram apology as deliberately deferred, but the stated rationale (forward_to_openclaw has its own retry envelope, so head-of-line stalls are less acute) explains the retry behavior, not why the user is left uninformed when a message is genuinely dropped. The cross-channel inconsistency (same failure, different UX for no user-facing reason) is what makes it a defect rather than intended design. All scaffolding already exists: the error_msg framework with dropped_message / dropped_message_with_excerpt keys (English plus other languages), excerpt extraction, and strip_internal_framing are in place; only the Telegram send call is missing.

user_story: As a system, I want to restore user cron jobs from the pre-image snapshot after a container restart so that user-created reminders are not permanently lost when an image update wipes SQLite.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `restore_crons_after_image_update` — published 90s after image update with idempotency key `post-image-restore-{tenant_id}-{tag}`. Guards: if no tenant or no container_id → return. If no snapshot → falls back to `seed_cron_jobs`. Reads existing jobs via `cron.list`. System crons are excluded from restoration (those are reconciled via Postgres CronJob rows). Deduplicates snapshot by name (dirty snapshots may have duplicates). For each non-system user job not already present: strips gateway-internal fields (id, jobId, createdAt, state, createdAtMs, updatedAtMs, nextRunAtMs, runningAtMs) and calls `cron.add`. After restore: if `postgres_cron_canonical`, enqueues `regenerate_tenant_crons` with 5s delay and idempotency key. Calls `_fire_missed_crons_after_restore` to trigger any cron-kind jobs that should have fired during downtime. If tenant has `fuel_profile.use_session_scheduling`, calls `regenerate_fuel_crons`.
dependencies: OpenClaw gateway (cron.list, cron.add, cron.run), QStash, Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: Missed-fire catch-up is needed because the restored jobs have no `lastRunAtMs` (gateway-internal fields are stripped), so OC 5.7's `planStartupCatchup` (gated on `lastRunAtMs`) silently drops missed fires. Cap: _MAX_MISSED_FIRES_PER_RESTORE=5 per restore. _sync: and _fuel: prefixes are excluded from snapshot restoration.

user_story: As a system, I want to re-enable all suspended cron jobs 60 seconds after a container wakes so they can fire again after hibernation.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `resume_hibernated_crons` — published 60s after wake. Calls `resume_tenant_crons(tenant)` from `apps.cron.suspension` which calls `cron.update` for each disabled job (enabled=False → enabled=True). Idempotent: already-enabled crons are counted as `already_enabled`. Returns {enabled, already_enabled, errors}. Raises on failure so QStash retries. Same logic used by `resume_tenant_crons_task` (via Stripe webhook path).
inventory_notes: `resume_tenant_crons_task` is enqueued with a 30s delay by `handle_checkout_completed` (Stripe reactivation) to give the freshly-woken container time to boot.

user_story: As a system, I want to seed the default cron jobs (morning briefing, evening check-in, etc.) into a new tenant's OpenClaw container so they get proactive AI check-ins from day one.
expected_behaviour: Called from `seed_cron_jobs_task` (QStash) and management command `seed_cron_jobs`. Builds job list via `build_cron_seed_jobs(tenant)`. Postgres-canonical path (when `tenant.postgres_cron_canonical=True`): creates `CronJob` rows with `source=SYSTEM, managed=True` — skips names already present (case-insensitive); signal triggers `regenerate_tenant_crons`. Legacy path: calls `cron.list` with 3 retries on 502/503/504; if response unparseable, refuses to seed and returns `skipped=True`. Diffs by name (case-insensitive), calls `cron.add` for missing jobs (3 retries on transient errors). After creation: dedup pass via `dedup_tenant_cron_jobs`. Then `restore_user_cron_jobs` from `cron_jobs_snapshot` for any user jobs not in the post-seed set; another dedup pass after restore.
dependencies: OpenClaw gateway (cron.list, cron.add, cron.remove), Supabase/Postgres (CronJob model)
inventory_notes: Management command requires tenant status=ACTIVE. Unparseable cron.list responses are treated as 'refuse to seed' to avoid duplicates. SYSTEM_JOB_NAMES and _SYSTEM_GENERATED_PREFIXES exclude _sync:/_fuel: from user-job restore path.

feature: System Cron Rows Sync from Seed (refresh_system_cron_rows_from_seed)
user_story: As a system, I want to keep the Postgres CronJob rows aligned with the current seed templates so that prompt or schedule changes propagate to the fleet via the signal-driven reconciler.
expected_behaviour: Called from `update_tenant_config`, the `update_cron_prompts` management command, and post-image-swap. Builds `build_cron_seed_jobs(tenant)`. For each seed job: if no matching `CronJob` row → creates one. If row exists: checks if stored message body (after `strip_date_line`) starts with any of `_KNOWN_DEFAULT_PREFIXES` — if default, overwrites entire data from seed. If custom (user-pasted message), merges: updates all non-message fields (model, kind, schedule, delivery, sessionTarget) from seed, preserves stored message. If `row.data == new_data` → skips (unchanged count). On change: saves row, triggering post_save signal → debounced `regenerate_tenant_crons`. Reaper pass: deletes managed system CronJob rows whose names are NOT in the current seed (e.g., deprecated 'Heartbeat Check-in' for tenants using built-in), excluding _sync:/_fuel: prefixes.
inventory_notes: _KNOWN_DEFAULT_PREFIXES must be extended whenever a seed prompt's opening sentence changes — old form must remain for one release to avoid misclassifying existing rows as user-customized.

user_story: As a system, I want to reconcile the Postgres CronJob rows with the tenant's OpenClaw gateway so that managed crons stay in sync even after container restarts that wipe SQLite.
expected_behaviour: Called from `regenerate_tenant_crons_task` (QStash, debounced 30s via signal) and `reconcile_tenant_crons_task` (hourly fleet-wide). No-op if `tenant.postgres_cron_canonical=False`. Calls `cron.list` against gateway, extracts jobs. Skips unmanaged crons: `_sync:` prefix, `kind='at'` schedule. Diff by name: for managed CronJob rows not in gateway → `cron.add`. For gateway jobs not in managed rows and not unmanaged → `cron.remove`. Janitor pass: reaps `kind='at'` jobs whose fire time is > 1h in the past (gateway auto-delete may have failed). Enforces `_at` cron caps: >50 → PlatformIssueLog MEDIUM; >200 → reaps newest-first back to 200 + PlatformIssueLog HIGH.
dependencies: OpenClaw gateway (cron.list, cron.add, cron.remove), Supabase/Postgres (CronJob model, PlatformIssueLog)
inventory_notes: Hourly fleet task `reconcile_tenant_crons_task` filters by `postgres_cron_canonical=True, status='active'`. `_sync:` crons are agent-managed and self-cleaning — reconciler must not own them. `kind='at'` jobs auto-delete after firing via `deleteAfterRun=true`; reconciler skips them to avoid racing the auto-delete.
error_detail: Confirmed. regenerate_tenant_crons treats any gateway cron that is neither _sync:-prefixed nor schedule-kind 'at' as managed. Fuel session crons are _fuel:{8-hex} with schedule kind 'cron' (fuel_cron.py:84,88), so they pass both guards (_UNMANAGED_PREFIXES at line 44 contains only _sync:; _UNMANAGED_SCHEDULE_KINDS at line 50 contains only 'at') and are classified managed at line 319. These jobs have NO managed=True CronJob row: regenerate_fuel_crons writes them straight to the gateway via cron.add (fuel_cron.py:159-161) and never creates rows, and build_cron_seed_jobs suppresses fuel emission when use_session_scheduling=True (config_generator.py:1638). So desired_by_name (line 247, from CronJob.objects.filter(managed=True)) lacks them; they land in current_managed but not desired_by_name, fall into to_remove (line 322), and cron.remove deletes them (line 381). Both reconcilers co-occur on the same tenant: postgres_cron_canonical=True for all tenants (migrations 0058/0067); regenerate_tenant_crons fires hourly (tasks.py:1095), on every CronJob save/delete signal, and on container-start, independently of the fuel signal that runs regenerate_fuel_crons. Result: a destructive flapping race where the tenant reconciler silently wipes the per-workout prep crons the fuel reconciler just added, so users miss scheduled workout notifications. The FUEL_SESSION classification in _classify_source (postgres_canonical.py:71-75) only runs on dashboard-create/backfill, not the live regen path, so it does not protect these crons.

user_story: As an operator, I want to remove duplicate cron jobs from a tenant's container so that race-condition duplicates from parallel seed attempts don't cause a cron to fire twice.
expected_behaviour: `dedup_tenant_cron_jobs(tenant, dry_run, jobs)`. Calls `cron.list` (or accepts pre-fetched list). Groups by name; for each name with >1 job: sorts by `createdAtMs` descending (most recent first) and keeps the first, removes the rest via `cron.remove`. Returns {kept, deleted, errors, duplicates}. In dry_run mode returns duplicates list without calling `cron.remove`. Management command `dedup_cron_jobs` wraps this with `--apply` / `--tenant` flags (default is dry-run).
inventory_notes: Sorted by `createdAtMs` (epoch ms from gateway), NOT by `createdAt` string or `id` lex order. Also called as a post-creation dedup pass inside `seed_cron_jobs` and after user-job restore.

user_story: As a system, I want to update system cron prompts that still have default content and fix timezone drift so that prompt template changes propagate to the fleet without overwriting user customizations.
expected_behaviour: Called from various update paths (cron refresh, config bump, management commands). Calls `cron.list`, builds `existing_by_name`. For each desired system job: checks `_is_default_prompt(existing_message)` — compares stripped message body to `_KNOWN_DEFAULT_PREFIXES`. If default: patches payload. If custom: skips message but still patches schedule if tz drifted. Non-message payload drift (model, kind) detected via `_payload_non_message_drift` — triggers delete+create (not cron.update) for `_SYSTEM_JOB_NAMES`. Tz drift uses `cron.update({schedule})`. Missing system jobs are seeded via `cron.add`. `sync_heartbeat_cron` is called to add/remove/update the Heartbeat Check-in cron based on `tenant.heartbeat_enabled`. Pushes USER.md at the end. Returns {updated, skipped, errors}.
dependencies: OpenClaw gateway (cron.list, cron.add, cron.remove, cron.update), Azure Storage (File Share for USER.md), Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: Delete+create used for payload changes (instead of cron.update) because it's the proven migration path for legacy systemEvent→agentTurn shape changes. Container-unavailable GatewayError propagates so deferral helper can see it.

user_story: As a system, I want to ensure the Heartbeat Check-in cron exists when enabled and is absent when disabled so that the heartbeat feature toggles take effect on the container.
expected_behaviour: `sync_heartbeat_cron(tenant, existing_by_name)` — called from `update_system_cron_prompts` (with pre-fetched map) and standalone. If `existing_by_name` not provided: fetches via `cron.list`. Cases: desired (enabled=True) + not existing → `cron.add`; not desired + existing → `cron.remove`; both exist + schedule drift → `cron.update` with new schedule. Returns 'added' / 'removed' / 'updated' / 'ok' / 'error'. Container-unavailable GatewayError propagates.
inventory_notes: Called as the last step of `update_system_cron_prompts`. Standalone call used by `remove_zombie_heartbeats_task` logic path.

user_story: As a system, I want to remove orphaned Heartbeat Check-in cron jobs from containers where heartbeat is disabled so that stale crons don't fire after the feature is toggled off.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `remove_zombie_heartbeats`. Queries ACTIVE tenants with `container_id` and `heartbeat_enabled=False`. For each: calls `cron.list`; finds job named 'Heartbeat Check-in'. If found: calls `cron.remove`. Sleeps 1s between tenants to rate-limit gateway calls. Returns {removed, skipped, errors}. Management command `remove_zombie_heartbeats` exposes this.
inventory_notes: Also called by `refresh_system_cron_rows_from_seed` reaper pass indirectly. 1s sleep between tenants to avoid gateway flood.

user_story: As a system, I want to keep a tenant's _fuel:* workout-session crons in sync with Postgres workout data so that scheduled workout reminders are accurate after data changes.
expected_behaviour: `regenerate_fuel_crons_task(tenant_id)` — enqueued (debounced 30s) by fuel signals on Workout writes, also runnable on demand. Skips if `hibernated_at` is set or status=SUSPENDED (logs skip). Calls `regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant)` from `apps/orchestrator/fuel_cron.py`. Returns {added, removed, unchanged, errors}. `reconcile_fuel_crons_task()` — hourly fleet-wide: queries all `FuelProfile` with `use_session_scheduling=True`, calls `regenerate_fuel_crons` for each active tenant. Returns aggregate totals.
dependencies: OpenClaw gateway (cron.list, cron.add, cron.remove), Supabase/Postgres (FuelProfile, Workout)
inventory_notes: Hibernated/suspended tenants are skipped to prevent GatewayError noise from HealthKit background syncs. Post-image cron restore also calls `regenerate_fuel_crons` for session-scheduling tenants.
error_detail: Confirmed at code level. The hourly fleet-wide reconcile_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1042-1045) only skips tenants lacking container_fqdn. Hibernated containers retain their container_fqdn (hibernation sets hibernated_at but never clears the FQDN), so the sweep proceeds and calls regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant) directly (line 1047), which itself has no hibernation/suspension guard (fuel_cron.py:107-131). Its first action is invoke_gateway_tool(tenant, cron.list, ...), which POSTs to a scaled-to-zero container, raising requests.RequestException then GatewayError, caught at fuel_cron.py:138 with logger.exception (a full stack trace) plus errors++. For any FuelProfile with use_session_scheduling=True whose tenant is hibernated/suspended but still has a FQDN, this emits a stack-trace log and inflated error telemetry every hour. The sibling per-tenant regenerate_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1024-1026) deliberately guards exactly this case with a comment about avoiding recurring GatewayError noise for idle tenants and notes reconcile-on-wake catches up; the fleet path is missing that identical guard, an intentional-looking inconsistency. Impact is bounded: log/observability noise only, no user-facing breakage and no data loss (reconcile-on-wake rebuilds the crons), and it requires the non-default cutover flag use_session_scheduling=True (default False) plus a hibernated/suspended tenant that still has an FQDN. Hence real but low severity.

user_story: As a system, I want to ensure every kind='at' (one-shot reminder) cron has a corresponding QStash wake task so that reminders created mid-conversation are not silently dropped if the container goes down.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `ensure_at_cron_wakes` — runs every 5 minutes. For each active, non-hibernated tenant: calls `cron.list`. For each `kind='at'` enabled job: calls `_at_fires_at_ms(job)` to get fire time in ms. If `now_ms < fires_at_ms <= now_ms + 7200000 (2h)`: computes `delay = max(60, (fires_at_ms - now_ms)/1000 - 240)`. Publishes QStash `wake_for_cron` task with idempotency key `wake-cron-{tenant.id}-{fires_at_ms}` — deduplicates against wakes from the hibernation path. Returns {tenants, scheduled, errors, skipped}.
inventory_notes: _AT_WAKE_LOOKAHEAD_SECONDS = 7200 (2h). Only covers awake tenants — hibernated tenants' at-crons are covered by the hibernation snapshot path. Sub-5-minute reminders are expected to fire while the container is still awake from the conversation.

user_story: As a system, I want to keep USER.md on the Azure File Share up to date with the tenant's current goals, tasks, fuel, finance, and local time so that every OpenClaw turn has fresh structured context.
expected_behaviour: `push_user_md(tenant, force, debounce_seconds)` — renders the platform-managed region via `all_sections` from `envelope_registry` (per-app envelope.py modules), wraps in HTML-comment sentinels (BEGIN_MARKER / END_MARKER). Three-case merge with existing file: (1) empty/OC default boilerplate → write managed block alone; (2) has markers → replace only managed region; (3) agent-written content, no markers → prepend managed region, keep agent content. Writes to `workspace/USER.md` on Azure File Share. Leading-edge debounce (60s default) via Django cache key prevents signal storms from writing the same file repeatedly. `force=True` bypasses debounce. Hourly fleet task `refresh_user_md_fleet_task` calls `push_user_md(force=True, debounce_seconds=0)` for all active tenants.
dependencies: Azure Storage (File Share), Django cache (debounce), Supabase/Postgres (tenant state read), envelope_registry (per-app sections)
inventory_notes: _OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_USER_MD sentinel: OC's default placeholder text is treated as empty. The `_render_current_time_line` always includes the tenant's local time in their timezone. Bounded staleness is ≤1h from hourly fleet push.

feature: Fleet Tenant Health Check (check_health management command)
user_story: As an operator, I want a single command to check the health of all active tenant containers and the cache so that I can quickly identify which containers are down after a deploy.
expected_behaviour: Management command `check_health`. Calls `cache_ping()` to verify Upstash Redis connectivity. Calls `check_all_tenants_health()` — for each active tenant: calls `check_tenant_health(tenant_id)`. Per-tenant health: checks `gateway` by GETting `https://{fqdn}/health` with 10s timeout, captures response_time_ms. Returns `healthy = all checks ok`. Also reports `config_drift` (informational only: `pending_config_version > config_version`). With `--json` outputs structured JSON including cache result.
inventory_notes: config_drift does NOT affect healthy status — drift is expected after deploys and resolves on next idle cycle.

user_story: As an operator, I want to walk through every link in the cron→Gateway→journal chain for a specific tenant so that I can pinpoint exactly which step is broken.
expected_behaviour: Management command `check_gateway_health [--tenant-id UUID]`. Runs 7 sequential checks, stopping at first failure: (1) tenant exists and ACTIVE; (2) `socket.getaddrinfo` DNS resolution of container FQDN; (3) Key Vault secret `tenant-<uuid>-internal-key` readable via `read_key_vault_secret`; (4) GET `https://{fqdn}/health` → 200; (5) `invoke_gateway_tool(tenant, 'cron.list', {})` via `GatewayError`-catching wrapper; (6) Azure File Share read of `cron/jobs.json` → at least 1 job; (7) POST to `https://{fqdn}/api/v1/integrations/runtime/{id}/daily-note/append/` with internal key → 200/201. Prints PASS/FAIL per check with details.
dependencies: Azure Key Vault, Azure Storage (File Share), OpenClaw gateway (/health, tools/invoke, /api/v1/integrations/runtime/…), Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: Defaults to first active tenant if `--tenant-id` not provided. Check 7 tests the full gateway→Django round-trip. Check 6 reads the file share directly (not via gateway).
error_detail: Real but cosmetic diagnostic bug in an operator-only management command. invoke_gateway_tool returns data.get('result', {}) (gateway_client.py:231). For cron.list, the codebase documents and overwhelmingly uses the nested shape {'details': {'jobs': [...]}} -- canonical comments at services.py:880 ('Gateway wraps in {details:{jobs:[...]}} or {jobs:[...]}') and services.py:1387, plus the dominant {'details':{'jobs':[...]}} test fixtures across orchestrator/fuel/cron tests. The robust consumers (_extract_cron_jobs at services.py:870-889, cron_exists at gateway_client.py:295) explicitly unwrap details. Check 5 at check_gateway_health.py:133 instead does result.get('jobs', []) directly on the raw result, returning [] for the nested shape, so it always prints 'returned 0 jobs' even when the container has live crons. Impact is limited: (1) the check still returns PASS correctly regardless of count; (2) the count is purely a diagnostic stdout line; (3) the 'masking the seeding step' worry is overstated because Check 6 _check_cron_jobs_seeded (lines 178-185) independently reads cron/jobs.json from the file share and prints the authoritative seeded job count WITH names, so an operator running the full health check is NOT actually misled about whether seeding happened -- the truth is available two checks down. Net: a single misleading 'returned 0 jobs' line in an internal diagnostic command. Real but low.

user_story: As an operator, I want to probe whether a proactive cron's structured-state context contains specific ground-truth facts so that I can verify the USER.md envelope and journal search cover what a cron needs.
expected_behaviour: `probe_grounding(tenant, topic, expect_terms)` — read-only diagnostic. Renders the USER.md managed region via `render_managed_region` and checks for `topic` and each `expect_terms` substring. Runs `journal_search(tenant, topic, limit=20)` which replicates `RuntimeJournalSearchView` (Postgres full-text search on Document). Returns a `GroundingReport` with: `grounded` (all expected terms reachable), `topic_in_envelope`, `term_in_envelope` (per-term), `term_reachable` (in envelope OR any reachable doc), `term_in_project` (in a project-kind doc), `grounded_in_project`, `reachable_docs` list, `newest_source` datetime. Management command `grounding_probe` wraps this.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (Document FTS), Azure Storage (File Share for USER.md read)
inventory_notes: Read-only: no writes, no container calls. Designed as a test instrument for the proactive-grounding work — run before and after a change to verify the gap is closed.

feature: Suspended Tenant Container Hibernation (hibernate_suspended_task / hibernate_suspended command)
user_story: As a system, I want to scale suspended tenant containers to zero replicas so that billing is not incurred for containers belonging to lapsed subscribers.
expected_behaviour: `hibernate_suspended_task()` — QStash task. Queries ACTIVE=no, status=SUSPENDED tenants with non-empty `container_id`. Calls `hibernate_container_app(container_id)` for each. Returns {hibernated, failed, total}. Management command `hibernate_suspended --dry-run` wraps this. `force_hibernate_stale` management command covers additional cases: DB-marked hibernated with active Azure revisions, active tenants idle >24h, and optionally orphan oc-* containers in Azure with no matching tenant record.
dependencies: Azure Container Apps ARM API, Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: `force_hibernate_stale --include-orphans` lists all `oc-*` apps from Azure and removes those with no matching tenant DB record. Uses direct `client.container_apps_revisions.deactivate_revision` rather than the `hibernate_container_app` helper for revision-level control.

feature: Plugin Runtime Deps EmptyDir Mount (ensure_runtime_deps_mount / management command)
user_story: As an operator, I want to add the plugin-runtime-deps EmptyDir mount to existing tenant containers so that file-share EPERM errors on plugin installs are eliminated.
expected_behaviour: `ensure_plugin_runtime_deps_mount(container_id)` from `apps/orchestrator/azure_client.py` — checks whether an EmptyDir volume named `plugin-runtime-deps` is already in the container's template; if not, adds it via Azure ARM update (creates new revision in single-revision mode, which simultaneously wakes the container). Returns `True` if mount was added, `False` if already present. Management command `ensure_runtime_deps_mount --tenant UUID | --all [--dry-run]` calls this per tenant. Also called from `wake_hibernated_tenant` (step 1, plain-wake path) so hibernated tenants pick up the fix on next natural wake.
inventory_notes: New tenants get the mount at provisioning time via `create_container_app`. This command is a one-shot retrofit for previously provisioned active tenants. Skips hibernated tenants by default (`--all` only targets `hibernated_at__isnull=True`).

user_story: As a system, I want to delete delivered BufferedMessage rows older than 7 days so that the database does not accumulate stale delivery records.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `cleanup_delivered_buffers`. Deletes `BufferedMessage` rows where `delivered=True` and `created_at < now() - 7 days`. Returns {deleted: count}. Idempotent; runs as a periodic maintenance cron.
inventory_notes: 7-day retention covers the in-flight delivery window plus a buffer for debugging dropped messages. Only deletes delivered=True rows — FAILED rows are also deleted after 7 days.

user_story: As a system, I want to run journal extraction for each tenant when their local time hits 21:xx so that daily note data is processed at a consistent local-time anchor regardless of timezone.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `nightly_extraction` — hourly dispatcher. For each ACTIVE tenant: checks `_is_nightly_extraction_window_local(tenant, now=now, hour=21)` — compares `now.astimezone(tenant.user.timezone).hour == 21`. If not in window → skip. If `_already_ran_today_local(tenant, now=now)` → skip (uses `Tenant.last_nightly_extraction_at` stamped after prior run). Otherwise calls `run_extraction_for_tenant(tenant)` from `apps.journal.extraction`. Returns {considered, fired, skipped_window, skipped_already_ran, errored}.
inventory_notes: Uses local hour (not minute) so the hourly dispatcher has a full 60-minute acceptance window. Half-hour-offset timezones (IST, IRST, NPT) still get a clean daily hit. Guard uses `Tenant.last_nightly_extraction_at` so zero-item runs are still idempotent.

user_story: As a system, I want to push a fresh USER.md to every active tenant hourly so that the local-time line and state snapshot are never more than 1 hour stale.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `refresh_user_md_fleet`. Queries all ACTIVE tenants with `container_id`. For each: calls `push_user_md(tenant, force=True, debounce_seconds=0)`. Per-tenant failures are logged and skipped — one bad file share does not block the fleet. Returns {pushed, failed}. Includes hibernated tenants (file share is always mounted; the next wake-fired turn sees the freshly-pushed USER.md).
inventory_notes: Cost: one file-share read + one write per tenant (~150-400ms). Complements signal-driven refreshes which fire on state changes (goal saved, fuel logged, etc.).

user_story: As a system, I want to daily reconcile welcome crons for Fuel and Finance features so that tenants whose welcome was dropped (agent crash, container restart) are re-queued within 24 hours.
expected_behaviour: QStash task `reconcile_welcomes`. For each ACTIVE tenant with container: if `tenant.fuel_enabled` → calls `_schedule_fuel_welcome(tenant)` from `apps.fuel.views`; if `tenant.finance_active` → calls `_schedule_finance_welcome(tenant)` from `apps.finance.views`. Both schedulers are self-healing: replace stale one-shots whose fire date passed, skip confirmed-delivered, surface transport failures. Returns {tenants, fuel: Counter, finance: Counter, statuses} where Counter keys are WelcomeStatus values.
inventory_notes: Backstop for the deploy-time backfill + live toggle path. Any tenant whose welcome got orphaned will be re-queued within 24h.

user_story: As an operator, I want to send a one-off agent-driven message to a single tenant's container so that targeted announcements can be delivered via the assistant.
expected_behaviour: `broadcast_single_tenant_task(tenant_id, message)` — QStash task. Guards: tenant not found or no container_fqdn → return. No entitlement or not ACTIVE → skip with log. POSTs to `https://{fqdn}/v1/chat/completions` with `{model: 'openclaw', messages: [{role: 'user', content: message}]}`, using gateway JWT and X-User-Timezone header. Timeout 120s. Logs outcome (success, timeout, HTTP error, other error).
inventory_notes: The agent processes the message and delivers to the user via `nbhd_send_to_user`. This is the Django-side broadcast path — not used for cron-driven proactive outbound (those go through the container's internal cron scheduler).

user_story: As an operator, I want to list all tenants with their status, model tier, container info, and monthly message count so that I can get a quick fleet overview.
expected_behaviour: Management command `list_tenants [--status <status>]`. Queries all Tenant rows (with optional status filter) joined with User. Prints one line per tenant: UUID, display_name, status, model_tier, container_id, messages_this_month. No pagination or JSON output format.

user_story: As a system, I want settings-time gateway calls to be deferred gracefully for hibernated containers so that user-facing settings changes don't fail when the container is asleep.
expected_behaviour: `apply_or_defer_gateway_call(tenant, fn, label)` — synchronous helper used by settings endpoints that need to both write DB state and signal the running container. If `tenant.hibernated_at is None` (awake): calls `fn()`. If `fn()` raises `GatewayError` and `is_container_unavailable_error(exc)` is True: writes deferred state via `_write_deferred_state`. If hibernated: skips `fn()` immediately, calls `_write_deferred_state`. Returns the `DEFERRED` sentinel object in both defer cases, or the return value of `fn()` on success. `_write_deferred_state` calls `generate_openclaw_config + upload_config_to_file_share` once per request (guarded by `tenant._deferred_config_written` instance flag).
dependencies: Azure Storage (File Share), OpenClaw gateway (caller's fn), Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: Caller contract: DB mutation must already be committed before calling this. Caller must bump `pending_config_version` so the `apply_pending_configs` scheduler catches drift at next wake. Non-container-unavailable GatewayError propagates (not swallowed) — only genuine 'container is down' errors are deferred.

user_story: As a system, I want to render persona-specific workspace files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, IDENTITY.md, rule templates) at provisioning and config-refresh time so that each tenant's assistant has the correct personality and operational guidelines.
expected_behaviour: `render_workspace_files(persona_key, tenant)` from `apps/orchestrator/personas.py` — renders a set of workspace files keyed by env-var name (NBHD_AGENTS_MD, NBHD_SOUL_MD, NBHD_IDENTITY_MD, NBHD_DOC_TOOLS_REFERENCE, etc.) using Django template rendering with tenant context. Called during provisioning and from `update_tenant_config`. Overwrite-always files (AGENTS.md, doc/*) are written every refresh. Seed-once files (SOUL.md, IDENTITY.md) use `upload_workspace_file(..., skip_if_exists=True)` — never overwrite agent-evolved content. Rule files rendered by `render_workspace_rules()` are always overwritten to `workspace/rules/`.
dependencies: Azure Storage (File Share), Supabase/Postgres (tenant + user preferences)
inventory_notes: Persona key read from `tenant.user.preferences.get('agent_persona', 'neighbor')`. SOUL.md and IDENTITY.md use `skip_if_exists=True` to preserve agent evolution — mirrors the `[ ! -f ]` guards in the container's `entrypoint.sh`.

user_story: As a system, I want each tenant to have an isolated OpenRouter sub-key with a monthly spending cap so that one high-usage tenant cannot exhaust the shared API budget.
expected_behaviour: Created during `provision_tenant` when `OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED=True` and `secret_backend=keyvault`. Calls `create_sub_key(label, limit_dollars=TIER_COST_BUDGETS[tier], limit_reset='monthly')` from `apps.billing.openrouter_admin`. Stores key to Key Vault as `openrouter-<tenant_prefix>` secret, saves `openrouter_key_secret_name` and `openrouter_key_hash` to Tenant row. On provisioning failure: logs warning and falls back to shared key (non-fatal). Deleted during `deprovision_tenant`: `delete_sub_key(openrouter_key_hash)` then `_delete_secret_from_kv(openrouter_key_secret_name)`. Deletion failures log and continue — orphaned sub-keys are swept by `sweep_orphan_openrouter_keys` command.
dependencies: OpenRouter Admin API (create_sub_key, delete_sub_key), Azure Key Vault, Supabase/Postgres
inventory_notes: Feature-flagged via `OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED`. Limit reset is monthly. The Key Vault role assignment for the sub-key secret is included in `assign_key_vault_role` call alongside the internal-key secret.

user_story: As a system, I want to provision a new tenant's OpenClaw container so that a newly subscribed user has a running AI assistant.
expected_behaviour: Triggered by `provision_tenant(tenant_id)` (called from billing webhook or management command). Guards against unexpected status — only runs if tenant is PENDING or PROVISIONING; otherwise logs and returns. Steps in order: (1) generate OpenClaw config via `generate_openclaw_config`, run security audit (`audit_config_security`) — warnings logged, errors raise and reset status to PENDING; (2) create Azure User-Assigned Managed Identity named `mi-nbhd-<uuid[:20]>`; (3) generate per-tenant internal API key (64-char urlsafe token), write to Key Vault as `tenant-<uuid>-internal-key`, save on `Tenant.internal_api_key`; (3a) optionally create an OpenRouter per-tenant sub-key with tier-based spending limit, write to KV, save `openrouter_key_secret_name`/`openrouter_key_hash` — failure here is non-fatal, falls back to shared key; (4) grant MI Key Vault Secrets User on each secret (platform defaults + per-tenant key); (5) grant MI ACR pull role; (6) create Azure File Share `ws-<uuid[:20]>`, register with Container Apps environment; (7) upload `openclaw.json` to file share; (8) render workspace files for tenant's persona and write to file share; (9) create Container App named `oc-<uuid[:20]>` with image, identity, secrets, environment; (10) update Tenant row: `container_id`, `container_fqdn`, `managed_identity_id`, `container_image_tag`, `status=ACTIVE`, `provisioned_at`. On any exception in steps 1-10, status is reset to PENDING and exception re-raised. Post-provision non-critical steps (outside main try/except so failures don't revert): send Telegram welcome message and advance `onboarding_step`; send Day-0 welcome email (idempotent via `welcome_email_sent_at`); push USER.md with `force=True`; schedule `seed_cron_jobs` via QStash with 60s delay (falls back to direct call on QStash failure).
dependencies: Azure Container Apps, Azure Managed Identity, Azure Key Vault, Azure File Share, Azure Container Registry, OpenRouter Admin API (optional), QStash, Stripe/billing webhook (upstream trigger), apps/router (welcome message), apps/tenants/emails (welcome email), apps/orchestrator/workspace_envelope (USER.md)
inventory_notes: Status guard is strict: PENDING or PROVISIONING only. Security audit errors block provisioning. OpenRouter sub-key failure is non-fatal. Post-provision steps are isolated — a failure in welcome message does not revert the container. Azure MOCK mode (AZURE_MOCK=true) bypasses all Azure SDK calls.

user_story: As a system, I want to deprovision a cancelled tenant's Azure resources so that infrastructure costs are eliminated and the tenant's data is cleaned up.
expected_behaviour: Triggered by `deprovision_tenant(tenant_id)` (called from billing webhook on subscription cancellation, or management command). Sets `Tenant.status=DEPROVISIONING` immediately. Steps: (1) delete OpenRouter per-tenant sub-key via OpenRouter admin API (non-fatal on error, swept later by orphan sweeper); (2) delete KV secret for OpenRouter key (non-fatal); (3) delete Container App by `container_id` (skipped if empty); (4) delete Azure File Share + environment storage registration; (5) delete Managed Identity. On success: `status=DELETED`, `container_id`, `container_fqdn`, `managed_identity_id`, `openrouter_key_secret_name`, `openrouter_key_hash` all cleared to empty string. On exception: `status=SUSPENDED`, exception re-raised. OpenRouter cleanup failures are logged as warnings, not exceptions.
dependencies: Azure Container Apps, Azure Managed Identity, Azure File Share, Azure Container Apps Environment, OpenRouter Admin API (optional), apps/byo_models/services (KV secret delete)
inventory_notes: Leftover OpenRouter sub-keys are recovered by the sweep_orphan_openrouter_keys command, not blocking deprovision. File share deletion cascades the environment storage binding. No RLS/Postgres data is deleted here — only Azure resources are torn down.

user_story: As an operator, I want to re-run provisioning for tenants that are stuck in PENDING/PROVISIONING state or missing container metadata, so that failed provisioning attempts self-heal.
expected_behaviour: Invoked via `repair_stale_tenant_provisioning(tenant_id, limit, dry_run)` or management command `repair_tenant_provisioning`. Finds tenants with status in PENDING/PROVISIONING/ACTIVE that have empty `container_id` or `container_fqdn`. In dry-run mode, lists them with missing fields, returns skipped count. In live mode, calls `provision_tenant()` for each; after provision, re-reads tenant and reports `repaired` (both fields set + status ACTIVE) or `incomplete`. Per-tenant exceptions are caught and logged, incrementing `failed` count so one tenant's failure does not block others. Returns a summary dict: `{evaluated, repaired, failed, skipped, dry_run, results[]}`. Management command supports `--tenant-id`, `--limit` (default 50), `--dry-run`.
inventory_notes: The task form (`repair_stale_tenant_provisioning_task`) always runs with dry_run=False and limit=50. The management command exposes all three args. Active tenants with missing metadata are included because a partial provision leaves them in a broken state despite ACTIVE status.

user_story: As an operator, I want to atomically bump a tenant's OpenClaw version (config schema + container image) so that the new runtime and the new config are always in sync.
expected_behaviour: Implemented by `bump_openclaw_version_for_tenant(tenant, target_version, image_tag, registry)`. Takes a snapshot of the current `openclaw.json` from the file share for rollback. Sets `tenant.openclaw_version = target_version` in DB. Then: (1) calls `update_tenant_config` to regenerate and push the version-correct config to the file share and workspace files; (2) calls `update_container_image` to deploy `registry/nbhd-openclaw:image_tag`. If image push fails: restores prior `openclaw.json` to file share (best-effort, logs on failure) and rolls back `tenant.openclaw_version` in DB, then re-raises. If image push succeeds: updates `container_image_tag` in DB; clears `hibernated_at` if the tenant was hibernated. IMPORTANT: `update_tenant_config` failures do NOT trigger a file-share restore because gateway.reload failures on a hibernated/booting container should not undo the config fix. DB version is rolled back on any outer exception. Hibernated tenants are NOT skipped — single-revision mode auto-wakes them.
dependencies: Azure Container Apps, Azure File Share, apps/tenants models
inventory_notes: The task form is idempotent: skips if already at target_version + image_tag. Skips suspended/deprovisioning tenants. Management command supports `--tenant UUID`, `--all` (mutually exclusive), `--dry-run`. Fleet fan-out is done via QStash (rollout-atomic-bump endpoint) calling the task per tenant rather than sequential CLI calls. Config-snapshot restore is intentionally NOT triggered on update_tenant_config failure — see inline comments on the gateway.reload 404 gotcha.

user_story: As a system, I want to regenerate and push the OpenClaw config for a tenant so that config changes (model, tools, cron prompts, persona) take effect on the next container restart.
expected_behaviour: Invoked by `update_tenant_config(tenant_id)`. Guards: status must be ACTIVE and `container_id` must be non-empty; otherwise logs warning and returns. Steps: (1) call `generate_openclaw_config(tenant)`, run `audit_config_security` (warnings logged, errors raise); (2) write `openclaw.json` to file share via `upload_config_to_file_share`; (3) upload workspace files to file share: always-overwrite files include AGENTS.md, tools-reference.md, channel-formatting.md, cron-management.md, error-handling.md, privacy-redaction.md, platform-guide.md (or silent variant based on `feature_tips_enabled`), all rule templates from `templates/openclaw/rules/`; seed-once files (SOUL.md, IDENTITY.md) use `skip_if_exists=True` so agent-evolved content is never overwritten; (4) push USER.md via `push_user_md(tenant, force=True)`; (5) call `refresh_system_cron_rows_from_seed(tenant)` to sync Postgres CronJob rows from the current seed (signal fires `regenerate_tenant_crons`).
dependencies: Azure File Share, apps/orchestrator/config_generator, apps/orchestrator/personas, apps/orchestrator/workspace_envelope, apps/cron models (CronJob rows), QStash (reconcile signal)
inventory_notes: SOUL.md and IDENTITY.md are intentionally seed-once — overwriting them would wipe agent-evolved persona. The apply_single_tenant_config_task additionally advances `config_version = pending_config_version` and stamps `applied_model`/`applied_model_at` after a successful update_tenant_config call. force_apply_configs management command bypasses the pending-version gate and applies to all active tenants immediately.

user_story: As a system, I want to apply a pending config version for a single tenant so that model or settings changes queued by the subscriber console reach the container on the next restart.
expected_behaviour: `apply_single_tenant_config_task(tenant_id, _is_followup_retry=False)`. Looks up tenant; if not found, returns. If `config_version >= pending_config_version`, returns (nothing pending). Calls `update_tenant_config(tenant_id)` — on exception, logs and returns without advancing version. After successful update, advances `config_version = pending_config_version`, stamps `config_refreshed_at`, `applied_model`, `applied_model_at`. For hibernated tenants, logs that the file write is ready for wake but does not wake. The `_is_followup_retry` flag is accepted for compatibility but no longer drives retry behavior.
inventory_notes: Enqueued by apply-pending-configs cron endpoint for each tenant whose pending_config_version > config_version. Version gate is idempotent — duplicate QStash deliveries are no-ops.
error_detail: Confirmed real. apply_single_tenant_config_task advances config_version to pending_config_version unconditionally (tasks.py:182-187) regardless of whether update_tenant_config actually wrote. update_tenant_config (services.py:570-581) guards: if status is not ACTIVE or container_id is empty, it logs a warning and returns silently WITHOUT raising. So if a tenant becomes SUSPENDED or loses container_id after the task version gate at tasks.py:171 but before the status re-read at services.py:574, the file-share write is skipped, yet the task still stamps config_version equal to pending_config_version. The race window is wider than the report frames it: it spans the whole QStash delivery latency between apply_pending_configs enqueuing the task (when tenant was ACTIVE, awake, idle; cron/views.py:441-446) and the services.py:574 re-read. Once config_version is wrongly advanced, no automated path recovers the dropped change: apply_pending_configs only enqueues when pending greater than config (cron/views.py:442); billing reactivation re-queues only if pending greater than config (billing/services.py:370-373), now false; idle-wake reconcile only force-bumps for version_synced or config_version zero (hibernation.py:544); even the operator manual force_apply_configs gates on pending greater than config (force_apply_configs.py:21), so a human force-apply also no-ops. The dropped change reaches the share only after some unrelated future config edit re-bumps pending or a deliberate bump_config_version. Impact: a config change (model swap, cron-prompt edit) silently never reaches the container; the dashboard reports it as applied (has_pending_update false, tenants/views.py:283); recovery needs a manual re-bump nothing prompts for. Genuine but low-frequency (suspensions are Stripe-webhook driven and must coincide with an in-flight apply) and self-limiting (next legitimate config edit re-applies). Medium because it silently drops persisted desired state and evades every recovery path including manual force-apply.

feature: Container Image Update Task (with Cron Snapshot + Restore)
user_story: As a system, I want to update a tenant's container image while preserving their cron jobs so that an image swap does not silently wipe user-created crons.
expected_behaviour: `apply_single_tenant_image_task(tenant_id, desired_tag)`. Skips hibernated tenants (they pick up on wake). Phase 1: snapshots current cron list via `cron.list` gateway call and saves as `Tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot` JSON (non-fatal on failure). Phase 2: calls `update_container_image` with `registry/nbhd-openclaw:desired_tag`; also syncs `openclaw_version` by calling `openclaw_version_for_image_tag(desired_tag)` — if version changed, updates `Tenant.openclaw_version` and re-runs `update_tenant_config` to regenerate the schema-correct config for the new image; updates `Tenant.container_image_tag`. Phase 3: publishes QStash task `restore_crons_after_image_update` with 90s delay and idempotency key `post-image-restore-{tenant_id}-{desired_tag}`.
dependencies: Azure Container Apps, Azure File Share, apps/orchestrator/tool_policy (version-tag mapping), QStash, apps/cron/gateway_client
inventory_notes: Config regen after version sync is critical: the batch config task (fired alongside this one) ran against the OLD openclaw_version and produces wrong-schema output for the new image. Version-image lockstep mirrors bump_openclaw_version_for_tenant. Hibernated tenants are skipped explicitly — their cron snapshot is already persisted and restore happens on wake.

user_story: As a system, I want to restore cron jobs from a pre-image snapshot after the container restarts so that user-created crons are not permanently lost.
expected_behaviour: `restore_crons_after_image_update_task(tenant_id)`. Called ~90s after image update. Gets snapshot from `Tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot`; falls back to `seed_cron_jobs` if missing. Calls `cron.list` on the live container to get already-restored jobs; falls back to `seed_cron_jobs` if list fails. Filters snapshot to skip: (a) system cron names (from `build_cron_seed_jobs`) — those are restored by the postgres-canonical reconciler; (b) jobs already present on container; (c) duplicate names within snapshot (dirty snapshots). For each user job to restore: strips internal fields (`id`, `jobId`, `createdAt`, `state`, `createdAtMs`, `updatedAtMs`, `nextRunAtMs`, `runningAtMs`), calls `cron.add`. For postgres-canonical tenants, also enqueues `regenerate_tenant_crons` with 5s delay (idempotency key dedupes with signal path). Runs `_fire_missed_crons_after_restore` to re-trigger cron-kind jobs that should have fired during the outage (capped at 5 distinct jobs; skips `_sync:` and `_fuel:` prefixes; skips jobs without a future `nextRunAtMs`). If tenant has fuel profile with `use_session_scheduling`, calls `regenerate_fuel_crons`.
inventory_notes: System crons are intentionally excluded from snapshot restore — Postgres is canonical for them and the reconciler pushes fresh seed state with correct model/kind. The missed-fire catch-up exists because OC 5.7's startup catch-up requires `lastRunAtMs` on the job, which is stripped during restore. Cap of 5 missed fires prevents gateway flood on long outages.

user_story: As a system, I want to deactivate container revisions for tenants idle for more than 2 hours so that Azure compute costs are minimized during inactive periods.
expected_behaviour: `hibernate_idle_tenants_task()`: runs hourly. Queries active tenants with `container_id`, no `hibernated_at`, whose `last_message_at < now-2h` (or `provisioned_at < now-2h` if never messaged), and `cron_wake_at` is null or old. Uses `SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` to claim candidates without holding the lock across Azure calls. Per-tenant: refreshes fields, skips if already hibernated, recently messaged, or cron-woken. Calls `_cron_active_or_imminent(tenant, window=300s)` — if any cron is `runningAtMs` set (in-flight) or `nextRunAtMs <= now+300s`, defers and increments `skipped_imminent_cron`. Otherwise calls `hibernate_idle_tenant(tenant)`. `hibernate_idle_tenant`: (1) captures cron schedules via `cron.list` (falls back to snapshot then seed); (2) suspends crons via `suspend_tenant_crons`; (3) calls `hibernate_container_app` (deactivate all revisions to 0 replicas); (4) sets `hibernated_at=now`, clears `cron_wake_at`; (5) schedules QStash `wake_for_cron` task with idempotency key `wake-cron-{tenant_id}-{earliest_ms}` and delay = max(60, (next_cron_ms - now_ms)/1000 - 240).
dependencies: Azure Container Apps, apps/cron/suspension, apps/cron/publish (QStash), apps/tenants models, croniter (next-run computation)
inventory_notes: The 300s defer window prevents killing a container when a cron fires at the same minute as the sweep (the :00/:00 race). `_cron_active_or_imminent` is conservative on GatewayError — returns None (do not block sweep) rather than deferring, because the 2h cutoff and cron_wake_at already act as backstops. force_hibernate_stale management command covers 4 cases: DB-marked hibernated, idle >24h active, suspended, and orphan containers.

user_story: As a system, I want to wake a hibernated tenant's container before their next scheduled cron fires so that the cron message reaches the user without a cold-start miss.
expected_behaviour: `wake_for_cron_task(tenant_id)`: QStash-delivered ~240s before the next scheduled cron. If tenant not hibernated: re-arms the next cron wake by capturing schedules and scheduling the next `wake_for_cron` task, returns `already_awake`. If not ACTIVE: returns `not_active`. Otherwise calls `wake_hibernated_tenant(tenant)`. `wake_hibernated_tenant`: (a) if `desired_tag != current_tag`, calls `update_container_image` (image-refresh path), syncs `openclaw_version` via `openclaw_version_for_image_tag`, updates `container_image_tag` and optionally `openclaw_version` in DB; (b) else if `ensure_plugin_runtime_deps_mount` returns True (mount added, new revision created), container wakes via revision flip; (c) else calls `wake_container_app` to activate existing revision. Clears `hibernated_at`, stamps `last_wake_at`. If version was synced or `config_version == 0`, bumps pending config and enqueues `apply_single_tenant_config`. Schedules QStash `deliver_buffered_messages` with 45s delay (retries=1) and `resume_hibernated_crons` with 60s delay. After wake, stamps `cron_wake_at=now`, schedules `check_cron_wake_idle` with 1800s delay.
dependencies: Azure Container Apps, QStash, apps/cron/suspension (resume), apps/orchestrator/tool_policy (image-to-version mapping), croniter
inventory_notes: Wake lead time is 240s (`_CRON_WAKE_LEAD_SECONDS`) to cover worst-case cold start including plugin-runtime-deps install on EmptyDir. Image-refresh on wake fixes the 'wake-on-old-image' bug where hibernated tenants miss fleet rollouts. Single QStash retry (retries=1) on deliver_buffered_messages avoids concurrent turn storm on BYO Claude with long first turns.

user_story: As a system, I want to re-hibernate a cron-woken container after 30 minutes of inactivity so that containers don't stay awake indefinitely after their cron fires.
expected_behaviour: `check_cron_wake_idle_task(tenant_id)`: called 30 minutes after a cron wake. Returns early if: tenant not found, no `cron_wake_at`, already hibernated. Checks `last_message_at > cron_wake_at` — if user messaged, clears `cron_wake_at` and returns `user_active`. Otherwise calls `_next_cron_within_window(tenant, window=5400s)` — if another cron fires within 90 min, schedules a deferred re-check at `(next_cron_ms - now_ms)/1000 + 1800s` and returns `deferred_for_upcoming_cron`. If scheduling fails, falls through to re-hibernate. If no user activity and no upcoming cron: clears `cron_wake_at`, refreshes tenant from DB, calls `hibernate_idle_tenant(tenant)` (which schedules the next cron wake), returns `re_hibernated`. The 90-min look-ahead avoids forcing back-to-back crons through cold-start cycles.
inventory_notes: Falls through to re-hibernate if publish_task raises, so the system never gets stuck in an awake state with no future check. `_next_cron_within_window` prefers live `cron.list`; falls back to `tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot` on GatewayError.

user_story: As a system, I want to forward buffered messages from hibernated tenants to their container after wake so that messages sent during hibernation are not lost.
expected_behaviour: `deliver_buffered_messages_task(tenant_id)`: called ~45s after wake via QStash (retries=1). Resolves per-model timeout via `_resolve_chat_timeout` (BYO Claude/reasoning models get longer timeout). Loops over undelivered `BufferedMessage` rows for the tenant. LINE rows: claims a batch via `_claim_buffered_batch_for_tenant` (SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, lease = timeout * 1.5); voice rows are singletons. For multi-row batches: coalesces into one `/v1/chat/completions` POST using `format_coalesced_user_content`. Sends response to user via `relay_ai_response_to_line`. Telegram rows: claimed individually via `_claim_next_buffered_message`, forwarded via `forward_to_openclaw` (async, 30s timeout, 1 retry). On transient 5xx/connection errors: retries with backoffs [5s, 15s, 45s] before counting as a delivery attempt. Past `_MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS` (3): marks `delivered=True, status=failed`, calls `_send_apology_for_dropped_message` (LINE only; Telegram apology not implemented). On any failed delivery, raises `RuntimeError` so QStash retries the task. Returns dict with `{delivered, failed, dropped, skipped_in_flight}`.
dependencies: apps/router/models (BufferedMessage), apps/router/services (forward_to_openclaw, format_coalesced_user_content), apps/router/line_webhook (relay_ai_response_to_line, _send_line_text), apps/cron/gateway_client, QStash
inventory_notes: SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED prevents concurrent QStash retries from double-delivering the same row. Lease duration = timeout * 1.5 bounds how long a concurrent retry must wait. LINE coalescing is the cold-start solution for multi-message bursts. Telegram apology is logged-only, not implemented.

user_story: As a system, I want to deactivate container revisions for billing-suspended tenants so that suspended accounts do not accrue Azure compute costs.
expected_behaviour: `hibernate_suspended_task()`: queries all tenants with `status=SUSPENDED` and non-empty `container_id`. For each, calls `hibernate_container_app(tenant.container_id)` which deactivates all revisions to 0 replicas. Returns `{hibernated, failed, total}`. Does NOT set `hibernated_at` (that field tracks idle hibernation of ACTIVE tenants; suspended tenants have a separate billing-status path). Management command `hibernate_suspended` supports `--dry-run`.
inventory_notes: Distinct from idle hibernation: SUSPENDED = billing status, hibernated_at = idle cost-saving. The management command also handles the one-shot force-hibernate-stale sweep that covers DB-marked-hibernated, idle->24h, suspended, and orphan containers.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As a system, I want settings changes to be written to the file share when a tenant is hibernated so that the container picks up the new state on next wake.
expected_behaviour: `apply_or_defer_gateway_call(tenant, fn, label)`: if tenant is not hibernated, runs `fn()` synchronously; on `GatewayError` where `is_container_unavailable_error` is True, defers (writes config to file share); re-raises non-availability GatewayErrors. If tenant is hibernated, skips the live call entirely and defers. Defer path calls `_write_deferred_state(tenant, label)` which generates the config via `generate_openclaw_config`, serializes, and uploads to file share. A per-request instance flag `_deferred_config_written` deduplicates multiple deferred wraps in a single request. Returns the sentinel `DEFERRED` object when the live call was skipped, or the `fn()` return value on success. Caller contract: DB mutation and `pending_config_version` bump must already have happened before calling this helper.
inventory_notes: Used by dashboard settings endpoints (model change, heartbeat toggle, etc.) to avoid 5xx when the container is down. The apply_pending_configs cron catches up at next wake via the bumped pending_config_version. Only non-availability GatewayErrors are swallowed — real bugs propagate.

user_story: As a system, I want to seed default cron jobs into a new tenant's container so that Morning Briefing, Evening Check-in, Background Tasks and other system crons are active from day one.
expected_behaviour: `seed_cron_jobs(tenant)`: resolves tenant if string. For postgres-canonical tenants (`postgres_cron_canonical=True`): upserts CronJob rows from `build_cron_seed_jobs`, skipping names already present; signal fires `regenerate_tenant_crons`. For legacy tenants: calls `cron.list` with 3-attempt retry on 502/503/504; refuses to seed if response is unparseable (would create duplicates). Diffs by name (case-insensitive); creates only missing jobs via `cron.add` with retry. After creation: runs `dedup_tenant_cron_jobs`. Then: queries post-seed `cron.list`, calls `restore_user_cron_jobs(tenant, post_seed_names)` to re-add user-created jobs from `Tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot` that were not in the seed set. Runs a final dedup after restore. Returns `{tenant_id, jobs_total, created, errors, skipped_existing, user_jobs_restored}`.
dependencies: apps/cron/gateway_client (cron.list, cron.add), apps/cron/models (CronJob), apps/orchestrator/config_generator (build_cron_seed_jobs), QStash (post-provision delay)
inventory_notes: Refusing to seed on unparseable cron.list is intentional to prevent duplicates on transient gateway errors. System generated prefixes (`_sync:`, `_fuel:`) are excluded from user-job restore. In MOCK mode, always returns success without Azure/gateway calls. Scheduled via QStash with 60s delay post-provision to allow container warm-up.

user_story: As a system, I want to keep Postgres CronJob rows in sync with the current seed definition so that config refreshes update system cron prompts fleet-wide without manual intervention.
expected_behaviour: `refresh_system_cron_rows_from_seed(tenant)`: iterates `build_cron_seed_jobs`. For each: if no row exists, creates one with `source=SYSTEM, managed=True`. If row exists: compares stored `payload.message` body (after stripping date preamble) against `_KNOWN_DEFAULT_PREFIXES` — if message matches a known default, refreshes all fields from seed; if user-customized, refreshes all non-message fields (model, kind, schedule, delivery) but preserves the custom message. Records counts: created, updated, preserved_custom, unchanged, reaped. After loop: reaps managed system crons not in seed names (name fell out of `build_cron_seed_jobs`) by deleting them — CronJob post_delete signal pushes `cron.remove` to container. Skips `_sync:` and `_fuel:` prefixes in reap pass. Called from `update_tenant_config`, the management command `update_cron_prompts`, and `restore_crons_after_image_update_task`.
inventory_notes: The `_KNOWN_DEFAULT_PREFIXES` list must be extended when a seed prompt's opening sentence changes to prevent the entire fleet from being classified as user-customized. Old prefixes must remain for one release cycle. The shared preamble marker `**MANDATORY — do this BEFORE` is the dominant prefix since it precedes all cron message bodies.

user_story: As an operator, I want to remove duplicate cron jobs from tenant containers so that system crons don't fire multiple times and users see a clean schedule.
expected_behaviour: `dedup_tenant_cron_jobs(tenant, dry_run, jobs)`: if `jobs` not pre-fetched, calls `cron.list`. Groups by name; for each name with >1 job, sorts by `createdAtMs` descending, marks all but newest for deletion. In dry_run mode returns duplicates list without deleting. Otherwise calls `cron.remove` for each duplicate. Returns `{kept, deleted, errors, duplicates}`. `dedup_cron_jobs_task(tenant_id)`: thin wrapper for QStash dispatch. Management command `dedup_cron_jobs` defaults to dry-run; requires `--apply` to actually delete; supports `--tenant UUID`; logs all duplicates per tenant.
inventory_notes: Sort is by `createdAtMs` epoch milliseconds (the field OC returns), not `createdAt` string. Old code used `createdAt` string and UUID fallback, which kept an arbitrary copy. `cleanup_cron_jobs` management command is an older implementation that also handles the older gateway response shape (top-level `jobs` key without `details` wrapper).

feature: User Cron Job Restore from Snapshot
user_story: As a system, I want to restore user-created cron jobs from a Postgres snapshot after a container restart so that user reminders and custom schedules are not permanently lost.
expected_behaviour: `restore_user_cron_jobs(tenant, existing_job_names)`: reads `Tenant.cron_jobs_snapshot.jobs`. Excludes system job names (SYSTEM_JOB_NAMES set), `_sync:` and `_fuel:` prefixes, jobs already in `existing_job_names` (case-insensitive). Deduplicates within snapshot. For each remaining job: strips internal gateway fields, calls `cron.add`. Returns `{restored, errors}`. Called from `seed_cron_jobs` post-seed and from `restore_crons_after_image_update_task`.
inventory_notes: System job names exclusion prevents restoring stale system cron payloads from before a schema migration. Snapshot dedup handles dirty snapshots saved before the tenant_views dedup fix.

user_story: As a system, I want to update system cron prompts and timezones on the live container so that timezone corrections and prompt template changes reach running tenants without requiring a container restart.
expected_behaviour: `update_system_cron_prompts(tenant)`: calls `cron.list` on live gateway. For each desired system job: checks message body against `_KNOWN_DEFAULT_PREFIXES` (default = replaceable); checks non-message payload fields (`model`, `kind`) for drift; checks timezone. If only timezone differs: patches via `cron.update`. If payload (model/kind/message) differs and job is in `_SYSTEM_JOB_NAMES`: deletes then re-adds (delete+create path for migration channel). After update loop: seeds any missing system jobs in `_SYSTEM_JOB_NAMES`. Calls `sync_heartbeat_cron` for heartbeat add/remove/schedule drift. Calls `push_user_md(tenant, force=True)`. Returns `{tenant_id, updated, skipped, errors}`. Container-unavailable GatewayErrors propagate so the deferral helper can intercept.
inventory_notes: Non-message payload drift (model, kind) forces recreate even if the user customized the message, because a silently-failing cron (wrong model rejected by preflight) is worse than losing a custom prompt. User-customized message loss is logged as a warning. sync_heartbeat_cron is an orthogonal check: heartbeat_enabled=True → job must exist; False → job must not exist.

user_story: As a system, I want to schedule wake tasks for upcoming one-off at-crons so that reminder crons created mid-conversation are not missed if the container goes down before their fire time.
expected_behaviour: `ensure_at_cron_wakes_task()`: runs every 5 minutes. Queries active non-hibernated tenants. For each, calls `cron.list`. Iterates jobs with `schedule.kind == 'at'` and `enabled != False`. Computes fire time via `_at_fires_at_ms`. Skips fires already past or more than 2 hours out. For qualifying fires: publishes QStash `wake_for_cron` task with `delay = max(60, (fires_at_ms - now_ms)/1000 - 240)` and idempotency key `wake-cron-{tenant_id}-{fires_at_ms}`. Returns `{tenants, scheduled, errors, skipped}`. Skips tenants that fail `cron.list` (non-fatal).
inventory_notes: Covers the gap left by the main wake scheduling path: idle_hibernate_tenants only schedules wake when it hibernates a container; active containers don't go through that path. 5-minute window is acceptable because sub-5-minute at-crons fire while the container is still awake. Idempotency key on (tenant, fire_time_ms) dedupes across sweeps.

user_story: As a system, I want to re-enable suspended cron jobs after a container wakes from hibernation so that the cron schedule resumes without operator intervention.
expected_behaviour: `resume_hibernated_crons_task(tenant_id)` and `resume_tenant_crons_task(tenant_id)`: called ~60s after wake via QStash. Looks up tenant; returns early if not found or no `container_fqdn`. Calls `resume_tenant_crons(tenant)` from `apps.cron.suspension`, which flips crons with `enabled=False` to `enabled=True`. Idempotent: already-enabled crons are no-ops. Returns `{enabled, already_enabled, errors, job_names}`. `resume_tenant_crons_task` is for Stripe webhook-triggered reactivation (delayed 30s after checkout); `resume_hibernated_crons_task` is for wake path (delayed 60s).
inventory_notes: Two separate tasks for semantic clarity: resume_hibernated_crons is for wake; resume_tenant_crons is for billing reactivation. The 60s delay for wake path is necessary because the container gateway is not ready immediately after revision activation.

user_story: As an operator, I want to roll out a new OpenClaw container image to all active tenants simultaneously so that urgent changes (new binary, security fix) reach the fleet without waiting for organic wake cycles.
expected_behaviour: Management command `bump_all_tenant_images`. Requires `--tag <sha>` (refuses `latest` for idempotence). Queries active tenants with non-empty `container_id`; optionally includes hibernated tenants via `--include-hibernated`. Computes idempotence: skips tenants already at `target_tag`. Uses `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=5)` (configurable via `--max-workers`) to call `update_container_image` concurrently, then updates `Tenant.container_image_tag` on the main thread after each success. Reports `succeeded/failed/skipped`. Non-zero exit (CommandError) if any tenant fails. Dry-run lists targets without calling Azure.
inventory_notes: This command does NOT update `openclaw_version` — it's an image-only bump. For combined version+config+image bump, use `bump_openclaw_version` instead. DB tag update happens on main thread after the concurrent Azure call to keep test transactions correct. Rate limit: ~30 RPM for Container Apps ARM API; 5 workers provides ~10-batch rollout.

user_story: As an operator, I want to migrate existing tenants from the shared internal API key to per-tenant keys in Key Vault so that a compromised tenant cannot pivot to other tenants' data.
expected_behaviour: Management command `migrate_tenants_to_per_tenant_keys`. Finds active tenants with `internal_api_key == ''` and non-empty `container_id`/`managed_identity_id`. Per tenant: (1) generate `token_urlsafe(48)`; (2) write to Key Vault as `tenant-<uuid>-internal-key`; (3) resolve managed identity's `principal_id` via Azure SDK; (4) grant Key Vault Secrets User RBAC on the new secret; (5) rebind the container's `nbhd-internal-api-key` secret reference to the KV secret (triggers new revision); (6) save `Tenant.internal_api_key = token` in DB (last, so failures allow clean retry). Supports `--tenant-id`, `--dry-run`, `--max N`. Idempotent: already-migrated tenants are skipped. Per-tenant error isolation.
dependencies: Azure Key Vault, Azure Managed Identity, Azure Container Apps, apps/tenants models
inventory_notes: DB save is last so any Azure failure allows clean retry (earlier Azure idempotent). Dual-validation in `apps/integrations/internal_auth.py` keeps the old shared key working during the revision rollout window. This is a one-shot fleet migration command, not a recurring task.

user_story: As a system, I want to push a rendered USER.md to the tenant's Azure file share so that the AI assistant has up-to-date profile, goals, tasks, fuel, finance, and journal context on every turn.
expected_behaviour: `push_user_md(tenant, force, debounce_seconds)`: merges platform-managed block into existing USER.md on the file share. Platform block is wrapped in HTML comment sentinels (`BEGIN: NBHD-managed user state` / `END: NBHD-managed user state`). Three-case merge algorithm: (1) empty/boilerplate → write managed block alone; (2) has sentinels → replace only the managed region; (3) agent content without sentinels → prepend managed region, preserve agent content below. Managed region includes: current local time line (from `tenant.user.timezone`), synthesis hint, and all registered envelope sections from `apps.orchestrator.envelope_registry.all_sections()`. Uses leading-edge cache debounce (default 60s) keyed by tenant to prevent N post_save signals from flooding the file share. `force=True` bypasses debounce. Fleet push task `refresh_user_md_fleet_task()` calls `push_user_md(tenant, force=True, debounce_seconds=0)` for all active tenants (including hibernated, since the file share is always mounted).
dependencies: Azure File Share (download + upload workspace file), apps/orchestrator/envelope_registry, apps/tenants models, Django cache (debounce)
inventory_notes: SOUL.md and IDENTITY.md are agent-owned after first seed; `push_user_md` never touches them. The `_OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_USER_MD` constant identifies OC's boilerplate placeholder and treats it as empty so first refresh replaces it cleanly. `audit_user_md` command classifies each tenant's file as missing/empty/boilerplate/managed/agent without writing.

user_story: As a system, I want to run nightly data extraction for each tenant during their local 9 PM hour so that journal entries and structured data are extracted and stored daily.
expected_behaviour: `nightly_extraction_task()`: hourly dispatcher. For each active tenant (iterator to avoid loading all rows): checks `_is_nightly_extraction_window_local(tenant, now, hour=21)` — returns True if the tenant's local time hour equals 21 (using `tenant.user.timezone`). Checks `_already_ran_today_local(tenant, now)` via `Tenant.last_nightly_extraction_at` compared to local date. If in window and not yet run: calls `run_extraction_for_tenant(tenant)`. Per-tenant exceptions are logged and counted as `errored`. Returns counts: `{considered, fired, skipped_window, skipped_already_ran, errored}`.
inventory_notes: Hour-only matching (not minute) gives a 60-minute acceptance window so tenants in half-hour-offset timezones (IST, NPT) still get a clean daily hit. `last_nightly_extraction_at` guard is per-local-date so the entire 21:xx hour can fire without re-firing. Runs even for tenants with no journal activity (extraction may still commit to updating last_nightly_extraction_at).

user_story: As a system, I want to reconcile _fuel:* cron jobs in the container with the derived set from Postgres so that workout session reminders stay in sync with the user's fuel schedule.
expected_behaviour: `regenerate_fuel_crons_task(tenant_id)`: enqueued by fuel signals with 30s debounce. Skips hibernated or suspended tenants (logs and returns early). Calls `regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant)` from `apps.orchestrator.fuel_cron`. Returns `{added, removed, unchanged, errors}`. `reconcile_fuel_crons_task()`: hourly fleet-wide; iterates all `FuelProfile` rows with `use_session_scheduling=True`; skips tenants without `container_fqdn`; calls `regenerate_fuel_crons` per tenant; accumulates totals.
inventory_notes: Hibernated/suspended skip is important: HealthKit background syncs trigger fuel signals and would produce recurring GatewayError noise for idle tenants. Reconcile-on-wake catches drift. Fleet reconcile uses FuelProfile as the source of truth, not Tenant.status directly.
error_detail: Confirmed. The hourly fleet-wide reconcile_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1030-1056) iterates every FuelProfile with use_session_scheduling=True and calls regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant) for any tenant whose container_fqdn is non-empty. Its only filter is 'if not tenant.container_fqdn: continue'. Hibernation does NOT clear container_fqdn: hibernate_idle_tenant (hibernation.py:108-110) only sets hibernated_at and scales the container to 0/0; container_fqdn is cleared only on deprovision/DELETE (services.py:741). So hibernated and suspended tenants pass the fqdn check, and the sweep issues a doomed gateway POST to a scaled-to-zero container. regenerate_fuel_crons (fuel_cron.py:107-141) has no hibernation/suspension guard of its own (it only checks use_session_scheduling), then calls invoke_gateway_tool 'cron.list', which after a 45s timeout plus one retry raises GatewayError, caught as summary['errors'] += 1. The signal-driven per-tenant path regenerate_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1018-1027) DOES skip hibernated/suspended tenants, with a comment (tasks.py:1021-1023) explicitly naming this exact failure mode (gateway-call noise on scale 0/0 containers). The fleet reconcile bypasses that reasoning. Operator impact: per hibernated/suspended tenant with an active session-scheduled fuel profile, every hourly sweep wastes up to ~90s on a failing gateway call, logs a GatewayError, and inflates totals['errors'] (masking real drift errors); the POST may also cold-start an idle container, partially undermining hibernation. No user-visible data loss (reconcile-on-wake catches up). Low severity given the small current fleet, but it scales with adoption.

user_story: As a system, I want to reconcile the live container's cron SQLite with the Postgres CronJob table so that system cron drift (from container restarts or SQLite wipes) is automatically corrected.
expected_behaviour: `regenerate_tenant_crons_task(tenant_id)`: no-op if `postgres_cron_canonical=False`. Calls `regenerate_tenant_crons(tenant)` from `apps.orchestrator.cron_reconcile`. Returns `{added, removed, unchanged, errors}`. `reconcile_tenant_crons_task()`: hourly fleet-wide; iterates active tenants with `postgres_cron_canonical=True` and non-empty `container_fqdn`; calls `regenerate_tenant_crons` per tenant; accumulates totals.
inventory_notes: Enqueued by `apps/cron/signals.py` with 30s debounce on CronJob save/delete. The per-tenant task is idempotent: diff between Postgres truth and live container state, only add/remove what changed. Fleet task skips tenants on the legacy direct-gateway path (`postgres_cron_canonical=False`).

user_story: As a system, I want to detect and re-schedule missed welcome crons for tenants so that users with Fuel or Finance features always receive their onboarding message.
expected_behaviour: `reconcile_welcomes_task()`: daily fleet sweep. Iterates active tenants with containers. For each: calls `_schedule_fuel_welcome(tenant)` if `tenant.fuel_enabled`; calls `_schedule_finance_welcome(tenant)` if `tenant.finance_active`. Tallies `WelcomeStatus` outcomes per feature. Returns `{tenants, fuel: {status_counts}, finance: {status_counts}, statuses}`. The schedulers are self-healing: stale one-shot crons whose fire date already passed are replaced with fresh ones; confirmed-delivered tenants (`Tenant.welcomes_sent[feature]`) are short-circuited. Management command `backfill_welcomes` provides the same behavior on-demand with `--feature` and `--tenant` filters.
inventory_notes: The backstop exists because the original design had no recovery when both the live-toggle path and the deploy-time backfill failed (e.g. agent crashed mid-turn during the first welcome fire).
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As a system, I want to remove Heartbeat Check-in cron jobs from tenants that have heartbeat disabled so that disabled heartbeats stop firing and generating unnecessary turns.
expected_behaviour: `remove_zombie_heartbeats_task()`: iterates active tenants with `container_id__gt=''` and `heartbeat_enabled=False`. For each: calls `cron.list`; looks for a job named 'Heartbeat Check-in'; if found, calls `cron.remove`. Returns `{removed, skipped, errors}`. Sleeps 1s between tenants and 0.5s when no heartbeat found to avoid gateway rate limits. Management command `remove_zombie_heartbeats` exposes dry-run.
inventory_notes: The `sync_heartbeat_cron` function in services.py is the live-path sync (called from update_system_cron_prompts); this task and command are the sweep backstop. The two mechanisms are complementary: sync_heartbeat_cron fixes one tenant at a time on config refresh; remove_zombie_heartbeats sweeps the entire fleet.

user_story: As a system, I want to send a one-off agent-driven message to a single tenant so that operators can push proactive communications without going through the Telegram/LINE channels.
expected_behaviour: `broadcast_single_tenant_task(tenant_id, message)`: looks up tenant; returns if not found or no `container_fqdn`. Checks `has_entitlement` and `status == ACTIVE`; logs and returns if not entitled. Posts to `https://{container_fqdn}/v1/chat/completions` with the message as a user turn. Sets `Authorization: Bearer {gateway_token}` and `X-User-Timezone`. Timeout 120s. Logs success/timeout/error. Does not raise on failure (fire-and-forget for broadcast semantics).
inventory_notes: The container's agent handles the message and calls `nbhd_send_to_user` internally. There is no response returned to the caller — the container delivers to the user via their connected channel (Telegram, LINE, or iOS push). No retry on failure.

user_story: As a system, I want to delete delivered BufferedMessage rows older than 7 days so that the database does not accumulate unbounded historical delivery records.
expected_behaviour: `cleanup_delivered_buffers_task()`: deletes all `BufferedMessage` rows where `delivered=True` and `created_at < now - 7 days`. Returns `{deleted}`. Simple bulk delete with no tenant filtering — applies to all delivered rows fleet-wide.
