So a user still in onboarding who sends a voice message experiences: loading -> ~5-10s wait through full transcription -> loading again -> a rejection telling them voice isn't supported yet. User impact: a confusing >10s loading animation before an outright rejection, plus wasted Whisper API cost on a transcript that is discarded. The expected_behaviour documents the rejection text but not this wasteful ordering. Severity is low: it only affects voice-during-onboarding (a narrow window — first interaction or after re-introduction), still produces a correct rejection, and has no data/security impact.

feature: LINE markdown stripping and table conversion for plain-text fallback
user_story: As the system, I want to strip markdown formatting from AI responses when Flex rendering fails so that LINE subscribers receive readable plain text instead of raw markdown syntax.
expected_behaviour:
_strip_markdown removes code blocks (``` blocks), bold (**), italic (*), links ([label](url) → 'label: url'), inline code (`), heading markers (#). _convert_tables converts markdown tables (| col | col |) to key:value paragraph format: each data row becomes 'Header1: Value1
Header2: Value2

'. These two functions are used as a fallback path when build_flex_bubble throws an exception and as a pre-processing step before Flex rendering (tables and code blocks removed before classification). The 5000-char LINE text limit is enforced with _split_message (paragraph/line/word boundary splitting).
inventory_notes: Three fallback layers in relay_ai_response_to_line: Flex bubble → _strip_markdown plain text → emergency _strip_markdown on exception. Tables are converted in both Flex and plain text paths so the same preprocessing applies everywhere.

user_story: As a subscriber using Siri / App Intents, I want a deterministic, instant snapshot of my current state so that Siri can speak it aloud without waking my container.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/siri/status/ requires JWT authentication (IsAuthenticated). Returns 404 {"error": "no_tenant"} if the user has no tenant. On success, calls render_context_digest (same source as ChatContextView) capped at 4000 chars, PII-rehydrates real names from tenant.pii_entity_map (fail-open on rehydrate error), and returns {"snapshot_md": <markdown string>, "generated_at": <ISO timestamp>} with Cache-Control: no-store. No LLM call; no container wake; no per-tenant model budget consumed. Rate-limited at 60 requests/minute per user (SiriStatusMinuteThrottle).
inventory_notes: PII rehydrate failure is non-fatal and logged; the unrehydrated snapshot is still returned. The 4000-char cap is bounded by CONTEXT_DIGEST_MIN/MAX_CHARS from workspace_envelope.

feature: Siri Fast Responder with Escalation (Tier 2 / Tier 3)
user_story: As a subscriber using Siri, I want a spoken answer within a few seconds so that simple questions are answered instantly, and complex questions are routed to my full assistant.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/siri/respond/ requires JWT auth. Body: {"intent": "...", "client_msg_id": "<optional>"}. Validates: non-empty intent (400 empty_intent), intent <= 1000 chars (400 intent_too_long), client_msg_id <= 64 chars (400 invalid_client_msg_id). Builds a 4000-char PII-rehydrated snapshot, then calls chat_completion with platform OpenRouter ZDR key using models [deepseek-v4-flash, deepseek-v4-pro] at 8s timeout / 300 max tokens. If the fast model replies without the [[ESCALATE]] sentinel, returns {"answered": true, "text": "<spoken answer>"} with no persistence. If the model returns [[ESCALATE]] (leading, case-insensitive), is empty, or throws, escalates: creates AppChatMessage + PendingMessage via enqueue_tenant_turn (budget-gated via check_budget — over-budget turns get status=error/error=budget_exhausted and no container wake), returns {"answered": false, "escalated": true, "client_msg_id": "<id>", "turn": {...}}. Escalation is idempotent on client_msg_id. Rate-limited at 30/minute (SiriRespondMinuteThrottle). All responses have Cache-Control: no-store.
dependencies: apps/common/openrouter.chat_completion (OpenRouter ZDR key), apps/router/chat_views.enqueue_tenant_turn, apps/router/chat_views.check_budget, PostgreSQL (AppChatMessage, PendingMessage, ChatThread)
inventory_notes: Stray mid-reply mixed-case [[escalate]] sentinel (not at start) is stripped from the answer, not spoken. Model errors and transport failures all fall through to escalation rather than returning an error to the user. The fast model cost is platform-absorbed (not per-tenant quota). Sentinel trailing content (e.g. '[[ESCALATE]] needs calendar tool') still escalates.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As a subscriber on iOS, I want to register my device's APNs token so that the assistant can push me a notification when my reply is ready.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/push/register/ requires JWT auth. Body: {"device_token": "<hex>", "environment": "production|sandbox", "bundle_id": "<optional>"}. Validates token as hex 32–200 chars (400 invalid_token), rejects missing tenant (404 no_tenant). Upserts a DeviceToken row keyed on the token string (globally unique constraint): re-points the token to the registering user atomically via update_or_create, absorbing concurrent retries via the unique constraint (the same token can be re-registered to a new user on account switch/device handoff without a delete+create race). Unknown environment values are silently normalized to 'production'. Returns {"registered": true} 200. No rate limit.
inventory_notes: Token is globally unique: registering re-points the token to the new user — a prior user's row is overwritten, preventing cross-user push. No explicit 201/200 distinction: all successful registrations return 200.

user_story: As a subscriber on iOS, I want to unregister my APNs device token on logout or app uninstall so that I stop receiving push notifications for that device.
expected_behaviour: DELETE /api/v1/push/register/ requires JWT auth. Token is read from query param ?device_token= first; if absent, from request body. Returns 400 invalid_token if no token provided. Deletes DeviceToken rows matching (user=request.user, token=<token>), so the caller can only delete their own tokens. Returns 204 No Content on success (even if no row existed — silent no-op).
inventory_notes: The query-param fallback handles clients whose HTTP stack sends DELETE with no body. Scoped to request.user: cannot delete another user's token.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send a test push notification to my own devices so I can verify end-to-end APNs delivery is working.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/push/test/ requires JWT auth. If APNs is not configured (missing APNS_AUTH_KEY/KEY_ID/TEAM_ID/BUNDLE_ID), returns {"sent": 0, "failed": 0, "skipped": "not_configured"} 200. If user has no registered tokens, returns {"skipped": "no_tokens"} 200. Otherwise, fans out to all registered tokens grouped by environment (sandbox vs production), sending the fixed static body "Test push — notifications are working." (no user data in payload). APNs tokens reported as unregistered (410 / BadDeviceToken) are pruned from DeviceToken. Returns {"sent": N, "failed": N, "unregistered": N} 200. Rate-limited at 10/minute per user (PushTestMinuteThrottle). Never returns user PII or token values in the response.
dependencies: apps/common/apns.send_push, apps/common/apns.apns_configured, PostgreSQL (DeviceToken), Apple APNs HTTP/2 API, httpx[http2] package
inventory_notes: If the h2 package is not installed, send_push returns skipped=http2_unavailable. The test push cannot be directed at any specific device — it always targets all devices registered to the JWT user, so it cannot push to another user's device.

feature: APNs Reply-Ready Push (iOS Chat Turn Completion)
user_story: As the system, I want to push a notification to the user's iOS device when a chat turn completes so that the user is notified without reopening the app.
expected_behaviour: Called internally by apps/router/pending_queue.py after _drain_ios_batch completes a turn. notify_app_reply_ready(tenant, client_msg_ids, reply_text) claims notified_at on AppChatMessage rows (atomic isnull→now UPDATE) — only the first claim pushes, re-drains are no-ops. Strips markdown to a 140-char plain-text preview (or falls back to generic body for tables/empty replies). Routes to each device grouped by environment, sends with collapse_id=client_msg_id and content_available=True (hybrid alert + background wake). APNs 410/BadDeviceToken tokens are pruned. notify_app_reply_error sends a fixed error body with no diagnostic reason. Both are fail-open — any exception is logged, not re-raised. Short-circuits if APNs is not configured.
dependencies: apps/common/apns.send_push, PostgreSQL (AppChatMessage.notified_at, DeviceToken), Apple APNs HTTP/2 API
inventory_notes: Idempotency is dual-layer: server-side atomic notified_at claim prevents double-push on QStash retry; device-side apns-collapse-id coalesces multiple pushes for the same turn. content_available=1 triggers background fetch which iOS may throttle or suppress on force-quit/Background App Refresh off.

user_story: As the system, I want to push a notification to the user's iOS device when a cron job or proactive send completes so that iOS-only users are notified without Telegram/LINE.
expected_behaviour: Called by apps/router/proactive_context.py after recording a ProactiveOutbound row. notify_proactive_ready(tenant, proactive_id, body_source) claims ProactiveOutbound.notified_at atomically (isnull→now UPDATE); only the first claim pushes. Resolves the recipient from tenant.user (no User FK on ProactiveOutbound). Finds the tenant's main ChatThread id to set thread-id in the push payload. Sends with collapse_id='cron:<proactive_id>', content_available=True, extra={"id": "cron:<id>", "source": "cron"}. Routes per device environment, prunes stale tokens. Fail-open: any exception is logged but not re-raised. Short-circuits if APNs is not configured or proactive_id is falsy.
dependencies: apps/common/apns.send_push, PostgreSQL (ProactiveOutbound.notified_at, DeviceToken, ChatThread), Apple APNs HTTP/2 API
inventory_notes: The push body is a glanceable PII-safe taste; the authoritative text reaches the app via GET /chat/messages/?since= feed (the cron:<id> row). The 'app' channel on ProactiveOutbound covers iOS-only users who have no Telegram/LINE chat_id.

feature: APNs Provider JWT Signing and Caching
user_story: As the system, I want to mint and cache a short-lived ES256 provider JWT for APNs so that push requests are authenticated without re-signing on every send.
expected_behaviour: apps/common/apns._provider_jwt() signs a JWT with APNS_TEAM_ID as issuer using APNS_AUTH_KEY (ES256 private key, .p8 format) and caches it module-level for up to 3000 seconds (50 minutes, inside Apple's 1h validity window). On cache miss or expiry, re-signs via PyJWT. send_push() calls this before every request batch; if signing fails, returns {"skipped": "jwt_error"} without raising. send_push() also requires httpx[http2] — without the h2 package, returns {"skipped": "http2_unavailable"}. APNs is fully gated behind apns_configured() which checks all four env vars.
dependencies: PyJWT library, httpx[http2] library, Apple APNs API (api.push.apple.com / api.sandbox.push.apple.com), settings: APNS_AUTH_KEY, APNS_KEY_ID, APNS_TEAM_ID, APNS_BUNDLE_ID, APNS_USE_SANDBOX
inventory_notes: Module-level JWT cache (_jwt_cache) is process-local — multiple container replicas each maintain their own cache (not shared via Redis). APNS_USE_SANDBOX global controls sandbox fallback when per-device environment not explicitly passed. Token truncated to 64 bytes for apns-collapse-id per Apple spec.

feature: Core AI On-Device Model Manifest Endpoint
user_story: As an iOS subscriber, I want to fetch the on-device model manifest so my app can download and verify the correct model bundle files.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/coreai/model/manifest/ requires JWT auth (IsAuthenticated). Returns 404 {"detail": "No on-device model is configured."} if settings.COREAI_MODEL_BASE_URL or settings.COREAI_MODEL_MANIFEST_PATH are unset, or if the manifest file does not exist at the path. Returns 503 {"detail": "Model manifest unavailable."} if the manifest file is present but cannot be parsed (OSError, ValueError, KeyError, TypeError) — fail-closed so a corrupt manifest is never served to clients that verify sha256. On success returns {"name", "version", "total_bytes", "files": [{"path", "url", "sha256", "bytes"}, ...]} where each URL is constructed as <COREAI_MODEL_BASE_URL>/<name>/<version>/<percent-encoded-path>. total_bytes is taken from the manifest if present, otherwise summed from file entries.
dependencies: settings.COREAI_MODEL_BASE_URL (Azure Blob / CDN), settings.COREAI_MODEL_MANIFEST_PATH (local filesystem path to JSON), local filesystem
inventory_notes: The manifest JSON is read from disk on every request (no caching). File URLs use per-segment URL encoding (quote() per slash-separated part), not whole-path encoding. The 404 path allows the iOS app to gracefully fall back to Apple's on-device model (iOS 26) without user-facing error.

feature: Core AI Manifest Generation Management Command
user_story: As an operator, I want to generate a manifest JSON from an exported CoreML LanguageBundle directory so that the manifest endpoint serves the correct file list with verified sha256 checksums.
expected_behaviour: python manage.py generate_coreai_manifest --bundle <dir> --name <name> --model-version <ver> --out <path>. Walks the bundle directory recursively, skipping .DS_Store files. For each file computes streaming sha256 (1MB chunks) and byte size. Writes JSON {"name", "version", "total_bytes", "files": [{"path", "sha256", "bytes"}]} to the --out path (creates parent dirs). Exits with CommandError if the bundle dir is missing or contains no files. On success prints a message with file count, total GB, and the expected upload path pattern. --version is reserved by BaseCommand; the model version arg is --model-version.
inventory_notes: Files are sorted by rglob before hashing, giving a deterministic ordering. The operator must separately upload the bundle files to <COREAI_MODEL_BASE_URL>/<name>/<model_version>/<path> (e.g. Azure Blob). --version flag name collision with BaseCommand is the reason --model-version is used.

feature: Chat Turn Progress Narration (Internal Runtime Callback)
user_story: As the OpenClaw container runtime, I want to report coarse activity phases (waking/thinking/tool/composing) to the control plane so the iOS polling client can show live progress instead of a spinner.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/internal/runtime/<tenant_id>/chat/progress/ — no DRF auth classes; auth via X-NBHD-Internal-Key + X-NBHD-Tenant-Id headers validated by validate_internal_runtime_request (returns 401 internal_auth_failed on mismatch). Body: {"client_msg_id": "<optional>", "phase": "<required, max 24 chars>", "detail": "<optional, max 200 chars>"}. Returns 400 missing_fields if phase is absent. If client_msg_id is provided, updates that specific PENDING AppChatMessage. If omitted, updates the tenant's most-recently-created PENDING turn (the in-flight one). Finished (non-PENDING) turns are never mutated — returns {"updated": false} as a no-op. Unknown or absent turns also return 200 {"updated": false}. Fields phase and phase_detail on AppChatMessage are exposed on the polling endpoint GET /api/v1/chat/messages/<client_msg_id>/.
inventory_notes: Telegram/LINE turns do not create AppChatMessage rows, so a runtime hook firing during a Telegram turn has no row to update and silently no-ops. The per-tenant turn serialization (one container per tenant) is what makes 'most recent PENDING = in-flight' safe — there can only be one in-flight app turn at a time.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want my APNs token stored server-side and automatically pruned when it expires so that push notifications reliably reach my current device.
expected_behaviour: DeviceToken (db_table: device_tokens) stores one row per physical device install. Fields: token (globally unique, max 200 chars, hex), environment (sandbox|production), bundle_id, user FK, tenant FK, created_at, last_seen_at (auto-updated). Token uniqueness is enforced at DB level (UniqueConstraint on token). Self-healing: any push path (notify_app_reply_ready, notify_app_reply_error, notify_proactive_ready, PushTestView) collects APNs 410 / BadDeviceToken responses and deletes matching DeviceToken rows, so stale tokens from reinstall or token rotation are pruned automatically without a janitor cron.
inventory_notes: A sandbox and production token can coexist for one user (different environments). last_seen_at uses auto_now=True (updated on every save, including upsert). The global unique constraint on token means a device re-registration re-points the row to the new user atomically.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want messages I send to the shared Telegram bot to be routed to my personal OpenClaw container so that I receive AI responses in Telegram.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/telegram/webhook/ verifies the X-Telegram-Bot-Api-Secret-Token header via HMAC constant-time compare; returns 403 on mismatch, 503 if secret unconfigured. Sets RLS service-role context for DB access. Claims update_id via ProcessedInboundEvent (get_or_create) to deduplicate Telegram retries — returns 200 'ok' immediately on duplicate. Dispatches /start, callback_query, rate-limit, tenant-lookup, status, and budget checks in order before forwarding. On success forwards the decorated update to the tenant's OpenClaw container FQDN at /telegram-webhook with a 30s (or 120s for reasoning models) timeout, records usage, captures the conversation turn, and returns the container's JSON response. On timeout returns 200 'ok' silently (agent replies async via bot token). Returns 400 on malformed JSON.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API, OpenClaw container, Supabase/Postgres (ProcessedInboundEvent, Tenant/User lookup, UsageRecord), apps/billing/services:check_budget, apps/router/inbound_dedup:claim_inbound_event
inventory_notes: Webhook mode is latent in production — the platform runs single-revision with the long-poll poller instead. Webhook path is covered by all-channels rule and kept current. The container response's JSON is returned verbatim to Telegram as a 200+JSON body, which Telegram executes as a Bot API method call (sendMessage etc.).

user_story: As the system, I want a single long-polling process to receive Telegram updates for all tenants so that single-revision Container Apps deployment avoids 409 webhook conflicts.
expected_behaviour: Management command `python manage.py poll_telegram` starts TelegramPoller.start(), which calls deleteWebhook (drop_pending_updates=False) then enters a long-poll loop using getUpdates with timeout=30s, allowed_updates=[message, callback_query, edited_message]. For each update, sets RLS service-role context, claims update_id via claim_inbound_event (idempotency), then calls _handle_update for routing. Offset is advanced only AFTER successful handling or a logged poison-update, not before, so a DB error (e.g. Supabase pool exhaustion in set_rls_context) doesn't ack-and-drop the update. Catches SIGINT/SIGTERM for graceful stop. Calls close_old_connections() after every poll cycle to avoid pinning a Supavisor pool slot.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (getUpdates, deleteWebhook), Supabase/Postgres (RLS context, DB connections), apps/router/inbound_dedup:claim_inbound_event
inventory_notes: Production mode: poller process runs as a separate Azure Container App revision pinned to single-revision mode. Backoff is exponential up to 60s on errors. TimeoutException during long-poll is normal and loops without backoff.

user_story: As the system, I want each Telegram update_id to be processed exactly once so that Telegram's at-least-once redelivery and poller restart replays don't produce duplicate AI replies.
expected_behaviour: claim_inbound_event(f'tg:{update_id}') performs a get_or_create on ProcessedInboundEvent.event_key (UNIQUE column). Returns True (process) if created, False (skip) if already exists. On IntegrityError (concurrent race) returns False. On any other exception (DB down) returns True — fail-open so real messages are never dropped. A 1% probability prune pass deletes up to 500 rows older than 3 days to prevent table growth. Used in both the webhook and the poller path before any user-visible side effect.
inventory_notes: Retention window is 3 days — comfortably longer than Telegram's redelivery window and poller restart replay horizon. Self-pruning is probabilistic (1%) so no separate cron is needed.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send /start TOKEN to the Telegram bot so that my Telegram account is linked to my NBHD subscriber account.
expected_behaviour: Detected at the top of both the webhook (handle_start_command) and the poller (_handle_update) before any routing. Parses /start <token> from the message text. Extracts telegram_user_id, telegram_chat_id, telegram_username, telegram_first_name from the update. Delegates to apps/tenants/telegram_service:process_start_token which validates the token and links the User row. Returns a JSON sendMessage response on success ('Your account is now linked!') or failure. On success, invalidates the in-memory route cache for chat_id so subsequent messages are routed immediately. No auth required — the token is the credential.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (User model update, Tenant lookup), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage response)
inventory_notes: handle_start_command returns None for non-/start messages; callers check for None before routing. Cache invalidation is in-process only; a multi-process deployment would require a shared cache.

user_story: As the system, I want to rate-limit each Telegram chat_id to 30 messages per minute so that individual users cannot flood the system.
expected_behaviour: is_rate_limited(chat_id) maintains an in-memory deque per chat_id. Drops events older than 60s from the front, then checks if the deque length >= ROUTER_RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE (default 30). Returns True (rate limited) before appending the new event. If limit <= 0, always returns False. In webhook: returns HTTP 429 'Too many requests'. In poller: logs warning and returns (no response sent). Rate-limit state is in-process memory only.
inventory_notes: ROUTER_RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE setting from Django settings. In-memory only — resets on process restart, not shared across workers.

user_story: As an anonymous Telegram user, I want to receive a sign-up link when I message the bot so that I know how to subscribe.
expected_behaviour: When resolve_tenant_by_chat_id returns None (no User with matching telegram_chat_id or no ACTIVE tenant), both the webhook and poller call send_onboarding_link(chat_id) which builds a sendMessage response pointing to the FRONTEND_URL. Webhook returns it as JsonResponse; poller calls _execute_telegram_response. Message: 'I don't recognize your account yet. Sign up at {frontend_url}...'

user_story: As a subscriber whose container is still provisioning, I want to receive a status message so that I know my assistant is not yet ready.
expected_behaviour: Both webhook and poller check tenant.status in (PENDING, PROVISIONING) after tenant lookup. Returns a localized sendMessage response (error_msg(lang, 'waking_up')) telling the user to try again in a minute. No forwarding to OpenClaw occurs. Lang is taken from tenant.user.language or 'en'.
error_detail: Confirmed localization gap. In the Telegram poller path (apps/router/poller.py:846-853), the PENDING/PROVISIONING branch sends a hardcoded English string ("Your assistant is waking up! ... send your message again in a moment!") via self._send_message, instead of error_msg(lang, "waking_up"). The webhook path (apps/router/views.py:246-255) correctly localizes the identical scenario using lang = tenant.user.language or "en" and error_msg(lang, "waking_up"). The poller already imports error_msg (poller.py:27) and uses it with tenant.user.language for many nearby messages, including provisioning-related ones (e.g. poller.py:1257-1259: lang = getattr(tenant.user, "language", None) or "en"; error_msg(lang, "telegram_provisioning_almost_ready")). The "waking_up" key has real non-English translations in error_messages.py (Japanese at lines 130-134), so the localized output genuinely differs from English. User impact: a non-English-speaking subscriber whose tenant is still provisioning, messaging via the polling (single-revision Telegram) path, receives this transient (~1 minute) message in English regardless of language preference; the next post-wake message is localized, so impact is cosmetic and short-lived — hence low severity. The adjacent SUSPENDED branch (poller.py:855-865) shares the same hardcoded-English pattern but is outside this finding's scope.

user_story: As a suspended subscriber (payment lapsed, no active subscription), I want to receive a message explaining my account is paused and pointing to billing so I can reactivate.
expected_behaviour: Both webhook and poller check tenant.status == SUSPENDED and not tenant.is_trial and not bool(tenant.stripe_subscription_id). Returns localized error_msg(lang, 'suspended', billing_url=...) pointing to {FRONTEND_URL}/settings/billing. Suspended tenants who still have a stripe_subscription_id or are on trial are not blocked here (trial quota handling takes precedence).
error_detail: Confirmed real localization inconsistency. The webhook path (apps/router/views.py:258-270) renders the suspended-tenant message via error_msg(lang, "suspended", billing_url=...), which has translations for ~12 locales (en/ja/es/fr/de/pt/zh/ko/it/nl/ru/ar). The poller path (apps/router/poller.py:857-865) instead sends a hardcoded English literal for the identical SUSPENDED + not-trial + no-subscription state. The poller is a live production path (run via the poll_telegram management command; Telegram delivery is poller-based per the single-revision setup). It is also an internal inconsistency, not just a missing feature: the same poller file already imports error_msg (line 27) and already localizes other unavailable-state messages with the tenant's language (telegram_provisioning_almost_ready / telegram_restarting / telegram_resend_after_failed_wait at lines 1257-1275, using getattr(tenant.user, 'language', None) or 'en'). Impact: a non-English subscriber whose account is paused (trial ended or payment lapsed with no active Stripe subscription) sees the pause/billing explanation in English instead of their language. Low severity — the message still contains the working billing URL and the user can recover; only the copy is un-localized for this one branch.

feature: Budget check and container hibernation on quota exhaustion
user_story: As the system, I want to block messages and hibernate the container when a tenant's monthly budget is exhausted so that costs do not overrun.
expected_behaviour: Both webhook and poller call check_budget(tenant) before waking/forwarding. If budget_reason is non-empty, _hibernate_for_quota(tenant) is called: if container is not already hibernated, calls azure_client.hibernate_container_app(container_id), sets Tenant.hibernated_at=now, and calls send_cost_exhausted_email (idempotent via cost_exhausted_email_sent_at). Then _build_budget_exhausted_message / _send_budget_exhausted is called, returning a localized message. Global budget (reason='global') uses 'budget_unavailable' copy; per-tenant uses 'budget_exhausted_trial' or 'budget_exhausted_paid' with a billing URL. Budget-exempt tenants and tenants with purchased_credit skip hibernation via _handle_openrouter_credit_limit.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (Tenant.hibernated_at, estimated_cost_this_month, effective_cost_budget), Azure Container Apps (hibernate_container_app), Mailgun/Django email (send_cost_exhausted_email)
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As a subscriber approaching my monthly budget cap, I want to receive an email warning at 90% usage so that I am not surprised when chat stops.
expected_behaviour: send_cost_approaching_email(tenant) is called from billing reconcile cron or the OR credit-limit path when estimated_cost_this_month crosses 90% of effective_cost_budget. Idempotent: skips if cost_warn_sent_at is already set. Skips if no user.email, tenant is budget_exempt, or tenant is not ACTIVE/SUSPENDED. Renders cost_approaching_subject/body.txt/html templates. On success stamps cost_warn_sent_at. fire_threshold_emails_if_crossed(tenant, before, after) compares pre/post costs and fires the appropriate email only when the threshold is crossed upward in this update.
inventory_notes: Reset monthly via reset_monthly_counters so the chain re-arms each cycle.

feature: Hibernated tenant message buffering and wake
user_story: As a subscriber whose container is idle-hibernated, I want my message to be buffered and the container to be woken so that I receive a reply without having to resend.
expected_behaviour: handle_hibernated_message(tenant, 'telegram', update, msg_text) is called when tenant.hibernated_at is set. Checks for existing undelivered BufferedMessage rows: if the oldest is >5 minutes old, the prior wake stalled — forces a fresh wake and clears hibernated_at defensively. Buffers the current message as a BufferedMessage row. On first-ever queued message: calls wake_hibernated_tenant(tenant), returns ACK_FRESH. On additional message during in-flight wake: returns SILENT. On stall recovery: returns ACK_RECONNECT. Caller maps ACK_FRESH→'hibernation_waking' message, ACK_RECONNECT→'hibernation_reconnecting' message, SILENT→HTTP 200 'ok'. Used only in the webhook path; the poller path goes straight to PendingMessage queue and handles hibernation in the drain task.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (BufferedMessage, Tenant.hibernated_at), Azure Container Apps (wake/activate revision via orchestrator)
inventory_notes: Buffered messages are flushed by deliver_buffered_messages_task (orchestrator/hibernation.py) after wake. The drain-task path (PendingMessage) has its own parallel hibernation-on-404 handling.

user_story: As the system, I want rapid-fire messages from the same Telegram chat to be serialized into the OpenClaw session so that the claude-cli backend does not reject concurrent turns.
expected_behaviour: enqueue_message_for_tenant creates a PendingMessage row (tenant, channel='telegram', channel_user_id=str(chat_id), payload, user_text_excerpt), stamps first_message_at on the tenant's first-ever inbound, and publishes a QStash drain_pending_messages_for_tenant task with retries=3. drain_pending_messages_for_tenant_task uses SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED with a soft delivery_in_flight_until lease to claim the oldest batch per (tenant, channel, channel_user_id) key. Singles and coalesced cold-start batches (voice always singleton) are POST'd to the container as one /v1/chat/completions turn. On success, marks rows DELIVERED, clears stale hibernated_at if live gateway response received. On hibernated 404, calls wake_hibernated_tenant and defers drain by _WAKE_DEFER_SECONDS=20s without burning attempt counter. On boot-grace 404 (within 240s of last_wake_at), releases lease and defers without burning attempt counter. On failure increments delivery_attempts; at _MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3 marks FAILED and sends apology. Stale rows older than 600s are dropped with apology instead of delivered.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (PendingMessage model, router migrations 0004/0009), QStash (drain task publish/retry), OpenClaw container (/v1/chat/completions), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage for apology/relay), apps/billing/services:record_usage
inventory_notes: The reaper task (reap_stuck_inbound_messages_task) runs every ~60s via QStash cron and republishes drain tasks for rows stuck PENDING >90s with no live lease — covers publish failures, DLQ, and worker-kill scenarios. Coalesced batches use format_coalesced_user_content to build a single prompt from multiple user_text rows.

feature: Telegram message forwarding to OpenClaw (poller path)
user_story: As a subscriber, I want messages I send on Telegram to be forwarded to my OpenClaw container and the AI response to be delivered back to me.
expected_behaviour: _forward_to_container in the poller decorates message_text with proactive_context block + datetime marker + chat context marker, then calls enqueue_message_for_tenant (PendingMessage queue). The queue drain task POSTs to https://{container_fqdn}/v1/chat/completions with Authorization: Bearer {gateway_token}, X-Telegram-Chat-Id, X-Channel: telegram, X-User-Timezone headers. On a healthy response, relay_ai_response_to_telegram rehydrates PII, strips/records insights markers, renders [[chart:]] markers into images uploaded to Azure File Share, sends MEDIA: images via sendPhoto, and delivers the markdown response via _send_telegram_markdown (render_telegram_html → parse_mode=HTML with plain-text fallback on 400). Returns True on success. Records token usage via _record_usage_safe.
dependencies: OpenClaw container (/v1/chat/completions), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage parse_mode=HTML, sendPhoto), Azure File Share (chart PNG upload), apps/pii/redactor:rehydrate_text, apps/insights/markers:extract_and_record_insights, apps/router/charts:render_chart
inventory_notes: One typing indicator sent before the POST. Reasoning/BYO Claude models get REASONING_MODEL_TIMEOUT (240s) instead of DEFAULT_CHAT_TIMEOUT (120s). OpenRouter credit-limit (402 or credit-limit string in body) trips the budget circuit breaker and hibernates the container.
error_detail: Confirmed real. Production Telegram runs the central long-poller, not the webhook: startup.sh (lines 14, 61) launches the poll_telegram management command, and TelegramPoller.start() deletes any registered webhook on startup (poller.py:70-71), so the webhook view receives no live Telegram traffic. The poller subscribes to callback_query updates (allowed_updates at poller.py:154) and routes them in _handle_update's callback block (poller.py:726-837), which has explicit prefix cases for tz_, lesson:, extract:, gate_approve:/gate_deny:, container_update:, and agent: but NO case for task_action:. The nightly extraction summary sends Remove/Undo buttons with callback_data task_action:undo:ID (extraction.py:353). When a Telegram subscriber taps that button, the callback matches none of the poller's prefixes, falls through the callback block unhandled, is never answered (no answerCallbackQuery), and the undo is never performed (undo_task_action never called). The working handler handle_task_action_callback (task_action_callbacks.py:69-111) is wired only into the webhook view (views.py:234-237) and the LINE webhook (line_webhook.py:1301), both bypassed by the Telegram production path. User impact: every Telegram user who taps the morning reconciliation Undo button gets a spinning/no-op button and the reconciled change is not reverted. Recoverable (the user can ask the assistant in chat to revert), so degraded-feature rather than data loss, hence medium not high.

user_story: As the system, I want AI assistant markdown output to be converted to Telegram-safe HTML so that users see formatted text instead of raw markdown symbols.
expected_behaviour: render_telegram_html(text) parses CommonMark/GFM into blocks, renders each to Telegram HTML: headings→<b>, bold/italic/strikethrough→<b>/<i>/<s>, inline code→<code>, fenced blocks→<pre><code class="language-X">, links→<a href>, blockquotes→<blockquote>, lists→bullet/numbered/task-checkbox items, GFM tables→aligned <pre> monospace grid, --- rules→──────────. All literal text is HTML-escaped before tag insertion. Every block is validated by _is_valid_html against the allowed tag set; invalid blocks degrade to escaped plaintext. Output is split into ≤4096-char chunks on block boundaries. _send_telegram_markdown sends each chunk with parse_mode=HTML; on 400 rejection falls back to strip_telegram_html (tag-strip). Returns [] for empty input.
inventory_notes: Stdlib-only, no external dependencies. NUL bytes are stripped from input to prevent placeholder collision. The _is_valid_html validator checks tag balance and that only allowed tags appear; malformed content degrades to escaped plaintext, never raw markdown.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send photos to the bot and have them forwarded to my AI assistant so that the assistant can see and respond to images I share.
expected_behaviour: In the poller's _handle_update, if message has photo field, _upload_photo_to_workspace downloads the largest photo (max 5MB) from Telegram getFile, re-encodes as base64, stores to workspace/media/inbound/{hash}.{ext} on the tenant's Azure File Share via upload_workspace_file_binary, and returns the container-local path /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/media/inbound/.... The message_text is then prepended with [Photo attached: {path}]. On outbound, _send_photo (poller) and _send_telegram_photo (drain) download a workspace image from Azure File Share and call Telegram sendPhoto with multipart data. Captions are rendered via markdown_to_plaintext (max 1024 chars).
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (getFile, sendPhoto), Azure File Share (upload_workspace_file_binary, ShareFileClient)
inventory_notes: Photos >5MB are silently skipped (no download). The container-local path is what OpenClaw sees; the workspace mount maps it to the file share.
error_detail: Confirmed. When a Telegram photo's largest size exceeds 5 MB, _download_photo logs a warning and returns None (poller.py:531-533), so _upload_photo_to_workspace returns None (poller.py:570-571) and image_path stays None in the forward path (poller.py:939-943). The "if image_path:" guard is False (poller.py:946-948), so no "[Photo attached: ...]" prefix is added. Separately, _extract_message_text builds message_text as the caption or the literal "User sent a photo" (poller.py:1097-1099). Net result: the message is forwarded to the assistant with only that text, NO image, and NO explanation that the photo was dropped. The user gets a confident AI reply that appears to address a photo the assistant never saw. This is inconsistent with sibling media handlers in the same function: oversized documents get an explicit "too large to process" marker injected into the text (FA-1025), and failed voice transcription yields "[Voice message couldn't transcribe, please try sending as text]" (FA-1024). Only the photo path silently degrades. Severity is low: it triggers only above 5 MB (Telegram compresses typical inline photos, so it is not the common case), it is degraded behavior not a crash, and no data is lost, but the silent blind reply is real and user-visible.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send voice messages to the bot and have them transcribed to text so that the AI assistant can understand and respond to spoken input.
expected_behaviour: In the poller's _extract_message_text, if message has voice or audio field, _transcribe_voice(file_id) is called. Downloads the audio via Telegram getFile + file download URL, then POSTs to OpenAI Whisper API (https://api.openai.com/v1/audio/transcriptions, model=whisper-1) with OPENAI_API_KEY. Returns transcribed text or None on failure. On success, message_text becomes '🎤 Voice message: "{transcript}"'. On failure: '[Voice message — couldn't transcribe, please try sending as text]'. Typing indicator is sent before transcription begins.
dependencies: OpenAI Whisper API (api.openai.com/v1/audio/transcriptions), Telegram Bot API (getFile, file download), OPENAI_API_KEY setting
inventory_notes: Returns None if OPENAI_API_KEY is not configured. File extension is inferred from the Telegram file_path.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to send text documents to the bot and have their content forwarded to my AI assistant so that it can process documents I share.
inventory_notes: No upload to file share — content is injected inline into the message text. Max content 10,000 chars.

feature: Sticker, video, location, contact message handling
user_story: As a subscriber, I want non-text Telegram messages (stickers, videos, locations, contacts) to be described to my AI assistant so that it can acknowledge and respond to them.
expected_behaviour: In _extract_message_text: sticker → '[User sent a sticker {emoji}]'; video/video_note → '[User sent a video ({duration}s, {size} MB)]' + optional caption; location (no venue) → '📍 User shared their location: {lat}, {lng} https://maps.google.com/maps?q={lat},{lng}'; venue → '📍 User shared a venue: {name} — {addr} ({lat},{lng}) {maps_url}'; contact → '📇 User shared a contact: {name} ({phone})'. Returns None for update types with no extractable text.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want forwarded Telegram messages to include the source attribution so my AI assistant knows the content was forwarded.
expected_behaviour:
In _extract_message_text, if message has forward_from or forward_from_chat: extracts sender first_name or chat title, prepends '[Forwarded from {name}]
' to the message text or caption. Reply context prefix (if replying to a bot message, '[Replying to: "{truncated}"]

') is also prepended.
inventory_notes: Reply context is only injected for replies to bot messages (reply_from.is_bot == True), not user-to-user replies.

user_story: As the system, I want every user message to be prefixed with the current local time so that the AI assistant always knows 'now' without needing to call a tool.
expected_behaviour: build_datetime_context(user_timezone) builds '[Now: 2026-04-21 06:47 JST (Monday)]\n' using the tenant's IANA timezone string (fallback UTC). Injected into the message text before forwarding in both the webhook (msg['text'] = proactive_block + datetime + chat_marker + msg['text']) and the poller (_forward_to_container: message_text = proactive_block + datetime + chat_marker + message_text). The chat context marker '[chat: user is mid-conversation, reply concisely...]' signals the agent to skip the heavy AGENTS.md session-start context-load.
inventory_notes: Coalesced batches use build_coalesced_chat_marker instead, which tells the agent to treat N messages as one combined request.

user_story: As the system, I want recent proactive/cron outbound messages to be surfaced as context when the user replies so that the AI assistant can thread the reply back to the original proactive message.
expected_behaviour: surface_proactive_context(tenant, channel='telegram', channel_user_id=str(chat_id)) queries ProactiveOutbound for the last 3 rows within 24h matching tenant+channel+channel_user_id. Filters: returns unconsumed rows, plus consumed rows whose consumed_at is within 5 minutes (follow-up window). Marks first-time-surfaced rows consumed_at=now in an atomic transaction. Renders rows as '[earlier-from-you {datetime} job={name}:\n{message}\n]' blocks, ordered oldest-first. If any row has parsed_items (list items extracted from markdown), prepends a [thread-rule:...] guidance for index-based reply mapping. Returns empty string if no rows. Block is prepended before datetime marker.
inventory_notes: record_proactive_outbound is called from CronDeliveryView after a successful push. It also dispatches an iOS APNs push via _dispatch_ios_push off the request path.

user_story: As a subscriber returning after 30+ minutes, I want my AI assistant's first message to be enriched with my active goals, tasks, and relevant history so that it can provide context-aware responses.
expected_behaviour:
In the poller, _is_new_session returns True if tenant.last_message_at is >30 minutes ago. Then _build_session_context_inner is called. Step 1: renders render_goals(tenant, max_chars=1500) and render_open_tasks(tenant) from journal envelope. Step 2: generate_embedding(message_text[:500]) via apps/lessons/services, then cosine-similarity search DocumentChunk (≥0.65 similarity, max 2) and Lesson (status='approved', ≥0.65, max 1 additional). Context is prepended as '[Context for this conversation:]

## Your active goals
...
## Your current tasks
...
## Relevant history
...'

[Now respond to the user's message:]

'. Fails open — returns original message_text on any error.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (DocumentChunk with pgvector CosineDistance, Lesson model), apps/lessons/services:generate_embedding, apps/journal/envelope:render_goals/render_open_tasks
inventory_notes: SESSION_GAP_SECONDS = 30 minutes. Embedding search is skipped (best-effort) if generate_embedding fails. Only active in the poller (long-poll) path, not the webhook path.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to see a typing indicator in Telegram while my AI assistant processes my message so that I know it received my input.
expected_behaviour: _send_typing(chat_id) in the poller POSTs sendChatAction with action='typing' to the Telegram API with a 5s timeout. Called before forwarding a message, before photo uploads, and during voice transcription. _send_telegram_typing_safe(chat_id) in the drain task does the same, fail-open (never raises). Typing indicator auto-clears after ~5s; no repeating heartbeat is currently implemented (was removed when forwarding moved to the queue).
inventory_notes: No repeating heartbeat during long BYO Claude turns — a follow-up improvement per code comment.

feature: Lesson approval via Telegram inline button
user_story: As a subscriber, I want to approve or dismiss AI-suggested lessons from a Telegram inline button so that approved lessons are embedded and added to my learning constellation.
expected_behaviour: callback_data starting with 'lesson:' is routed to handle_lesson_callback (both webhook and poller). Format: 'lesson:{approve|dismiss}:{lesson_id}'. Looks up Lesson with id=lesson_id, tenant=tenant, status='pending'. On 'approve': sets lesson.status='approved', lesson.approved_at=now, calls process_approved_lesson (embedding generation), and if approved_count ≥ 5 calls refresh_constellation. On 'dismiss': sets lesson.status='dismissed'. In both cases, calls editMessageText to update the original message (removes inline buttons), and returns answerCallbackQuery. Returns 'Invalid action' / 'Lesson not found' on bad data.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (Lesson model, apps/lessons/models), apps/lessons/services:process_approved_lesson, apps/lessons/clustering:refresh_constellation, Telegram Bot API (answerCallbackQuery, editMessageText), apps/pii/redactor:rehydrate_for_tenant
inventory_notes: PII placeholders in lesson.text are rehydrated before display in the edit message.

feature: Proactive extraction approval via Telegram inline button
user_story: As a subscriber, I want to approve or dismiss AI-extracted goals, tasks, and lessons from Telegram inline buttons so that they are saved to my journal.
expected_behaviour: callback_data starting with 'extract:' routed to handle_extraction_callback. Format: 'extract:{action}:{pending_id}'. Actions: approve_lesson (creates Lesson, status=approved, calls process_approved_lesson + refresh_constellation if ≥5 approved), approve_goal (creates typed Goal row OR appends to goals Document per experimental_typed_journal_lifecycle flag), approve_task (creates typed Task row OR appends to tasks Document), dismiss (marks DISMISSED), undo_lesson/undo_goal/undo_task (reverses approved item, marks UNDONE). All approve actions set PendingExtraction.status=APPROVED, resolved_at=now. Edits original message via editMessageText to strip buttons and show confirmation text. Returns answerCallbackQuery. Returns 'Already processed' if PendingExtraction is not in expected state.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (PendingExtraction, Lesson, Goal, Task, Document models), apps/lessons/services:process_approved_lesson, apps/lessons/clustering:refresh_constellation, Telegram Bot API (answerCallbackQuery, editMessageText)
inventory_notes: experimental_typed_journal_lifecycle flag controls typed Goal/Task rows vs legacy markdown Document. Undo stores lesson/goal/task FK on PendingExtraction for reversal.

feature: Task action undo via Telegram inline button
user_story: As a subscriber, I want to undo an AI-applied task or goal state change from a Telegram inline button so that I can correct automated reconciliation decisions.
expected_behaviour: callback_data starting with 'task_action:' routed to handle_task_action_callback. Format: 'task_action:undo:{action_id}'. Resolves PendingTaskAction by id+tenant. Returns 'Already undone' if status=UNDONE, 'Cannot undo' if not APPLIED. Calls undo_task_action(action), sets status=UNDONE, resolved_at=now. Edits original message via editMessageText to show '~{label}~ — undone', answers callback '✓ Undone!'. On failure sets status=FAILED and returns appropriate message.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (PendingTaskAction model, apps/journal/models), apps/journal/reconciliation:undo_task_action, Telegram Bot API (answerCallbackQuery, editMessageText)

feature: Action gate approve/deny via Telegram inline button
user_story: As a subscriber, I want to approve or deny AI-proposed destructive actions from Telegram inline buttons so that I maintain control over significant automated changes.
expected_behaviour: In the poller, callback_data starting with 'gate_approve:' or 'gate_deny:' is routed to _handle_gate_callback. Parses 'gate_{approve|deny}:{action_id}'. Resolves PendingAction by id+tenant. Returns 'Already {status}' if not PENDING. Checks action.is_expired: if expired, sets status=EXPIRED, creates ActionAuditLog, calls update_gate_message, answers '⏰ Expired'. On approve/deny: sets action.status=APPROVED/DENIED, responded_at=now, creates ActionAuditLog, calls update_gate_message. Answers '✅ Approved' or '❌ Denied'.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (PendingAction, ActionAuditLog, ActionStatus models, apps/actions/models), Telegram Bot API (answerCallbackQuery)
inventory_notes: Only handled in the poller, not the webhook view (webhook only handles lesson:, extract:, task_action: callbacks).

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to tap inline buttons generated by the AI assistant so that the button selection is forwarded to the agent as a natural message.
expected_behaviour: In the poller, callback_data starting with 'agent:' is handled. Strips 'agent:' prefix to get button_value. Answers the callback query with '✓'. Edits the original message's reply_markup to remove buttons. Calls _forward_to_container with '[User tapped button: "{button_value}"]' and raw_user_text=button_value. The agent receives the button tap as if the user typed the button label. Inline buttons in AI responses are parsed from [[button:Label|callback_data]] markers by _send_rich_response and sent via reply_markup.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (answerCallbackQuery, editMessageReplyMarkup, sendMessage with reply_markup)
inventory_notes: [[button:Label|data]] markers are parsed from the AI response text and sent as inline_keyboard. Button callback_data is prefixed with 'agent:' so the poller can distinguish from system callbacks.
error_detail: Confirmed for the Telegram channel. Every Telegram reply in production flows poller._forward_to_container (poller.py:1307-1380) -> enqueue_message_for_tenant -> queue drain task -> relay_ai_response_to_telegram (pending_queue.py:1543-1644). That relay handles PII rehydration, insight markers, chart rendering, and MEDIA image sending, but does NOT parse [[button:Label|callback_data]] markers; the comment at pending_queue.py:1615-1619 explicitly says 'no inline button parsing yet'. The poller's _send_rich_response (poller.py:317-431), which DOES parse buttons (lines 404-413, building agent:-prefixed callback_data + reply_markup), is dead in production: it is referenced only from tests (test_proxy_phase3.py:62-85). Git history confirms it was the pre-queue synchronous send path (commit bef0156 'Phase 3 proxy improvements - inline buttons'), orphaned when the per-tenant queue landed (commit e89e08a) and forwarding moved fully into the queue. As a result the agent: callback handler at poller.py:819-837 is unreachable on Telegram because no button is ever rendered to tap. User impact: when the agent emits a button marker on Telegram, the user sees literal [[button:Label|data]] text instead of a tappable inline keyboard, and the callback flow is unusable. Severity low (not medium): button markers are a LINE-spec feature (docs/line-phase2-spec.md:104) and the LINE drain path DOES honor them (line_webhook.py:714 extract_quick_reply_buttons); no system-prompt guidance promotes [[button:]] for Telegram, so the agent rarely emits them, limiting frequency. When it does, it is a genuine leaked-marker + dead-callback UX defect on Telegram.

user_story: As a new subscriber using Telegram, I want a guided onboarding flow that captures my name, language, and timezone so that the assistant is set up correctly for me from the start.
expected_behaviour: get_onboarding_response(tenant, message_text, telegram_lang) is called by the poller when tenant.onboarding_complete is False or onboarding_step==0. Step 0: sends localized welcome message, advances to step 1. Step 1: parses name from text, auto-detects language from Telegram language_code (if non-English), shows country inline keyboard buttons (tz_country:) or asks for language. Step 2: parses language from text, shows country keyboard. Step 3: processes country selection — single-TZ countries set timezone directly (step→4), multi-TZ countries show zone keyboard (tz_zone:) and set step=35. Step 35: parses timezone text fallback, advances to step 4. Step 4: captures interests, writes USER.md to workspace, sets onboarding_complete=True, step=5. Returns None on step 5+ to resume normal agent routing. needs_reintroduction returns True for backfilled users with ≥3 default-value profile fields, triggering re-intro flow.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (Tenant.onboarding_step, User.display_name/language/timezone/preferences), Azure File Share (USER.md write via upload_workspace_file), apps/router/timezone_data:build_country_keyboard/build_zone_keyboard/resolve_country_timezone
inventory_notes: Onboarding tz_country: and tz_zone: callbacks are handled before other callback routing in the poller. Language detection uses Telegram's language_code field and TELEGRAM_LANG_MAP; detects 30+ languages. Messages are localized for English, Japanese, Spanish with English fallback for other languages.

feature: Container update prompt and silent update via Telegram
user_story: As a subscriber, I want my OpenClaw container to be updated to the latest image either silently (if I've been idle 2+ hours) or with my consent so that I always have the latest features.
expected_behaviour: check_and_maybe_update(tenant) is called in the poller after onboarding check. If is_container_outdated (current_tag != latest_tag) and not on cooldown (1h in-memory cooldown): if idle ≥2 hours, calls update_container silently and returns {action: 'silent_update'} — poller spawns a background thread to delay 15s then forward the pending message. If user was recently active, returns {action: 'ask_user', text, reply_markup} — poller stores message_text in _pending_messages[chat_id] and sends the prompt with Yes/No inline buttons. container_update:yes callback: debounced (in-progress guard), edits original message to remove buttons, answers 'Updating now...', in background: calls update_container, waits up to 120s for container health, then forwards pending message with '[System: just updated...]' framing. container_update:no: clears pending message and forwards it to current container.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (Tenant.container_image_tag, container_id), Azure Container Apps (update_container_image via orchestrator/azure_client), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage, editMessageReplyMarkup, answerCallbackQuery), OPENCLAW_IMAGE_TAG setting
inventory_notes: is_container_outdated returns False when OPENCLAW_IMAGE_TAG=='latest' (CI not set). Cooldown is in-memory per tenant_id, resets on process restart. _wait_for_container_ready polls /health every 5s up to 120s.

feature: Container restart recovery with message retry
user_story: As the system, I want messages sent to a restarting container to be retried after the container becomes healthy so that users don't lose messages during restarts.
expected_behaviour: _handle_container_restart(chat_id, tenant, message_text) is called from the poller when a container returns a restart-indicating response. Sends a localized error_msg ('telegram_restarting') to the user. Spawns a background daemon thread that calls _wait_for_container_ready (polls /health every 5s up to 120s). On success, sends typing indicator and forwards message with '[System: assistant was restarting when this arrived. User's message:]\n{message_text}' framing. On timeout, sends 'resend_after_failed_wait' error message. Uses _update_in_progress set to prevent double-notification.
dependencies: OpenClaw container (/health endpoint), Telegram Bot API (sendMessage)

user_story: As the system, I want each user message and AI reply to be persisted in ConversationTurn so that isolated cron sessions can see what happened in chat today.
expected_behaviour: record_conversation_turn(tenant, channel, channel_user_id, user_text, reply_text) creates a ConversationTurn row. user_text is capped to 2000 chars, reply_text to 800 chars, local_date is the tenant's local date. clean_reply_for_capture rehydrates PII placeholders and strips [[chart:]], [[insight:]], [[button:]] markers and MEDIA: lines from the reply. On success, triggers schedule_user_md_refresh(tenant) which calls push_user_md with debounce_seconds=120 (via on_commit + background thread). Probabilistic prune (1%, batch 500) deletes rows older than 35 days. Called fail-open from the drain task chokepoints (_drain_telegram_batch) and the webhook path. iOS turns are stored in AppChatMessage instead (not ConversationTurn).
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (ConversationTurn model, router migration 0010), Azure File Share (USER.md push via push_user_md), apps/pii/redactor:rehydrate_text
inventory_notes: build_conversation_digest renders the USER.md 'Conversation so far' section combining ConversationTurn (Telegram/LINE) + AppChatMessage (iOS), max 6 today-turns + 3-day rollup. Retention 35 days.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want chart images referenced in AI responses to be rendered, uploaded to Azure storage, and delivered as Telegram photos so that I can see visual data from my assistant.
expected_behaviour: [[chart:type|param=val,...]] markers in AI response text are detected by chart_pattern regex in both _send_rich_response (poller) and relay_ai_response_to_telegram (drain). render_chart(chart_type, tenant, params) generates a PNG. On success, PNG is uploaded to workspace/charts/{type}_{uuid8}.png on the tenant's Azure File Share, and the marker is replaced with 'MEDIA:{container_path}'. MEDIA: and workspace image paths are then sent via sendPhoto. On render failure, marker is replaced with empty string (silently suppressed).
dependencies: Azure File Share (upload_workspace_file_binary), Telegram Bot API (sendPhoto)

user_story: As the LINE messaging platform, I want to fetch chart PNG images from a URL so that charts can be delivered as LINE image messages.
expected_behaviour: GET /chart/{tenant_id}/{filename} serves a PNG from workspace/charts/{filename} on the tenant's Azure File Share. Filename is validated against ^[\w-]+\.png$ to prevent path traversal. Returns 404 on invalid filename, Azure mock mode, or missing storage account. Fetches account key from Azure storage_accounts.list_keys, constructs ShareFileClient for ws-{tenant_id[:20]} share, downloads and streams with content_type=image/png and Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600. No authentication — security relies on unguessable UUID filenames.
inventory_notes: URL is registered in the main URL conf, not router/urls.py. Only used by LINE (Telegram uses sendPhoto from the worker directly).

feature: Meditation audio serving with Range support
user_story: As a subscriber using the web Core tab, I want to stream meditation audio files with Range support so that iOS AVPlayer can determine the true duration and stop cleanly at the end.
expected_behaviour: GET /meditation/{tenant_id}/{filename} serves mp3/ogg from workspace/meditations/{filename} on the tenant's Azure File Share. Filename validated against ^[\w-]+\.(mp3|ogg)$ for path traversal prevention. Content-Type is audio/mpeg for mp3, audio/ogg for ogg. _audio_range_response handles the Range header: serves HTTP 206 with Content-Range for valid byte ranges, 416 Range Not Satisfiable for invalid ranges, 200 with Accept-Ranges: bytes for full-body fallback. Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600. No authentication — security relies on unguessable UUID filenames.
inventory_notes: Range support was added specifically to fix AVPlayer's indefinite duration bug where the meditation appeared to loop forever. Suffix ranges (bytes=-N) are supported.

user_story: As the system, I want to detect when OpenRouter rejects a chat completion due to the tenant's sub-key spending limit and hibernate the container, so that overages are stopped immediately.
expected_behaviour: _looks_like_openrouter_credit_limit detects: HTTP 402, or 4xx/5xx bodies containing 'insufficient credit', 'credit limit', 'quota exceeded' (case-insensitive). _handle_openrouter_credit_limit is called from both _drain_telegram_batch and _drain_line_batch on detection. First refreshes tenant from DB. If is_budget_exempt or purchased_credit > 0: calls sync_or_key_limit to raise key ceiling (does NOT hibernate). Otherwise: bumps estimated_cost_this_month to effective cap, calls _hibernate_for_quota (hibernates container + sends cap-exhausted email), sends budget-exhausted message to Telegram via _send_telegram_markdown (or LINE). Returns False from drain helper so gateway_responded=False prevents stale hibernated_at clear.
dependencies: Supabase/Postgres (Tenant.purchased_credit, is_budget_exempt, estimated_cost_this_month, effective_cost_budget), Azure Container Apps (hibernate_container_app), apps/billing/credits:sync_or_key_limit, apps/router/billing_quota_handlers:send_cost_exhausted_email, Telegram Bot API (sendMessage)
inventory_notes: Budget-exempt tenants (canary, internal) must not be hibernated — the 2026-06-10 canary outage was caused by this missing check.

user_story: As the system, I want a periodic reaper to republish drain tasks for PendingMessage rows that have been stuck for >90s so that messages are never permanently lost due to QStash publish failures or DLQ.
expected_behaviour: reap_stuck_inbound_messages_task scans PendingMessage for rows with delivery_status=PENDING, created_at older than 90s (_REAPER_STUCK_AGE_SECONDS), and no live in-flight lease. Groups by distinct (tenant_id, channel, channel_user_id) keys (capped at 200 per tick). Republishes drain_pending_messages_for_tenant task via QStash for each key with retries=3. The drain task's SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED safely handles concurrent execution. Logs warning with counts only when stuck rows found. Returns {stuck_keys, republished, errors}.
inventory_notes: Three scenarios it covers: (1) publish_task raised at enqueue time, (2) QStash DLQ after all retries failed, (3) gunicorn worker killed mid-claim. Stale-message guard in the drain task prevents 7-hour-old messages from being delivered to OC.

user_story: As a subscriber whose message could not be delivered after 3 attempts or sat stuck for >10 minutes, I want to receive an apology message so that I know what happened and can resend.
expected_behaviour: _send_apology_for_dropped_pending_message is called when a PendingMessage reaches _MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3. _send_apology_for_stale_pending_message is called when the head row is older than _STALE_MESSAGE_AGE_SECONDS=600s. Both use localized error_msg templates (dropped_message/dropped_message_with_excerpt or stale_message/stale_message_with_excerpt). For Telegram, sends via httpx POST to sendMessage. user_text is sanitized by strip_internal_framing to remove [System:...]/[Now:...]/[chat:...] framing before excerpting (max 50 chars). Age is formatted as Nm or ~Nh. iOS turns instead flip AppChatMessage status to ERROR and fire notify_app_reply_error APNs push.
inventory_notes: Excerpt stripping via strip_internal_framing prevents users from seeing '[System: just updated. User's message:] ...' in apology messages.
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

feature: Wake-up ack for hibernated Telegram tenant (drain path)
user_story: As a subscriber whose container is woken from hibernation by the drain task, I want to receive a 'waking up' message immediately so that I know my message was received during the ~60s boot.
expected_behaviour: _notify_waking(tenant, channel, channel_user_id) is called by the drain task when it detects a hibernated-container 404 and initiates wake. For Telegram channel only, sends error_msg(lang, 'hibernation_waking') via httpx POST to sendMessage. Best-effort — never raises. Mirrors the webhook path's ACK_FRESH response. The _mark_ios_waking function stamps waking_at on AppChatMessage rows for iOS clients instead.
dependencies: Telegram Bot API (sendMessage), Supabase/Postgres (AppChatMessage for iOS)

feature: Token usage recording after Telegram turns
user_story: As the system, I want token usage to be recorded after every AI turn so that billing and quota enforcement reflect actual consumption.
expected_behaviour: In the webhook path: _record_usage_from_openclaw_result extracts usage from the OpenClaw response (checks usage.input_tokens/input/output_tokens/output, also nested under result.usage or result.result.usage). Calls record_usage(tenant, event_type='message', input_tokens, output_tokens, model_used). model_used is resolved via resolve_model_for_attribution(tenant, result). Logs USAGE_MISSING if no usage payload, USAGE_ZERO if payload has zero counts. In the drain task path: _record_usage_safe extracts prompt_tokens/input_tokens/completion_tokens/output_tokens, passes message_count=len(batch) so per-tenant message counters count user-perceived sends.
inventory_notes: Usage recording failures are caught and logged but never re-raised — billing failure must not wedge the queue or lose a delivered message.

user_story: As the system, I want tenant routing lookups to be cached in memory so that repeated DB queries per Telegram update are avoided.
expected_behaviour: resolve_container(chat_id) checks the in-process _route_cache dict. If entry exists and is <ROUTE_CACHE_TTL=60s old, returns cached FQDN. Otherwise queries User by telegram_chat_id, fetches ACTIVE Tenant with container_fqdn, caches and returns. Returns None if user/tenant not found. invalidate_cache(chat_id) removes entry. clear_cache() clears all. Used in historical webhook path; current poller path uses resolve_tenant_by_chat_id which does a DB query on each update (cache not used for the full Tenant object).
inventory_notes: In-process only — not shared between workers or poller instances.

user_story: As the system, I want the HTTP timeout when forwarding to OpenClaw to be extended for reasoning and BYO-Claude models so that long inference turns are not prematurely aborted.
expected_behaviour: get_forwarding_timeout(tenant) returns (timeout_seconds, is_reasoning). Checks tenant.preferred_model against REASONING_MODELS and BYO_SLOW_MODELS sets. Reasoning/slow models get REASONING_MODEL_TIMEOUT (240s); others get DEFAULT_CHAT_TIMEOUT (120s). In the webhook path, is_reasoning drives wh_timeout=120.0 vs 30.0. In the drain path, _resolve_chat_timeout returns the timeout for httpx.post.
error_detail: Verified discrepancy, but no live user/operator impact - latent code-consistency issue only. get_forwarding_timeout (services.py:259-274) classifies a tenant's model against REASONING_MODELS only; it omits BYO_SLOW_MODELS (ANTHROPIC_SONNET_MODEL/ANTHROPIC_OPUS_MODEL, constants.py:188-191). Its sibling _resolve_chat_timeout (pending_queue.py:299-331) correctly checks both sets. Concrete effect: in the webhook path (views.py:354-355) a BYO Claude tenant gets wh_timeout=30s instead of 120s. However: (1) the webhook handler telegram_webhook is INACTIVE in prod - poller.py calls deleteWebhook (poller.py:134-142) so getUpdates polling is the live path; (2) even in the ACTIVE poller path, get_forwarding_timeout's result (poller.py:1357) is used ONLY for typing/nudge timing - the per-attempt chat-completion timeout is re-resolved in the drain via _resolve_chat_timeout, which DOES include BYO_SLOW_MODELS (explicit comment at poller.py:1353-1356); (3) even if the webhook path were re-enabled, the agent replies directly via the bot token even if the forward times out (views.py:351-353), so a short timeout would only miss usage-tracking capture, not drop the user's reply. Net: no behavior any user or operator currently experiences is wrong; this is a maintainability/consistency gap between two timeout helpers that could only bite if the webhook path were ever reactivated as the primary delivery path.

feature: PII rehydration in outbound Telegram responses
user_story: As a subscriber, I want PII placeholder tokens in AI responses to be replaced with real names before delivery so that I see natural language instead of [PERSON_1] tokens.
expected_behaviour: In _send_rich_response (poller) and relay_ai_response_to_telegram (drain): if tenant.pii_entity_map is set, calls apps/pii/redactor:rehydrate_text(text, entity_map) before any other processing. Same in clean_reply_for_capture for ConversationTurn storage. Fails open — exceptions are logged but do not block delivery.

feature: Insight marker extraction in Telegram outbound responses
user_story: As the system, I want [[insight:slug]]...[[/insight]] markers in AI responses to be extracted and stored as AssistantInsight rows so that insights are persisted across channels.
expected_behaviour: In both _send_rich_response (poller) and relay_ai_response_to_telegram (drain), apps/insights/markers:extract_and_record_insights(text, tenant=tenant) is called before chart processing. Strips marker tokens and persists AssistantInsight rows. Fails open. Also called in _clean_assistant_text_for_app for iOS turns. Runs before chart processing so insights nested near chart markers still record.

user_story: As the system, I want to log when AI responses contain ASCII chart data without a [[chart:]] marker so that chart rendering issues can be detected and diagnosed.
expected_behaviour: In both _send_rich_response (poller) and relay_ai_response_to_telegram (drain), apps/router/output_guards:log_ascii_chart_leak(text, tenant_id, channel) is called before any content transformation. Logs a warning if ASCII chart patterns are detected without a corresponding [[chart:]] marker. Non-blocking, no effect on message delivery.

user_story: As an anonymous user, I want to create an account with email + password so that I can access the subscriber console and start onboarding.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/signup/ accepts {email, password, display_name (optional, defaults to 'Friend')}. If PREVIEW_ACCESS_KEY is set in settings, an invite_code field must also be supplied and must match exactly, otherwise returns 403. If the email is already registered returns 409. On success, creates the User via create_user (sets username=email) and returns {access, refresh} JWT pair with HTTP 201. The refresh token is minted via RefreshToken.for_user (no pw_iat claim — the user has never rotated their password, so pw_iat=0 is acceptable at this stage). No email verification step exists.
inventory_notes: No email verification. PREVIEW_ACCESS_KEY gate is a dev-only invite wall controlled by an env var. display_name defaults to 'Friend' if omitted.
error_detail: Confirmed. SignupView reads the raw email (auth_views.py:188, no normalization) and checks duplicates with a case-sensitive exact filter User.objects.filter(email=email) (line 207), then passes the same raw value to create_user(username=email, email=email, ...) (lines 213-216). The custom User model (apps/tenants/models.py:23) inherits AbstractUser's default UserManager whose create_user runs Django normalize_email, which lowercases ONLY the domain, not the local part. The email field is not overridden so it has NO DB unique constraint; username (set to the email) is unique but case-sensitive in Postgres, so it does not block the insert either. Result: signing up as Test@x.com then test@x.com both succeed, creating two accounts for the same effective address. This is inconsistent with the password-reset path which correctly uses email__iexact (line 105) - that path would then iexact-match both duplicates and resolve ambiguously via .first(). Real but low-impact: the domain-case collision is already handled by normalize_email, and triggering it requires deliberate case variation in the email local part (most users/clients type or lowercase emails consistently). Blast radius is duplicate accounts plus reset/login ambiguity, not data loss or a security hole.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to log in with my email and password so that I receive a JWT access + refresh token pair to authenticate API requests.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/login/ is backed by SimpleJWT's TokenObtainPairView with a custom serializer (EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer). The serializer uses email as the username_field, calls Django's authenticate() with email+password, rejects inactive accounts (401 no_active_account). On success it mints a token pair via get_token() which injects a pw_iat claim (unix timestamp of user.password_last_changed_at, or 0 for legacy users). Access token TTL is 15 minutes; refresh token TTL is 7 days. Both tokens carry pw_iat for force-logout enforcement.
inventory_notes: ROTATE_REFRESH_TOKENS is intentionally disabled — refresh-token blacklisting is not active. The pw_iat claim is the only force-logout mechanism (no simplejwt blacklist).

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to exchange a valid refresh token for a new access token so that I can maintain my session without re-entering credentials.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/refresh/ is the standard SimpleJWT TokenRefreshView. Accepts {refresh} and returns a new {access} token. Refresh token TTL is 7 days from issuance. ROTATE_REFRESH_TOKENS is off so the refresh token itself is not rotated. If the refresh token is invalid or blacklisted, returns 401. Note: the force-logout pw_iat check in JWTAuthenticationWithRLS fires on every API request using the access token, not at refresh time — so a refresh with an old refresh token may succeed but subsequent API calls will be rejected.
inventory_notes: ROTATE_REFRESH_TOKENS and BLACKLIST_AFTER_ROTATION are both off. pw_iat enforcement happens at authenticate() time, not here.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to log out so that my refresh token is invalidated and cannot be reused.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/logout/ requires IsAuthenticated (Bearer access token). Body must include {refresh}; omitting it returns 400. Calls RefreshToken(refresh_token).blacklist() via simplejwt's token_blacklist. If the token is invalid or already blacklisted, returns 400 with 'Invalid refresh token.' On success returns 204 No Content. Access tokens are short-lived (15 min) and cannot be invalidated at logout — clients must discard them.
inventory_notes: Access tokens remain valid up to 15 minutes after logout. Clients must delete their local copy.

user_story: As a subscriber, I want to retrieve my current user and tenant details so that the dashboard can render my profile and subscription state.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/auth/me/ requires IsAuthenticated. Returns UserSerializer data merged with TenantSerializer nested under 'tenant' key. If the user has no tenant, tenant is null. Response is cached per-tenant for 60 seconds via @tenant_cache(ttl=60, tag='tenant'). Tenant fields include status, model_tier, subscription state, trial info, container details, config versions, budget usage, and computed fields (effective_model, free_model_offer, platform_budget_exceeded, gravity_available). Returns 200.
inventory_notes: Also exists at GET /api/v1/tenants/me/ via TenantViewSet with same caching. The two endpoints overlap; auth/me/ returns user+tenant; tenants/me/ returns only TenantSerializer.

user_story: As the platform, I want every authenticated API request to validate JWT pw_iat against the user's current password_last_changed_at and set Postgres RLS session variables so that password rotations immediately invalidate all outstanding sessions.
expected_behaviour: JWTAuthenticationWithRLS (set as DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES[1]) extends SimpleJWT's JWTAuthentication. get_user() fetches User with select_related('tenant') in one query. authenticate() then checks pw_iat claim: if user.password_last_changed_at is not None and token.pw_iat < password_last_changed_at.timestamp(), raises AuthenticationFailed with code 'password_rotated' (HTTP 401). On successful auth sets Postgres session vars app.tenant_id and app.user_id via set_rls_context(). Legacy tokens (pw_iat=0) are only rejected if the user has a non-null password_last_changed_at.
inventory_notes: select_related('tenant') in get_user() saves one cross-region round trip per request. RLS vars are cleared in TenantContextMiddleware.process_response() via reset_rls_context().

user_story: As an anonymous user who has forgotten my password, I want to request a reset email so that I can regain access to my account.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/password-reset/request/ is AllowAny. Accepts {email}. Applies dual rate-limiting: per-email (3 req/hr) and per-IP (5 req/hr) using Django cache; cache unavailability fails open. Regardless of whether the user exists, always returns 200 with a constant message to prevent account enumeration. If user exists and is_active, generates a uid (base64-encoded PK) and Django's default_token_generator token, builds a reset URL pointing to FRONTEND_URL/reset-password?uid=&token=, and sends a multipart HTML+text email via Mailgun. Email failures are swallowed (logged only). Returns 429 if rate limit exceeded.
dependencies: Mailgun (via Django email backend), Redis (rate-limit cache)
inventory_notes: Rate-limit counters are per IP and per email address, checked independently. Cache failure fails open to avoid locking users out. Email send failure is silently swallowed on purpose (no enumeration leak).
test_result: flagged then cleared by adversarial review

user_story: As an anonymous user with a reset link, I want to set a new password so that I can sign in immediately after resetting.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/password-reset/confirm/ is AllowAny. Accepts {uid, token, new_password}. Missing any field returns 400. Decodes uid from base64 to locate User; invalid uid/user returns 400 'Reset link is invalid or has expired.' Validates Django HMAC token via default_token_generator.check_token(); tokens auto-expire after PASSWORD_RESET_TIMEOUT (Django default 3 days) or when password changes. Runs Django password validators (validate_password) — failures return 400. On success: sets new password via user.set_password() (which bumps password_last_changed_at), saves with update_fields=['password','password_last_changed_at'], then mints a fresh JWT pair using EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer.get_token() (carries pw_iat). Returns {access, refresh} with 200.
